5


Do I Trust You to Lead the Way? Exploring Trust and Mistrust in Leader–Follower Relations


Michelle C. Bligh and Jeffrey C. Kohles

Trust men and they will be true to you;

treat them greatly and they will show themselves great.

Ralph Waldo Emerson

He who does not trust enough, Will not be trusted.

Lao Tzu





5.1 Introduction


Numerous theorists have called for a reconceptualization of the traditional dichotomies between leaders and followers, managers and employees, supervisors and subordinates (e.g. Baker, 2007; Bligh, 2011; Kelley, 2008; Rost, 2008). In this chapter, we posit that trust is a dynamic yet critical link between these two interrelated organizational roles and processes. Prior research conceptualizes trust as an expectation or belief that one can rely on another person’s actions and words, and that that person has good intentions towards oneself. As Mayer et al. (1995) have noted, trust is most meaningful in situations in which one party is at risk or is vulnerable to another party. It is perhaps surprising then that many leadership studies fail to acknowledge the critical role of trust as the primary conduit or currency through which leaders and followers exchange power and influence. It is hard to imagine an organizational situation fraught with more risk and vulnerability than that between leader and follower, each of whom is vulnerable to deception, subterfuge, and even outright fraud by the other party. In this chapter, we provide a review of theoretical and empirical articles that highlight the importance of trust as a core cognitive and affective process in the dynamic leadership–followership relationship.

In our review, we build on Collinson’s (2005) call for leadership studies to explicitly incorporate “the multiple, shifting, contradictory and ambiguous identities of ‘leaders’ and ‘followers’” (p. 1436). Regardless of these labels, or perhaps more accurately in spite of them, both leaders and followers play a critical role in shaping collective responses to the most important organizational and societal problems of modern times. Followership has claimed a growing role in resolving many of the leadership challenges of the 21st century, and researchers and practitioners must tackle these challenges from both sides of the equation, emphasizing the evolving interpersonal processes that fundamentally define the leader–follower relationship.

In the following pages, we first review the literature on trust as it relates to both leadership and followership, exploring how followers work for leaders they either trust or mistrust, and how trust has been studied within these role-based distinctions. We first review previous work that explicitly addresses the role of trust in the leadership relationship, emphasizing that trust is a critical foundation for effective exchange-based and motivational leader–follower relationships. We then turn to empirical and theoretical work that explores the antecedents and consequences of trust in leader–follower relations, exploring what factors predict when trust is more or less likely to develop, as well as the differential outcomes that result from trusting dyads versus mistrustful dyads, where trust can act as an important buffer against negative workplace experiences. We subsequently explore relatively less-studied topics including when and why leaders trust followers, the dynamic transfer of trust between leaders and followers, and mistrust and trust repair. Finally, we conclude with the implications of this review for future research, as well as for organizations that seek to foster environments that are conducive to the development of trusting dyadic relationships.





5.2 The Foundational Role of Trust in Leader–Follower Relationships


Trust has always occupied a central and foundational role in the leader–follower ­relationship, and in this section we review the evidence for when and to what extent trust has been examined as a critical component of the leadership process (for comprehensive reviews of trust, see Lewicki et al., 2006; Li, 2007, 2008; Mayer et al. (1995); Rousseau et al. (1998); Schoorman et al., (2007); for meta-analyses, see Colquitt et al., 2007; Dirks & Ferrin, 2002). The study of trust development between leaders and followers dates back to Gabarro’s (1978) longitudinal qualitative study of how managers develop working relationships with subordinates. Over a 3-year time period, he identified two core foundations of leader–follower trust. The first was competence or ability, which included the knowledge and skills needed to do a job, along with the interpersonal skills and “general wisdom” needed to succeed. The second was character, which Mayer et al. (1995) subsequently split into two separate constructs: benevolence, or the extent to which a trustee is believed to want to do good for the truster, and integrity, or the degree to which a trustee is believed to adhere to sound moral and ethical principles.

From this early foundation, trust has subsequently been conceptualized as a core ­component of many if not most of the core leadership theories. However, the essence of these theories can still be tied to these two fundamental building blocks: how leaders establish that they have the competence to lead effectively, reflecting both task- and relationship-oriented skills, and how they establish their benevolence and integrity. Further, leaders have two primary mechanisms through which to establish these foundations. The exchange-based model asserts that supportive, participative, and empowering leadership behaviors send a message that the superior has confidence in, and concern and respect for, the subordinates, and these leadership behaviors foster higher levels of trust in the superior (Dirks & Ferrin, 2002). Other leadership theories highlight and provide support for the second, complementary motivational mechanism for the development of trust, which holds that more opportunities to participate in decision-making provide subordinates with greater intrinsic rewards from work and higher levels of psychological empowerment (Huang et al., 2010). The result is likely to be both greater levels of trust in the leader and improved work performance.

Trust forms a key foundation underlying core relationship-oriented leadership behaviors in the Ohio State University and University of Michigan studies, as consideration is characterized by mutual trust, respect and support for another person’s ideas, and appreciation of their feelings (Judge et al., 2004). Research dating back to the 1970s on the vertical dyad-linkage model has demonstrated that managers have different relationships with employees who are trusted compared with employees who are not trusted (Dansereau et al., 1975). In addition, leader–member exchange (LMX) research has demonstrated a relationship between LMX relationship quality and both identification-based trust and calculus-based trust (Scandura & Pellegrini, 2008). Other research suggests that the relationship between managerial trust and psychological experiences of empowerment is mediated by the quality of the LMX relationship between leader and follower (Gómez & Rosen, 2001). These findings highlight that a follower’s trusting relationship with his or her immediate leader is critical in predicting employee experiences, and that organizational efforts to redesign jobs and support empowerment initiatives may have limited utility where trust in leaders is absent.

The meaningful role of trust in transformational leadership has long been recognized both in its conceptualization (Bass, 1985) and in its measurement (i.e. the Multifactor Leadership Questionnaire, MLQ). Bass’s (1990) theoretical model of transformational leadership argued that this style of leadership is effective in part through its facilitation of follower trust. Other studies have similarly provided evidence that it is useful to distinguish between the behaviors of transformational or charismatic leaders and the level of trust that followers have in them in predicting follower outcomes (Kirkpatrick & Locke, 1996; Podsakoff et al., 1990; Shamir et al., 1998). Some empirical work suggests that the relationship (both direct and indirect) between transformational leadership and performance is mediated through both followers’ trust in the leader and value congruence (Jung & Avolio, 2000). Likewise, in a study of R&D teams, transformational and consultative leadership behaviors (specifically consulting team members when making decisions, communicating a collective vision, and sharing common values with the team) predicted 67% of the variance in team members’ trust towards leaders (Gillespie & Mann, 2004). These leadership behaviors signal that the leader is unlikely to breach trust, allowing followers to make themselves more vulnerable to the leader by disclosing sensitive information and relying on the leader’s judgments in ambiguous situations. Finally, leader behaviors using contingent-based reward and punishment have been shown to be more effective in engendering trust and citizenship than noncontingent leader behaviors (Rubin et al., 2010).

Other scholars have highlighted the role of trust in charismatic leadership processes. Choi (2006) presents a theoretical model of charismatic leadership that suggests empathy produces follower trust, emotional bonds with leaders, and identification with leaders. Seyranian & Bligh (2008) point out that use of inclusive language by leaders, stressing a sense of similarity, builds followers’ trust, which may help the leaders move followers’ social values towards change (see also Shamir, 2005).

Similarly, trust has been proposed as a key process in ethical, servant, and authentic leadership styles. This research highlights and builds upon the core foundation of trust as a leader who demonstrates integrity (for a theoretical model of leadership and integrity, see Palanski & Yammarino, 2009). Empirical research supports the critical role ­between followers’ perceptions of manager integrity and trust in the manager, which in turn influences follower attitudes and performance (Kaiser & Hogan, 2010). Many scholars have emphasized the critical relationship between servant leadership and follower trust. For example, Joseph & Winston (2005) examined the relationship between perceptions of servant leadership, leader trust, and organizational trust. Organizations that were perceived to be servant-led had followers who reported higher levels of both trust in direct leader and organizational-level trust. They argue that these findings support servant leadership as an important antecedent to both leader and organizational trust (see also Senjaya & Pekerti, 2010).

Walumbwa et al. (2011) argue that knowledge workers increasingly require an authentic leader whose values are aligned with the company’s mission in order to lead with transparency and trust. They emphasize that authentic leadership is fundamentally predicated on trust, which sustains a more candid process of dealing with difficult problems and leads to more effective functioning of knowledge workers. Stone & Cooper (2009) similarly highlight that to emerge as a credible leader an individual must first develop “credits” with potential followers before they will consent to be led in a new direction; trust may be a primary conduit through which these credits are built and maintained. Supporting this basic argument, Norman et al. (2010) found a relationship between leader transparency and positivity on the one hand and followers’ perceived trust and ratings of leader effectiveness on the other.

In the context of critically low levels of trust in leaders in the wake of the Enron and Worldcom debacles, Caldwell et al. (2010) propose that great leaders are “ethical stewards” who build trust by truly investing in and affirming the identities of those whom they serve. They argue that ethical stewardship is a morally superior governance model that generates increased employee commitment through a leader’s ability to align systems that build trust and ensure the welfare and growth of both followers and communities.

Overall, all of these leadership theories resonate with the core foundations of leader–follower trust: that leaders must be able to demonstrate their ability and competence to lead, and their integrity and benevolence towards those over whom they wield power. Recent leadership theories have focused a great deal of attention on integrity as a core foundation of leader–follower trust, arguably downplaying the role of task- and relationship-based behaviors in communicating a leader’s competence and benevolence. Lee et al. (2010) suggest that leadership-development efforts are overly focused on the capability of leaders in transformational, transactional, and empowering styles and roles, in the process neglecting the core emphasis on competence and expertise. They argue that, particularly in the context of developing knowledge workers, leadership scholars should pay additional attention to knowledge-building behaviors, such as skills in scanning the environment for new ideas, developing knowledge networks, sharing technical expertise, bringing in outside expertise, providing feedback, and overseeing the quality of work. Together, demonstrating competence in these skills engenders trust and knowledge-sharing, and leadership researchers should not neglect the role these competence-enhancing behaviors play in building leader–follower trust.

Burke et al. (2007) provide an integrative framework of the relationship between trust in leaders and both proximal and distal outcomes. They highlight that despite the fragmentation of research on leadership and trust, across the various perspectives there is a common recognition of: (1) the importance of truster and trustee characteristics; (2) behavioral, cognitive, and attitudinal predictors of trust; (3) situational/contextual factors that impact trust in leadership; and (4) the fact that trust in leadership results in behavioral and attitudinal outcomes. Their work highlights that leaders must demonstrate ability by setting a compelling direction, providing structure, and demonstrating task-relevant knowledge. In addition, leaders develop perceptions of benevolence through trans­formational, transactional, participative, and coaching behaviors that foster a ­supportive context. Finally, leaders develop and sustain perceptions of integrity through justice, value congruence, and accountability. Overall, this work provides a critical integrative framework for understanding how leader–follower trust is enhanced.





5.3 Development of Trust in Leader–Follower Relations: Antecedents


By far the most research attention on trust in leader–follower relationships focuses on its antecedents and consequences, which have been summarized in both Dirks & Ferrin’s (2002) meta-analysis of trust in leadership and 23 constructs, and in Burke et al.’s (2007) theoretical framework. Antecedents to the development of trust in leader–follower relations and their corresponding citations are listed in Table 5.1. In terms of proposed antecedents across studies, Dirk & Ferrin’s results indicate that transformational leadership has the strongest relationship with trust (r = .72), followed by perceived organizational support (POS) (r = .69). Next in magnitude, interactional justice and procedural justice have relationships of similar magnitude (r = .65 and .61), with transactional leadership and distributive justice having weaker relationships (r = .59 and .50). Participative decision-making also has a relatively strong relationship (r = .46), as does unmet expectations (r = −.40). Finally, propensity to trust has a small but significant relationship (r = .16); surprisingly, the length of the relationship is unrelated to trust.

Overall, Dirks & Ferrin (2002) suggest that leadership style and management practices are important antecedents of increasing trust in leadership, in terms of: justice, such as ensuring fair procedures, outcomes, and interactional processes; using participative decision-making; providing organizational support; ensuring expectations are fulfilled; and using transformational and transactional leadership styles. Transformational leadership, POS, and interactional justice have the largest relationships, followed by procedural justice, transactional leadership, distributive justice, participative decision-making, and unmet expectations. The authors note that the exact causal processes by which transformational leadership appears to increase follower trust remain unclear, and future research is necessary to identify the precise behavioral components responsible for the effect.

In addition, while trust in one’s leader has very high correlations with transformational leadership (r = .72) and satisfaction with one’s supervisor (r = .73), it is important to note that transformational leadership and trust may overlap in part because transformational leadership is measured using behavior-description items that are closely tied to trust (e.g. items in the Multifactor Leadership Scale (MLS) (Bass, 1985), such as “I am ready to trust in his/her capacity to overcome any obstacles”). The problem of item overlap is further compounded by the fact that followers are usually asked to complete assessments of both transformational leadership style and trust. Although the measures of trust and satisfaction with leaders are less likely to overlap, it is possible that when respondents report their attitudes about the leader, it is difficult for them to separate the two constructs (a halo effect). That is, they may be reporting their global assessment of the leader rather than reporting on two separate constructs. Consequently, these results likely overstate the true relationship between trust and these two variables.

Table 5.1 Trust antecedents.

Theme Key findings Reference

Characteristics Leaders and followers with higher propensity to trust more likely to develop positive exchange relationships Bernerth & Walker (2009)

Leader behaviors Transformational leadership Dirks & Ferrin (2002)

Quality of treatment by managers

Quality of managerial decision-making

Shared social bonds

Understandable actions by management

Trustworthiness Bijlsma & Koopman (2003)

Monitoring performance

Providing guidance to improve individual performance

Openness to followers’ ideas Bijlsma & van de Bunt (2003)

Conflict-management style of manager Chan Huang & Ng (2008)

Providing support in case of trouble with others

Consideration for employees’ needs and interests

Protecting employees’ rights Ertürk (2010); Fairholm & Fairholm (2000); Moye & Henkin (2006)

Behaviors denoting benevolence toward followers Lapierre (2007)

Perceived authenticity Gardner et al. (2009)

Ethics Perceptions of ethical leader behavior relate positively to trust Den Hartog & De Hoogh (2009)

Relationship between ethical organizational climate and turnover intentions is mediated by trust and other factors Mulki et al. (2008)

Perception of an ethical climate increases trust in leader Salamon & Robinson (2008)

Justice Interactional justice predicts trust in one’s direct leader DeConinck (2010); Stinglhamber et al. (2006); Zhang et al. (2008)

Distributive justice is related to organizational trust DeConinck (2010)

Procedural justice affects cognitive and affect-based trust De Cremer et al. (2006)

Procedural justice mediates the relationship between employee development and trust in leaders Tzafrir et al. (2004)

Perception of overall fairness defines trust in less-certain trust assessments Jones & Martens (2009)

Organizational politics High levels of organizational politics erode trust Poon (2006)

Relationship between organizational politics and job outcomes is moderated by trust Vigoda-Gadot & Talmud (2010)

Perceived organizational support Perceived organizational support has a strong relationship to trust

Unmet expectations impact trust relationship Dirks & Ferrin (2002)

Other research supports the basic argument that to gain the benefits of a trusting work environment, managers should demonstrate behaviors that promote trust, such as consistency, integrity, concern, and benevolence, by showing consideration for employees’ needs and interests and by protecting their rights (Ertürk, 2010; Fairholm & Fairholm, 2000; Moye & Henkin, 2006). In a special volume devoted to trust in organizations, Bijlsma & Koopman (2003) highlight that shared social bonds, quality of treatment by management, understandable actions by management, quality of managerial decision-making, and perceived trustworthiness are all antecedents of trust in managers (Connell et al., 2003; Costa, 2003; Tyler, 2003). Gardner et al. (2009) further posit that the perceived authenticity of the leader impacts the favorability of followers’ impressions and subsequent trust in the leader, and that leaders communicate their authenticity through both deep acting and genuine displays of emotions. However, these propositions have yet to be empirically tested, and the important role of both leader and follower emotional displays is an interesting and important area for future research on antecedents of trust.

Lapierre (2007) examined the separate and joint effects of supervisor behaviors ­denoting trustworthiness on subordinate willingness to help the leader with extra-role workplace contributions. In a lab experiment, supervisory behaviors denoting benevolence towards the participant had the strongest positive impact on their willingness to benefit the ­supervisor, likely due to the norm of reciprocity described by social-exchange theory (Blau, 1964). In addition, leader benevolence towards the subordinate’s peers also had a significant positive effect on that subordinat’s expressed willingness to benefit the supervisor. This finding is consistent with social information-processing theory (Salancik & Pfeffer, 1978), which posits that individuals observe how others are treated and amend their attitudes, intentions, and behaviors accordingly. Together, these findings suggest that both social-exchange theory and social information-processing theory are relevant in understanding the processes through which leader–follower trust develops.

Bijlsma & van de Bunt (2003) set out to identify a parsimonious set of leadership ­behaviors that serve as cues for how much followers should trust their direct leaders. In a case study of a Dutch hospital with 1800 employees, Bijlsma & van de Bunt (2003) collected both survey and interview data. Their results suggest that monitoring performance, providing guidance to improve individual performance, providing support in case of ­trouble with others, demonstrating openness to the ideas of followers, and utilizing cooperation-related problem-solving are relevant trust-related behaviors of managers. Also of note, a leader’s appreciation of a job well done was not significantly related to trust in them in this study.

Other subsequent work has continued to focus on the role of perceived justice in fostering both organizational trust and trust in one’s direct leader. DeConinck’s (2010) recent study, for example, included POS, perceived supervisory support (PSS), the three components of organizational justice (distributive, procedural, and interactional), and both organizational trust and supervisory trust in a single model. In addition, DeConinck used one dataset to test the hypothesized relationships and a second data set to confirm the results from the first study. His results suggested that the relationship between procedural justice and organization trust was mediated by POS. Interactional justice, in contrast, predicted trust in one’s direct leader both directly and indirectly through PSS (see also Stinglhamber et al., 2006; Zhang et al., 2008). Finally, distributive justice was related both directly and indirectly to organizational trust through PSS. While these findings suggest that all three aspects of justice are linked to trust, interactional justice was the most predictive of trust in the direct leader. Notably, in this study interactional justice assessed followers’ perceptions that they were receiving fair and interpersonal treatment from their leader, likely tapping into the benevolence and integrity dimensions of trust. Other findings suggest that employees are particularly likely to use perceptions of overall fairness as decision ­heuristics for determining whether to trust organizational authorities when they are less certain about their assessments of trust (Jones & Martens, 2009), indicating that justice perceptions may be particularly critical when employees are not sure whether or not to place trust in their leaders.

Although organizational practices such as interactional justice, procedural justice, and participative decision-making were related to trust in both direct and organizational leaders, they had stronger relationships with trust in the direct leader. De Cremer et al. (2006) similarly found that direct leaders enacting procedural justice positively affected organizational identification and both affective and cognitive trust in a sample of 257 Dutch human-resources managers. Further, followers’ affect-based trust mediated the relationship between procedural justice and organizational identification, and procedural justice mediated the impact of employee development and trust in managers (Tzafrir et al., 2004). In contrast, Dirks & Ferrin (2002) found that POS was more strongly related to trust in organizational leadership than to trust in one’s direct leader, suggesting that dyadic and organizational-level outcome variables will be differentially related depending on whether trust in a direct leader versus organizational leadership is assessed.

Interestingly, Dirks & Ferrin (2002) found no relationship between trust in leadership and the length of the relationship between leaders and followers, and found only a weak relationship between trust in leaders and followers’ propensity to trust. These findings are highly significant for managers who seek to develop trusting relationships with their followers, as they suggest that although followers vary in their dispositional propensity to trust, these dispositional differences explain little of the variance in followers’ trust in their leaders. In addition, it is important to point out that while managers may often assume that the length of their relationship with their followers is associated with the followers’ willingness to trust them, in actuality trust can be much more spontaneous, dynamic, and fluid. Therefore, managers should not assume that time will automatically lead to more trusting relationships, nor can they assume that followers with whom they have longer-term relationships are automatically more trustworthy than newer followers.

In contrast, however, Bernerth & Walker’s (2009) study of 195 matched employee–supervisor pairs across industries found that the highest-quality relationships, as defined by quality of social exchanges, were positively affected by individuals who are prone to trust one another. Specifically, their results suggest that leader and follower propensity to trust interact, such that the most positive exchange relationships are perceived to exist when both managers and employees are high in propensity to trust. Given that social exchanges inherently involve a certain amount of vulnerability, in that the other party’s behavior is not under one’s control, the results reported here suggest that those who are more dispositionally willing to trust others to reciprocate form the best relationships.

Finally, it is interesting to note that in a study of 126 Chinese followers, Chan et al. (2008) found that perceptions of a manager’s conflict-management style were a significant antecedent of trust, and that trust mediates the relationship between an integrating conflict-management style (characterized as both cooperative and demonstrating a concern for others) and positive attitudinal outcomes in followers. This study suggests that ­conflict-management style may also be a fruitful area for future research into when ­followers are more likely to trust their leaders.





5.3.1 Organizational-level variables: ethics and politics


In addition to the constructs already discussed, it is important to highlight that followers do not develop trust in their leaders solely on the basis of interpersonal processes. DeConinck’s (2011) findings in a sales environment suggests that followers’ perceptions of an ethical work climate are related directly to supervisory trust, suggesting that organizational-level factors can “bleed into” perceptions of a leader’s trustworthiness. Perceptions of high levels of organizational politics can erode the relationship between trust in supervisors and employee willingness to help coworkers (Poon, 2006). Vigoda-Gadot & Talmud’s (2010) work suggests a relationship between perceived organizational politics and job outcomes including job satisfaction, organizational commitment, stress, and burnout; importantly, this relationship is moderated by trust and social support.

As expected, Den Hartog & De Hoogh’s (2009) study of 503 employees in 79 work groups found that perceived ethical leader behavior was positively related to trust (Den Hartog & De Hoogh, 2009). Mulki et al. (2008) found that the relationship between ethical climate and turnover intention is fully mediated by role stress, interpersonal conflict, emotional exhaustion, trust in one’s supervisor, and job satisfaction. In addition, perceptions of an ethical climate or collectively-felt trust (Salamon & Robinson, 2008) increase trust in one’s supervisor, which in turn reduces interpersonal conflict and emotional exhaustion. Together, these findings highlight the detrimental role that unethical work environments and highly-politicized organizations can have on developing trusting relationships between leaders and followers, and suggest that the organizational context in which leaders and followers interact is critical to understanding the ­antecedents of trust.





5.4 Trust in Leader–Follower Relations: Consequences


Arguably the most robust and well-researched aspect of leadership and trust involves outcomes of trusting relationships on followers and organizations, which are summarized in Table 5.2. Dirks & Ferrin’s meta-analysis (2002) indicates that trust in leadership is significantly related to attitudinal, behavioral, and performance outcomes. For work behaviors and outcomes, trust is related to each of the types of organizational citizenship behavior: altruism (r = .19), civic virtue (r = .11), conscientiousness (r = .22), courtesy (r = .22), and sportsmanship (r = .20). Trust has a relatively weak but significant relationship with job performance as well (r = .16). Trust in leadership demonstrates a substantial relationship with attitudinal variables and had the strongest relationships with job satisfaction (r = .51) and organizational commitment (r = .49). Across studies, trust also has sizable relationships with turnover intentions (r = .40), belief in information provided by the leader (r = .35), and commitment to decisions (r = .24). Not surprisingly, trust is also highly related to satisfaction with leader (r =.73) and LMX (r = .69). Dirks & Ferrin (2002) highlight that further research is necessary to determine the direction of causality, as well as mediators and moderators of these relationships.

In terms of outcomes of trust, Dirks & Ferrin’s (2002) findings also highlight the importance of taking the level of analysis into consideration. They found that trust in a direct leader has an equal or greater effect on performance, altruism, intent to quit, and job satisfaction, than does trust in organizational leadership. However, trust in organizational leadership has a greater impact on organizational-level outcomes such as ­organizational commitment. These findings suggest that followers can and do develop different trusting relationships with their immediate leaders versus leaders higher up in the organizational hierarchy, and in turn those varying relationships have different effects on follower outcome variables. Huang et al. (2009) point out that while employees occupying higher-level positions such as managerial positions tend to attach more value to a sense of control and autonomy, lower-level employees such as clerical staff and frontline employees tend to place more emphasis on the quality of relationships with colleagues and supervisors as well as the extent to which their superiors are trustworthy.

Table 5.2 Trust outcomes.

Theme Key findings References

Attitudinal outcomes Trust in supervisor strengthens both affective commitment and organizational identification Costigan et al. (2006); Ertürk (2010); Straiter (2005)

Trust has a significant relationship to job satisfaction and organizational commitment Dirks & Ferrin (2002)

Behavioral outcomes Follower trust in the leader and identification with the collective both play an important role in translating a leader’s self-sacrifice into follower cooperation De Cremer & van Knippenberg (2005)

Trust is significantly related to altruism, civic virtue, conscientiousness, courtesy, and sportsmanship Dirks & Ferrin (2002)

Trust enhances employee role enlargement and organizational citizenship behaviors Chiaburu & Marinova (2006)

Performance outcomes Trust has a relatively weak but significant relationship with job performance (r = .16) Dirks & Ferrin (2002)

Trust in a direct leader leads to increased ability to focus on work tasks Frazier et al. (2010)

Organizational trust allows employees to focus and add value to the organization Mayer & Gavin (2005)

Other studies have subsequently supported the finding that both trust in one’s direct leader and organization-directed trust are positively related to employees’ role-­enlargement and organizational citizenship behaviors. In a survey of 186 line and administrative employees, Chiaburu & Marinova (2006) found that increasing levels of trust in the direct leader and the organization are both important for role enlargement. They advise training managers to increase their trust-related awareness and behaviors, as well as paying attention to organizational fairness-related modifications. These might include setting, implementing, and monitoring distributive (e.g. salary and reward ­allocation) and procedural (overall organizational procedures) policies.

Frazier et al.’s (2010) findings also highlight the importance of the trust referent: they indicate that trust in a proximal referent (a direct leader) leads to an increased ability to focus on work-related tasks, whereas trust in a distal referent does not influence the ability to focus. Trust in management allows employees to focus on the tasks that need to be carried out to add value to their organization. The results of this study indicate that trust in two levels of management is related to employees’ ability to focus attention on value-producing activities, and that this ability to focus is subsequently related to a multifaceted treatment of performance (Mayer & Gavin, 2005).

Trust in one’s supervisor is also strongly and positively associated with organizational identification, and a number of studies confirm that it is an important component in strengthening both affective commitment and organizational identification (Costigan et al., 2006; Ertürk, 2010; Straiter, 2005). However, Ertürk’s (2010) study suggests a potentially important difference between affect- and cognitive-based trust in one’s leader. This survey of blue-collar shipping employees in Turkey found that perceived competence and perceived control are positively related to organizational identification, but particularly so when cognition-based trust in a supervisor is high. The cognition-based trust of an employee in his/her supervisor is based on rational reasons, such as the supervisor’s past record of competence, responsibility, and trustworthy behaviors. On the other hand, ­affect-based trust is more emotional, and reflects care and concern for each party in the relationship. Ertürk found that affect-based trust in a supervisor moderated the relationship between goal internalization and organizational identification, and between POS and organizational identification. Hence, goal internalization and POS are positively related to organizational identification more strongly among employees who have higher affect-based trust in their supervisor.

Using both a public-good experiment and a cross-sectional survey in a German multinational company, De Cremer & van Knippenberg (2005) found that follower trust in the leader and identification with the collective both play an important role in translating a leader’s self-sacrifice into follower cooperation. Stated differently, the relationship ­between leader self-sacrifice and follower cooperation (measured by contributions in a public-good dilemma and organizational citizenship behaviors) is mediated by perceptions of trust in the leader and feelings of collective identification. This study is unique in that it involved both experimental and survey data, and it supports the notion that self-sacrifice for the collective may be an important aspect of leadership that moves followers beyond their own self-interests, but only when they trust their leader.





5.5 Trust As A Buffer: Mediation and Moderation of Trust Between Leadership and Follower Outcomes


Trust has long been proposed to mediate the effectiveness of leader behavior (Pillai & Meindl, 1998; Pillai et al., 1999; Podsakoff et al., 1990), and has frequently been posited to have a potential buffering effect. Bal et al. (2011) investigated the roles of age and trust in perceived procedural justice and employee turnover in a three-wave longitudinal survey among 1597 Dutch employees. Their results demonstrate that prior trust in one’s leader modifies the relation between procedural justice and turnover: specifically, the combination of high procedural justice and high trust in the leader diminishes the likelihood of employee turnover. This study highlights that followers with both high procedural justice and high trust in their leader are more willing to stay with their organization, and that this effect is strongest among older workers with high trust. Bal et al. (2011) conclude that, particularly for older workers, trust in the leader becomes essential in evaluations of procedural justice in determining whether or not to stay with the organization, noting that “theoretically, high trust could act as a buffer for older workers against negative effects of justice violations and contract breaches since trust in the leader reduces the impact of stressful situations resulting from injustice” (p. 79).

Rubin et al. (2010) found that the relationship between operant leadership and organizational citizenship behaviors is fully mediated by trust. Other research suggests that the relationship between transformational leadership and the in-role performance of followers is mediated by empowerment and trust in the leader (Bartram & Casimir, 2007). In this study of 109 leader–follower dyads in an Australian insurance company call center, Bartram & Casimir found that in order to improve both in-role performance and satisfaction with the leader, followers need to be empowered and to trust their leaders. However, while greater perceptions of follower empowerment lead to improved performance, they are not associated with higher job satisfaction. In contrast, follower trust in their leader is associated with greater job satisfaction but not performance. Finally, it is important to note that the authors conclude that leaders can facilitate the development of perceptions of trustworthiness through competence, fair treatment of their followers, and being ­genuinely concerned about the best interests of their followers, particularly in the highly-controlled, standardized, and formalized work setting endemic in the context of a ­call-center environment. There is also evidence that trust in management mediates the relationship between followers’ psychological capital (PsyCap) and performance, and partially mediates the relationship between followers’ perceptions of authentic leadership and performance (see Clark & Payne, 2006). Finally, the relationship between transformational leadership and job satisfaction is partially mediated by employees’ trust in their leader (Liu et al., 2010).

Also relevant are Holtz & Harold’s (2008) findings of follower responses “when the boss says ‘no.’” They examined the relationship between leadership style, trust, employee interpretations of managerial explanations, and justice perceptions using a critical-incident methodology. Their findings showed followers who felt their manager was transformational reported a higher degree of trust and more favorable reactions to managerial explanations. These results have been extrapolated to organizational change as well. Trust in senior leaders may also enhance employee readiness for corporate transformation changes (­Rafferty & Simons, 2006). Findings from 194 employees working in R&D teams at a multinational automotive company indicated that charismatic leadership and trust in top management were both positively related to innovation-implementation behaviors (­Michaelis et al., 2009).

Interestingly, some research has found an interaction effect between trust in one’s supervisor and perceptions of control over change processes, such that higher trust increases followers’ organizational commitment when perceived control over change is low (Neves & Caetano, 2006). In addition, this study found that the relationship between interpersonal justice, perceived supervisor support, and affective organizational commitment was fully mediated by employees’ trust in their supervisor.

Ferrin & Dirks (2003) report that the relationship between reward structures and trust is complex and varied, and mediated by causal schemas, suspicion effects, and self-­perception. They state that the impact of mixed reward structures on trust is biased by individuals’ preexisting expectations about their partners’ trustworthiness, suggesting that even followers’ interpretations of the meaning of rewards and how they are applied are strongly impacted by whether or not they trust their leader.





5.6 Leaders Trusting Followers


As is true of other aspects of leader–follower relations, much greater attention has been paid to the importance of followers’ trust in leaders than the reverse: to what extent leaders trust followers. This is perhaps the most notable and glaring shortcoming in the leadership and trust research. A notable exception is Hakimi et al.’s (2010) work across two studies of the relationship between leaders’ trust in follower performance and integrity on the one hand and leader-empowering behaviors on the other. Their results show that leader-empowering behavior depends on the trust leaders have in follower’s performance and integrity, and that this relationship is moderated by leader conscientiousness. They look at leader trust as an antecedent of leader-empowering behavior, instead of as a consequence, and demonstrate its importance in the empowerment process. Their findings are important to the study of leader–follower relationships more generally.

Another notable exception is Brower et al. (2009), who also demonstrate that effective leaders need to not only gain the trust of their followers but also learn to trust their followers. Specifically, they demonstrate that trusting leaders develop employees who are more productive, who offer and provide help beyond the requirements of their jobs, and who stay with an organization for longer periods of time. However, Brower et al. (2009) also point out that organizational systems often protect managers from subordinates who are not trusted, thus limiting those subordinates’ opportunities to demonstrate their trustworthiness. Therefore, leaders may benefit from extending trust to followers even before they have gained enough experience with them to assess their trustworthiness. In essence, this involves signaling to employees that the leader is willing to take risks and display vulnerability despite the potential for negative outcomes.

Brower et al. (2009) note that an additional benefit may be that managers who learn to trust and act on that trust enhance their own perceived trustworthiness. That is, as Whitener and colleagues (1998) argue, gaining the trust of subordinates may involve first acting as a trusting manager. Other findings suggest that employees also tend to place more trust in fellow coworkers who are trusted by team leaders (Lau & Liden, 2008), especially when the group’s performance is poor. This line of reasoning suggests that organizations need to take steps to enhance the willingness of managers to act in a trusting manner, such as by sharing sensitive information and empowering employees (Cummings, 1983; ­Deluga, 1994; Whitener, 1997).

Also relevant to the discussion of how leaders develop trust in followers are findings that status differences between leaders and followers influence conditions of trust. Specifically, Werbel & Henriques (2009) found that supervisors are more concerned about conditions of trust that deal with delegation, and reported that receptivity, availability, and discreteness were perceived to be more important in building leader trust in the follower and a quality LMX relationship. Followers, on the other hand, reported that availability, competence, discreteness, integrity, and openness were more important for follower trust in the leader. Further, followers are more concerned about those conditions of trust based on interactional justice. These findings suggest that differences in these foundational conditions of trust may create conflicting expectations about how to effectively build trust (Werbel & Henriques, 2009).





5.7 Trust in Dynamic Leader–Follower Processes:the Transfer of Trust


Also notably absent from the research on leader–follower trust is attention to the development of trust as a dynamic and evolving process. Ferrin et al. (2007) present evidence that trust perceptions play a crucial mediating role in the development of cooperation in both interpersonal and intergroup interactions. Overall, it does “take two to tango”: the development of mutual trust and cooperation involves an intricate dance that spirals over time and is fundamentally affected by the partners’ initial moves. Chughtai & Buckley (2008) posit that the relationship between trust and work engagement is mutually reinforcing and leads to an upward-spiral effect. That is, high levels of state- and trait-based trust boost work engagement, which in turn augments both forms of trust.

Atwater (1988) found that the most important predictors of perceived supportive leadership behaviors, such as resolving conflicts, supporting opportunities for follower development, and inspiring loyalty, were the levels of trust and loyalty towards the leader. In other words, the more trust and loyalty followers expressed towards their leader, the more positively the leader was perceived to behave. She argues that trusting leader–follower relationships are likely circular: if initial trust on the part of followers is met with supportive behaviors on the part of leaders, trust is likely to develop and grow (see also McKnight et al., 1998). Ferrin et al. (2007) similarly show in a comparative test of trust and cooperation spiral models that the earliest stages of a relationship are crucial for determining the quality of the future relationship. It is also noteworthy that trust and loyalty are more predictive of perceived supportive leader behaviors than the leader’s personality and job characteristics, suggesting that the development of trust can possibly mitigate task demands and personality differences between leader and follower. Bernereth & Walker’s (2007) findings are also relevant here. They report an interaction between managerial and employee propensity to trust, such that the most positive exchange relationships are perceived to exist when both managers and employees are high in propensity to trust.

Recent research suggests that the factors that influence what we find trustworthy in leaders and followers change as the relationship develops. Levin et al. (2006) assert that as trusters learn more about others, they base their trust on different kinds of information. From this perspective, Dirks & Ferrin’s (2002) earlier findings that leader–follower relationship length is not associated with greater levels of trust are not surprising; rather, trust should be affected by the interaction of relationship length and bases of trust. The results of Levin et al.’s (2006) survey of supervisors and subordinates support this prediction. Specifically, in newer leader–follower relationships, trust is associated with demographic similarity (i.e. same gender in this study); in intermediate relationships, with observations of trustworthy behavior; and in mature relationships, with shared perspective (Levin et al., 2006). These results indicate that the length of a relationship is critical to understanding antecedents of leader–follower trust, and that additional research is needed into how these relationships unfold over time.





5.8 Mistrust and Lack of Trust in Leader– Follower Relationships


Finally, it is somewhat disconcerting that very little research focuses on “the dark side of trust” and its consequences. For example, how far are followers willing to go in terms of unethical or unhealthy behaviors to please leaders whom they trust? Even more surprising is the lack of research that specifically addresses how breaches of trust can be repaired ­between leaders and followers. Mayer’s (2007) chapter reviews factors affecting trust in leaders, and highlights referents of trust, sources of risk, and past behavioral performance as factors influencing employees’ trust in management. In addition, Mayer examines how the past and anticipated future length of a relationship affect trust and the employment relationship, pointing to the potential role of catharsis in rebuilding broken trust and trust repair (for comprehensive treatments of trust repair, see Dirks & De Cremer, 2011; Kim et al. 2004, 2009; Tomlinson & Mayer, 2009; for a meta-analysis of the effects of psychological contract breach on trust, see Zhao et al., 2007). However, there is great need for empirical research into and application of these theories in the realm of leader–follower relationships specifically.

Another important variable for both leaders and followers in developing trusting relationships concerns the issue of monitoring. There are a number of contradictory findings which suggest that monitoring by supervisors can be both negatively and positively related to trust in leaders (e.g. Bijlsma & Van de Bunt, 2003; Ferrin et al., 2007). ­Bijlsma-Frankema et al. (2008) point out that these findings may be contingent upon the nature of the teams studied, where initially or in newer work groups monitoring may be interpreted as providing the footing followers need to make progress in goal attainment. They note that this poses a challenge for organizations to help leaders learn how to monitor employees without signaling distrust, but rather signaling benevolence. Previous research has shown that monitoring, when combined with fair assessment of performance, leader support, openness, and collaborative problem-­solving, is highly related to trust in managers (­Bijlsma & Van de Bunt, 2003). Thus, an important implication for managers is to combine careful monitoring with supportive leadership factors in order to facilitate greater levels of employee trust, rather than undermining it.

Mutually-beneficial cooperative relationships can also take place without trust, through alternative mechanisms such as reputation, enforcement of contracts, compensation schemes, social capital networks, and regulation of behavior through professional societies (Cook et al., 2005). Thus, it is important not to overemphasize the role of trust in bringing about cooperative leader–follower relationships, which can be mutually beneficial to both parties in the complete absence of trust. Further, in some organizational situations it may be less the person in the role who is trusted (or not), and more the system of expertise that produces and maintains role-appropriate behavior in role occupants (Kramer, 1999). Following Dawes (1994), “We trust engineers because we trust engineering and believe that engineers are trained to apply valid principles of engineering, moreover, we have evidence every day that these principles are valid when we observe airplanes flying” (p. 24). As applied to leaders and followers, the individuals within roles may sometimes be less important than our overall trust or mistrust of the organizational role that they occupy. For example, Mulder et al. (2009) found that the relationship between sanctions and follower behavior depends on both the trustworthiness of the leader and the general trust that people have in authorities. This study also revealed that when there are options to evade the sanction, people with low levels of general trust in authorities comply less with an untrustworthy leader than with a trustworthy leader (Mulder et al., 2009).

Empirically, Ferrin et al.’s (2007) interpersonal study found that trust perceptions and cooperation exhibited a strong upward trend, facilitating one another after each successive iteration. While this may apparently contradict Kelley & Stahelski’s (1970) finding that social dilemmas tend to degrade, one critical distinction may be the role of ­communication or of other opportunities to signal cooperative behavior. Through bidirectional leader–follower communication, parties are able to obtain a better understanding of dilemmas and challenges they face, gain opportunities to explain and possibly reframe their behaviors, and obtain insights into their partner’s behaviors. Finally, both the leader and the follower then have the opportunity to make commitments about their behaviors and solicit commitments or promises about the other’s behavior, and can jointly plan to coordinate their actions in the future so that they begin to cooperate simultaneously or in an exchange relationship. All of these factors should serve to enhance and promote cooperation and solidify initial levels of trust (Ferrin et al., 2007).

In a content analysis of 988 critical incidents collected from 733 cadets in officer training courses, Lapidot et al. (2007) found a relationship between behaviors reflecting leader benevolence and trust-building incidents. Specifically, behaviors reflecting leader ability and integrity were more salient in trust-erosion incidents, and behaviors reflecting leader benevolence were more salient in trust-building incidents. Greater subordinate vulnerability increased the importance of behaviors reflecting leader integrity or ability, and vulnerability increased the likelihood that trust would be eroded. Also relevant here are Keyton & Smith’s (2009) findings using coded discourse among employees, which indicate that distrust is a unique psychological construct rather than the opposite of trust. These findings suggest that the processes involved in leader–follower trust-building may be distinct from those involved in the erosion of trust, and that distinctions should be made between lower levels of trust and outright mistrust in both leaders and followers.





5.9 Future Research Directions


In outlining directions for future research, we echo Bijlsma & Koopman’s (2003) advice that much can be gained by systematic comparison of leader–follower trust across contexts of more- and less-stable work situations, virtual and face-to-face relations, temporary and stable leader–follower relationships, and local and global organizational forms. In addition we would add cross-cultural work and the impact of trust on followership, each of which we discuss briefly in this section.





5.9.1 Cross-cultural implications


Research findings around cross-cultural interactions and the extent to which leader–­follower trust is culturally contingent are beginning to emerge. In a cross-national study of Canadian and Japanese students, Branzei et al. (2007) offer a culturally-contingent model of how trust is formed in cross-cultural encounters. Their results show that trusters are more likely to rely on culture-consistent signs and tend to neglect inconsistent ones when assessing the trustworthiness of an unfamiliar partner. More specifically, collectivists are more responsive to situational signs and unresponsive to dispositional signs in their initial assessments of trustworthiness, while the opposite is true for individualists. In addition, their results show that differential attention to dispositional and contextual signs evokes distinct types of trustworthiness beliefs, which are likely to impact the development of trusting leader–follower relationships across varying cultural contexts. In another example, a cross-cultural comparison between Australian and Chinese followers showed that Australian followers reported higher levels of trust in their leaders than did Chinese followers, and culture moderated the mediation effects of trust on the leadership–performance relationship (Casimir et al., 2006). A four-country comparative study of the USA, Turkey, Poland, and Russia found that both followers’ affect-based and their ­cognition-based trust in their leader had a significant, but relatively modest, effect on the followers’ creativity and risk-taking behaviors (as rated by the leader) (Costigan et al., 2006). Interestingly, the cross-cultural variables of power distance and individualism–­collectivism did not significantly moderate this finding across the four countries. Finally, Huang & Van De Vliert (2006) reported a negative relationship between job formalization and trust in management in individualistic countries; this relationship was mediated by less-open employee–management communication. However, no strong link is found between these variables in collectivist countries (see also Jung et al., 2009). These research findings provide a useful foundation for understanding baseline differences in trust-­relevant factors across cultures, as well as for developing culturally-contingent models for helping both leaders and ­followers develop and maintain trusting relationships across cultural boundaries.





5.9.2 Followership and trust


Given the dearth of research on how leaders develop and maintain trust in followers, it is critical for future research to examine how trust interacts with the followership role. For example, researchers could explore whether or not active and courageous followership requires a foundation of trust, and what role trust plays in challenging leaders, “voice” behaviors, and whistle-blowing. In addition, relevant to the last section, research is necessary to examine potential cultural barriers (e.g. power distance, collectivism, uncertainty avoidance) that inhibit trust and voice and discourage versus encourage followers to question authority. It would also be useful to explore how leaders and followers can be encouraged to see that giving followers the “voice” to speak freely is not just in the ­communal interest, but in the leader’s and organization’s interests as well. Other important questions include the potential role of social contagion in the spread of trust between leaders and followers, the role of a “culture of trust” in suppressing or fostering a climate for effective followership, and perhaps most importantly, how organizations can foster the trust necessary for constructive dialogue as a mechanism to self-correct internally before a dangerous situation occurs.





5.10 Conclusion


Numerous scholars have emphasized the critical role that trust plays in leadership. Bennis (1999) highlighted trustworthiness as a key attribute of an effective leader, and Martin (1999) argues that trust is at the root of all “great leadership,” saying that leadership and trust have a unique relationship in that one means little without the other (see also Hart, 1988). McEvily et al. (2003) propose that conceptualizing trust as an organizing principle is a powerful way of integrating the diverse trust literature and distilling generalizable implications for how trust affects organizing, potentially connecting the psychological and sociological microfoundations of trust with the macrobases of organizing (see also Kramer & Tyler, 1996).

In the increasingly global organizations focused on knowledge work, creativity, and complex problem-solving, leaders who view followers as commodities or means to an end forfeit the strategic competitive advantage that leadership offers when it creates “a culture of love, forgiveness, and trust … that unlocks and empowers the untapped capabilities, overcomes the withheld commitment, and dissipates the reluctant distrust so prevalent in other leadership models that lack an authentic interest in the welfare of employees and other stakeholders” (Caldwell, 2010, p. 98). Leaders who are distant and aloof from employees and avoid connecting with them are becoming much less acceptable and far less normative, further enhancing the importance of trust. As Ernest Hemingway noted, “the best way to find out if you can trust somebody is to trust them.” This potentially precarious first step provides the core foundation for the leadership relationship, and we hope that future research will continue to explore what encourages leaders and followers to both take the risk and ultimately reap the rewards.





Acknowledgements


The authors would like to thank Angela Mouton and Cynthia Sherman for their assistance in compiling this chapter.
