7


When Leaders are Bullies: Concepts, Antecedents, and Consequences


Ståle Einarsen, Anders Skogstad, and Lars Glasø





7.1 Introduction


Superior–subordinate relationships may certainly be filled with rewards, feelings of ­recognition, and perceptions of meaning and competency for both parties. Yet, for a ­subordinate, this relationship may also form the basis for punishment, abuse, and mistreatment, accompanied by feelings of frustration, uncertainty, anxiety, and resentment (e.g. Glasø & Einarsen, 2006). Leadership research often equates a leader with a supportive and efficient leader (Kellerman, 2004), and hence mainly investigates factors associated with favorable outcomes among subordinates, such as increased motivation, performance, and positive work-related attitudes (Tepper, 2007). As is the case with the Full Range of Leadership model (Bass & Avolio, 1994), leader-oriented leadership models generally portray leader behaviors on dimensions ranging from passive to active, and from ineffective to effective. But what if leaders and supervisors in their treatment of subordinates are not passive and ineffective, but rather effective and actively bad? Moreover, what if good leaders are also bad, be it simultaneously, on another behavioral domain, or simply not being consistent in their behavior across time, situations, and subordinates (see also Aasland, et al., 2010, 2008; Schriesheim & Neider, 2010). Research on bullying at work has documented that some 5–10% of employees are subjected to severe and long-term bullying at any one time (Zapf et al., 2003), with between 50 and 80% of all cases involving a superior in the role of the alleged bully. An American study revealed that some 13.5% of all workers were severely affected by an abusive supervisor at any given time (Schat et al., 2006), while an English study showed 40% of public-sector workers to have experienced bullying from a leader during their working career (UNISON, 1997). Furthermore, there is ample evidence to conclude that consistent emotional abuse and mistreatment by leaders and managers are associated with a range of detrimental outcomes on both an individual and an organizational level (see Hogh et al., 2011; Tepper, 2007). In the following we will review the literature on supervisors who abuse and bully their subordinates, regarding both the ­concepts in use and the antecedents and outcomes studied to date. In doing so, this chapter sides with Tepper (2007), who claims that abusive supervision is a significant social problem that warrants continued scholarly interest, as well as with Burke (2006), who claims that the “dark side” of leadership needs to be better explored in order to create a more accurate view of what supervisors, leaders, and ­managers actually do to their ­subordinates and to the organizations they are paid to promote.





7.2 Concepts in Use


Research on abuse and mistreatment at work is a rather new animal in the fauna of ­organizational studies. In 1976, an American psychiatrist, Carroll M. Brodsky, published the seminal book The Harassed Worker, calling attention to the systematic mistreatment and emotional abuse of employees on all organizational levels by their superiors, and in some cases by their colleagues (Brodsky, 1976). After analyzing some 1000 cases in which employees had been injured, mistreated, or crippled while at work, Brodsky showed that such mistreatment was often carried out by psychological rather than physical means, involving subtle and discrete actions, yet with traumatic and devastating outcomes when repeatedly and systematically directed at someone who felt unable to retaliate in kind or to defend themselves. However, the book did not seem to gain much attention at the time of first publication, despite being an outstandingly valuable academic contribution that has gone on to become highly appreciated in the years since. In the mid 1980s, unaware of this book, the Swedish German-born psychiatrist and organizational psychologist Heinz Leymann came across a similar phenomenon in Swedish working life (see Leymann, 1990, 1996), and described how employees were victimized at work by superiors or colleagues, being exposed to systematic and long-term hostility and psychological abuse for no apparent reason. In parallel with this, sexual harassment of female employees received increasing attention, particularly in the USA (Gutek, 1985; Pryor & Fitzgerald, 2003). From this base, research on nonsexual bullying and emotional abuse at work spread quickly during the 1990s to many countries in Europe (see Einarsen et al., 2003 for an overview), with some few but important contributions from the USA (e.g. Ashforth, 1994, 1997; Keashly, 1998; Keashly et al., 1994). Since the turn of the millennium, research on the mistreatment and emotional abuse of employees has exploded on both sides of the Atlantic (see Einarsen et al., 2011; Keashly & Jagatic; 2011; Schyns & Hansbrough, 2010; Tepper, 2007 for reviews and an overview).

Yet the explosion of research since 2000 has come with a proliferation and plethora of concepts, terms, and definitions, especially in the USA (see Keashly & Jagatic, 2011), examples being “abusive supervision” (Tepper, 2000), “incivility” (Cortina et al., 2001), “­undermining” (Duffy et al., 2002), “petty tyranny” (Ashforth, 1994), “victimization” (Acquino & Bradfield, 2000), “bullying” (Namie & Namie, 2000), and “workplace harassment” (Bowling & Beehr, 2006). A wide range of concepts has been introduced to capture instances of leader failure, including the abuse of subordinates (see Table 7.1 for an overview). However, the most consistently used terms for the systematic and ongoing exposure to abuse and hostility from one’s supervisor are “abusive supervision” (Tepper, 2000, 2007) and “workplace bullying” (Einarsen, 1999, 2000; Hoel et al., 1999), the latter also called “mobbing” in some European countries (Leymann, 1996; Zapf, 1999).

Table 7.1 Examples of concepts and labels introduced to describe leader failure.

Concept Reference

Authoritarian leaders Adorno et al. (1950)

Machiavellian leaders Christie & Geis (1970)

Intolerable bosses Lombardo & McCall (1984)

Personalized charismatic leadership House & Howell, 1992

The dark side of charisma Hogan et al. (1990)

The dark side of leadership Conger (1990)

Crazy bosses Bing (1992)

Petty tyranny Ashforth (1994)

Flawed leadership Hogan (1994)

Derailed leadership Shackleton (1995)

Brutal bosses Hornstein (1996)

Abusive supervision Tepper (2000)

Impaired managers Lubit (2004)

Bad leaders Kellerman (2004)

Poor leadership Kelloway et al. (2004)

Psychopaths Furnham & Taylor (2004)

Toxic leaders Lipman-Blumen (2005)

Laissez-faire leadership Skogstad et al. (2007)

Destructive leadership Einarsen et al. (2007)

Tepper (2000) defines “abusive supervision” as the extent to which subordinates perceive supervisors as engaging in the sustained display of hostile verbal and nonverbal behaviors while at work, excluding physical abuse. “Bullying at work” has likewise been defined as repeated actions and practices directed against one or more workers, which are unwanted by the targets, and which may be carried out deliberately or unconsciously, but clearly cause humiliation, offence, and distress, and may interfere with work performance and/or cause an unpleasant working environment (Einarsen & Raknes, 1997). Hence both concepts relate to persistent exposure to hostile behaviors of a primarily psychological nature. According to Lutgen-Sandvik et al. (2007), workplace bullying is “a type of interpersonal aggression at work that goes beyond simple incivility and is marked by the characteristic features of frequency, intensity, duration and power imbalance” (p. 837).

Examples of the behaviors involved are persistent verbal insults or offensive remarks, persistent unfair criticism, illegitimate changes in a subordinate’s work tasks (e.g. undue work pressure), and in some few cases threats of physical abuse (Einarsen, 2000). A particularly stressful behavior is social exclusion and isolation; that is, giving someone the “silent treatment,” or as it is sometimes called, “sending someone to Coventry/Siberia” (­Williams, 2001). These behaviors are used with the aim or at least the effect of persistently humiliating, intimidating, frightening, or punishing the subordinate. Many of the unpleasant leader strategies described in studies on workplace bullying may be relatively common in the workplace (Leymann, 1996), yet when frequently and persistently directed towards the same subordinate they can be considered an extreme social source of stress (Zapf et al., 1996), particularly when there is an imbalance in the power relationship between the two parties (Einarsen et al., 2011; Keashly, 1998; Olweus, 1978), as is the case per definition in the concept of abusive supervision. The latter is probably why workplace-bullying research in practice is mainly about the persistent hostility shown by supervisors to their immediate subordinates. Research has consistently shown that supervisors are much more often the source of bullying than are colleagues, and particularly so in Anglo-American cultures (see Zapf et al., 2011). Furthermore, experiencing bullying or emotional abuse by a supervisor is generally found to be more harmful and stressful to subordinates than being attacked by one’s colleagues. Last, but not least, an important and consistent empirical finding has been that many employees are attacked by both their supervisors and one or more colleagues (see for example Einarsen & Skogstad, 1996), a fact that may be missed if one focuses solely on the abuse conducted by superiors (see Tepper, 2007 for arguments for a more narrow approach). Hence empirical findings from research on workplace bullying seem to indicate that some supervisors not only consistently behave in a hostile manner towards one or more subordinates but also lead or allow other subordinates to join in this victimization process.





7.2.1 Intentionality


An issue of constant debate in the field of bullying and abusive supervision has centered on intent. While some authors, particularly those anchored in aggression theory, see intent to cause harm on the part of the perpetrator as a key feature of the definition, others argue against including intent as a definitional characteristic (see also Keashly & Jagatic, 2011). Including intent in the definition of supervisor bullying and abuse may distinguish it from more accidental unpleasant behaviors such as incivility at work. It would also exclude single episodes of thoughtlessness or instances of fairly legitimate behaviors (see Einarsen et al., 2011). Theoretically, aggression research has generally built on the assumption that one must distinguish between accidental and intended harm (see also Keashly & Jagatic, 2011). Yet in most cases it is impossible to verify the presence of intent in cases of supervisory bullying (Hoel et al., 1999), as the only judge in this matter would be the alleged perpetrator, granting him or her a definitional veto (see also Buss, 1961). Tepper (2000) argues that some instances of abusive supervision may be more about indifference than about hostility, as has been found in studies on laissez-faire leadership, which has been shown to be ­associated with a range of negative consequences for followers and leader effectiveness (Hinkin & Schriesheim, 2008; Judge & Piccolo, 2004; Skogstad et al., 2007b).

The question would also be: intent in relation to what? (1) Intent to act in each single instance? (2) Intent to be repeated and systematic? (3) Intent to harm and victimize the target? (Einarsen et al., 2011). An arrogant and self-aggrandizing supervisor might unwittingly humiliate or ridicule others as a byproduct of his or her demonstration of superiority without having any particular intent to act as a bully, or even being aware of his/her own behavior. In one of the classic works on the psychology of aggression, Buss (1961) argues that including intent in the definition of aggression is both “awkward and unnecessary” as, among other things, “intent is a private event that may or may not be capable of verbalization, may or may not be accurately reflected in a verbal statement” (p. 2).

Following this line of reasoning, intent is problematic from both a theoretical and an applied perspective. Yet Keashly & Jagatic (2011) argue that the issue of intent is moot at the methodological level, as very few studies on aggression and abuse actually measure intent, or even perceived intent. An example of this is the study by Schat and colleagues (2006) on supervisory aggression, in which they theoretically define aggression as “behavior intended to cause harm,” but do not include intent in the actual measurement, where participants or subjects were only asked if different kinds of aggressive behavior were enacted at the workplace and who the actor was. The intent of the actor or the perceived intent by the target remained unknown. Hence it is, in our opinion, problematic and unnecessary to include intent as a criterion when defining leader bullying and abuse.





7.2.2 The need for a wider concept of destructive behavior


The concepts of workplace bullying and abusive supervision focus our attention on leaders and supervisors who intimidate followers, belittle or humiliate them in private or public, or expose them to aggressive nonverbal gestures (Aryee et al., 2007; Ashforth, 1994; Tepper, 2000). Yet leaders may also behave destructively in a way that primarily affects the organization (Einarsen et al., 2007; Kellerman, 2004; Lipman-Blumen, 2005; Vredenburgh & Brender, 1998). Leaders may be corrupt—working towards personal rather than organizational goals—hamper the execution of tasks, reduce the quality of work performed in their department, reduce the efficiency of subordinates, or hamper relationships with customers and clients (Padilla et al., 2007). Hence there is clearly also an organizational dimension in destructive leadership, which may complement our focus on bullying and abusive supervision. Based on such reasoning, Einarsen et al. (2007, 2010) describe two forms of abusive supervision, depending on whether the leader or supervisor simultaneously portrays destructive behavior against the organization.

Tyrannical leadership behavior is manifested when a leader who abuses and bullies subordinates otherwise behaves in accordance with the legitimate goals, tasks, and strategies of the organization, possibly even being a superior strategist or being high-performing in terms of technical skills or task-related efficiency. In such cases, upper management may see an efficient focus on task completion by the leader, while subordinates experience systematic abuse and bullying (Einarsen et al., 2007). Leaders who behave destructively towards subordinates may not necessarily be destructive in other interpersonal relationships, be they with customers, business partners, or upper management. Ma et al. (2004) define this phenomenon as “the paradox of managerial tyranny,” arguing that tyrannical leadership may lead to extraordinary performance, even when subordinates suffer, at least in the short run.

Leaders who demonstrate derailed leadership behavior mistreat both subordinates and the organization. Such leaders may bully, humiliate, manipulate, and deceive subordinates (see for example Ashforth, 1994; Lombardo & McCall, 1984) while at the same time conducting antiorganizational behaviors such as showing undue absenteeism, withdrawing from work, shirking, committing fraud, or stealing resources (be they financial, time, or material) (Einarsen et al., 2007; Lubit, 2004; McCall & Lombardo, 1983). An example would be a leader who uses his or her charismatic qualities for personal gains while exploiting both followers and his or her employer (Conger, 1990). Such leader characteristics are also prominent in descriptions of pseudotransformational leadership and narcissistic ­leadership (see Bass & Riggio, 2006).





7.3 Consequences of Exposure to Bullying and Abusive Supervision


In this rather new field of scientific enquiry, studies on outcomes are by far the most prevalent, as compared to antecedents and mechanisms, particularly in relation to outcomes on those targeted. These studies show that the costs of abusive and bullying leadership behavior are high on both an individual and an organizational level (see also Einarsen et al., 2011; Tepper, 2000). Being exposed to bullying and emotional abuse by one’s superiors has been classified as one of the most significant sources of stress at work (Zapf, 1999); it has even been claimed that it is a more crippling and devastating problem for employees than all other work-related stress put together (Wilson, 1991). Hence outcomes on an individual level have been studied both from a stress-theoretical perspective and from a trauma perspective (see Einarsen & Mikkelsen, 2003; Hogh et al., 2011 for reviews). Abusive and bullying leadership has also been related to organizational cost, both directly in terms of monetary cost (see Hoel et al., 2011) and indirectly in terms of altering the behaviors and attitudes of subordinates (Rafferty & Restubog, 2011). In this section we will present an overview of empirical research findings from studies focusing on either individual or organizational outcomes of abusive and bullying leadership behavior, as well as some theoretical frameworks that may help to explain these findings.





7.3.1 Individual consequences


Bullying behaviors by leaders have consistently been found to have considerable negative effects on valued individual and organizational outcomes, such as lower levels of job satisfaction and commitment (Duffy et al., 2002), increased levels of work–family conflict (Tepper, 2000), and higher levels of stress-related problems (Duffy et al., 2002; Tepper, 2000). A particularly substantial body of research has shown strong associations between exposure to workplace bullying and serious emotional, psychosomatic, and psychiatric problems (Einarsen & Mikkelsen, 2003, Hogh et al., 2011; Vie et al., 2011). According to Zapf et al. (1996), targets of bullying generally report lower levels of well-being and job satisfaction compared to other employees. In addition, low self-esteem, nervousness, and insecurity are common symptoms in this group (Leymann, 1990). Evidence has also been provided, employing longitudinal designs, for links between exposure to bullying at work and subsequent elevated risks of depression (Nielsen et al., 2012), cardiovascular disease (Kiwimaki et al., 2003), and sickness absence (Ortega et al., 2009). Evidence has even been provided for a link between exposure to abuse and subsequent physical reactions in the form of changed activity in the autonomous nerve system and the hormonal system, a finding that may help explain how an abusive supervisor can “get under the skin” of employees (see Hogh et al., 2011 for a review and discussion). Based on work with hundreds of cases, Carroll M. Brodsky (1976) identified three reaction patterns among individuals exposed to bullying behavior: (1) some targets reacted with vague physical symptoms such as loss of strength, chronic fatigue, pains, and various aches; (2) others reacted with symptoms related to depression, such as lack of self-esteem and sleeplessness; and (3) a third group portrayed various psychological symptoms, such as hostility, hypersensitivity, loss of memory, feelings of victimization, nervousness, and social withdrawal.

Moreover, it has been argued that victims of long-term bullying may even suffer from symptoms of posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) (Leymann & Gustafsson, 1996). PTSD includes a variety of chronic emotional, behavioral, cognitive, and physiological symptoms, such as anxiety, irritability, hostility, emotional instability, social avoidance and distrust, guilt, flashbacks, nightmares, impaired concentration, and extreme physiological reactions in the presence of trauma-related stimuli. This notion has been supported cross-nationally by studies undertaken in Italy (Balducci et al., 2009), Denmark (Mikkelsen & Einarsen, 2002), and Norway (Glasø et al., 2009b). It may seem strange that people develop symptoms of PTSD when exposed to interpersonal aggression that is often indirect or solely verbal in nature, yet the link between ongoing social abuse and such symptoms seems to be established.





7.3.2 Explaining individual consequences


According to Janoff-Bulmann (1992), events are traumatic to the extent that they challenge our most basic cognitive assumptions—namely, our beliefs about: (1) the world as benevolent; (2) the world as meaningful; and (3) the self as worthy. When exposed to highly distressing events, such as abusive and bullying leadership, often in combination with collegial aggression or ostracism, these assumptions may be shattered, resulting in an intense cognitive-emotional crisis. Some victims may have difficulty in resolving this psychological crisis, and remain in a chronic state of cognitive confusion and anxiety that is characteristic of PTSD (Janoff-Bulmann, 1992). The notion of a positive link between exposure to abusive and bullying leadership, on the one hand, and the experience of seeing one’s basic assumptions of the world shattered, on the other, has been empirically supported, yet only with cross-sectional data. A study of 118 Danish targets (Mikkelsen & Einarsen, 2002) showed that the targets considered themselves to be less capable, less worthy, and even less lucky compared to a control group. In addition, the targets of bullying perceived the world as less benevolent, controllable, and just, and considered other people to be less supportive and caring. Likewise, Glasø et al. (2009b), who compared 72 targets to a matched control group in Norway, reported significant differences between targets and nontargets regarding their basic assumptions. In this study in particular, the targets’ assumptions about the “world as benevolent” were severely undermined.

The concept of “ostracism” (Williams, 2001) may also help explain the reactions to and the negative consequences of being bullied at work, as systematic exclusion and rejection from a social group (i.e. ostracism) appears to be a common feature of bullying (Beale & Hoel, 2010). According to Williams (2001), exposure to ostracism, such as by one’s immediate superior, threatens four basic social needs: (1) the need for safety and belonging; (2) the need to receive the care and love of others; (3) the need to be in control of one’s own life and of one’s interactions with others; and (4) the need for a meaningful existence. Being left in a situation where one’s social needs are seriously in jeopardy will obviously lead to intense distress, and may have harmful effects on one’s health (Glasø et al., 2009a).

Most studies in this field, and particularly in the case of workplace bullying, have investigated the direct relationship between exposure to abuse by superiors and individual outcomes. Although these linear associations are strong, there are certainly other factors which may also moderate or mediate such relationships. Building on stress theory, where individual perception and appraisal of potential stressors are important for both the immediate and the long-term reaction of subordinates, it has been proposed by many that one should look at how individual differences might shape and explain how a stressful leader can affect the health and well-being of their subordinates. An explanation of the relationship between bullying and health may therefore be sought in personality dispositions such as “sense of coherence” (SOC) (Antonovsky, 1987; Nielsen et al., 2008). SOC is described as a global orientation to view the world and the individual’s environment as comprehensible, manageable, and meaningful. It is argued that people with a strong SOC will be more resistant to stress and experience better health than people with low SOC. Alternative individual characteristics, such as negative affectivity (NA) (Watson & Clark, 1984), may play a similar role. Individuals high on NA typically focus on the ­problematic aspects of life and tend to have a negative view of themselves, of other people, and of the world in general. Accordingly, high levels of NA were found among targets of bullying in studies undertaken in Finland (Vartia, 1996), Germany (Zapf, 1999), and Norway (Glasø et al., 2007). Yet studies have also shown that such dispositions mainly play a role under conditions of low and moderate exposure to abuse (see Nielsen et al., 2008). When intense enough, personality does not seem to guard subordinates against the effect of the abusive behavior of their superior.

Another explanation may lie in the coping strategies undertaken by subordinates when facing an abusive superior. In a study testing the interactive effects of abusive supervision, ingratiation tactics, and positive affect (PA) on job tension, emotional exhaustion, and turnover intentions, Harvey et al. (2007) found that employees’ use of ingratiation combined with PA neutralized the adverse effects of abusive leadership on outcomes. In another study among 204 employees of an automotive company, Harris et al. (2007) investigated abusive leadership behavior as a predictor of performance, with the meaning of the work as a potential moderator of this relationship. The results showed that abusive leadership was negatively related to performance, yet the meaning of the work moderated the relationship. Hence, both situational and personal factors may moderate the effect of an abusive leader on the health and performance of subordinates. Much work still needs to be done in this area.





7.3.3 Organizational consequences following individual ones


By far the greatest part of the empirical research on the consequences of bullying and abusive supervision has focused on individual-level outcomes. We know far less about how such leadership behavior might affect an organization. Yet some of these individual-level consequences could easily aggregate to form serious organizational-level effects, such as increased turnover intentions and elevated levels of absenteeism (see also Hoel et al., 2011; Tepper, 2000; Tepper et al., 2006). Organizational outcomes may also arise indirectly from observers who react negatively when witnessing bullying. Quine (2001), for instance, has shown that nonbullied employees who have witnessed bullying report significantly more stress reactions than those who have not witnessed it. As an extension of this, bullying and abusive supervision have also been related to outcomes that more directly hurt the organization, such as lowered task performance, counterproductive work behavior (CWB), and lowered organizational citizenship behavior (OCB). Performance, CWB, and OCB are interesting outcomes because of their visible and invisible characteristics, their potential for positive and negative organizational outcomes, and the great extent of control subordinates might exert over them. While task performance is about how and to what extent one performs one’s basic task, how one communicates and interacts with one’s peers and superiors, how much effort one puts into one’s job, and how one maintains one’s discipline (see Sackett, 2002), OCB has been defined as “individual behavior that is discretionary, not directly or explicitly recognized by the formal reward system and that in the aggregate promotes the effective functioning of the organization” (Organ, 1988, p. 4). Reduced levels of task performance and OCB make an important contribution to both individual-level and organizational-level outcomes (Podsakoff et al., 2009). CWB is defined as when employees willfully commit acts that may harm organizations or the people within them (Spector & Fox, 2005), including theft (e.g. Greenberg, 1990), sabotage (e.g. Ambrose et al., 2002), workplace violence and aggression (e.g. Barling et al., 2009; Kelloway et al., 2006), and revenge (Bies & Tripp, 2005). Furthermore, CWB may include relatively minor behaviors such as spreading rumors, inappropriate Internet use, and littering. A study by Tepper et al. (2008) showed abusive supervision to be positively related to organization deviance in subordinates in the form of theft, sabotage, lateness, and shirking.





7.3.4 Explaining how individual reactions may lead to organizational outcomes


According to social-exchange theory, individuals will reciprocate the treatment they receive from others; this applies to both positive and negative behavior (Molm, 1988). Exposure to abusive and bullying leadership behavior is thus likely to result in some adverse actions from the victim, according to the negative-reciprocity norm (see also Ashforth, 1997; Thau et al., 2009). However, due to hierarchical factors and power ­differences between superior and subordinates, the likelihood that subordinates will retaliate directly against their bullying immediate superior is relatively small (Zellars et al., 2002). One way in which subordinates can respond to the abuse from their ­superiors, and restore a sense of autonomy and control, with little or no risk of negative consequences, is by withdrawing OCBs or lowering their efforts, and thus their level of performance. Hence, the hidden costs of abusive and bullying leadership behavior for the organization may be various and severe, as employees may respond to such exposure by withholding voluntary contributions that benefit both the supervisor and the ­organization. This notion is in line with Cropanzano & Mitchell (2005), who claim that different kinds of exchange relationships in organizations have important implications for employee behavior and, ultimately, organizational effectiveness. In line with this, two studies have reported a strong association between exposure to abusive leadership and low levels of OCB (Aryee et al., 2007; Zellars et al., 2002). In line with this, a two-wave study by Tepper et al. (2007) showed how earlier exposure to abusive supervision was related to subordinates’ increased use of so-called regulatory tactics; that is, avoiding contact with one’s superior, censoring and distorting one’s messages towards the superior, and stretching the truth to avoid problems. In a Chinese context, a study which sampled 361 employees from 17 Chinese state-owned enterprises showed that leadership justice was negatively related to employee silence and organizational retaliatory behavior (Duan et al., 2010), reactions which in the long run may affect the performance of the whole work group.

In the UK, Rayner (1997) showed that one out of four targets of bullying reported that they had left a previous job due to their experiences of mistreatment. Targets may then see leaving the organization as a coping strategy, since this act removes them from the source of the problem (see also Berthelsen et al., 2011). Some targets may quit in despair or due to sickness resulting from prolonged strain and stress. In other cases, victims may be ­ostracized or expelled from the organization against their will (Hoel et al., 2003).





7.3.5 Organizational costs


Although Hoel & Cooper (2000) found only relatively weak correlations between bullying and absenteeism in a study in the UK, they revealed that victims of bullying took on average 7 days more sick leave per year than those who neither were bullied nor had witnessed bullying taking place. Estimated on the basis of a bullying rate of 10%, this would account for a total of 18 million lost working days annually in the UK alone. The weak correlations found between absenteeism and bullying may be caused by a number of factors, including people’s tendency to underreport their own absence and the possibility that targets might turn up for work because of pressure to attend, not wanting to be associated with malingering or disloyalty, or, not least, not wanting to lose their job (Hoel et al., 2003). Moreover, labor-market considerations, lack of mobility, and difficulties in finding new employment might prevent targets from leaving their current positions (Tepper 2000).

Organizational productivity is likely to decrease as a result of bullying leadership, but this particular outcome is difficult to measure as it is likely to be the function of a number of factors, including behavioral responses to bullying in terms of withdrawal and loss of concentration, reduced job satisfaction, increasing health problems, and indirect effects of absenteeism and turnover (Hoel et al., 2003). In a British study, targets of bullying rated their own performance as around 85% of normal capacity, whereas people who had no contact with bullying incidents reported a 92% capacity (Hoel et al., 2001). According to Rayner et al. (2002), creativity and innovation are other aspects of productivity that are likely to be negatively affected by bullying. A Norwegian study in restaurants showed that those restaurants in which apprentices reported a high exposure to abusive supervision were rated as far less creative and innovative by external raters than were those where no or little bullying existed (Mathisen et al., 2008). It could be argued that as creativity naturally involves risk-taking, this might be seen as dangerous strategy for targets of bullying, as a higher internal profile would make them even more vulnerable to being bullied. Based on a meta-analysis of bullying research, Sheehan et al. (2001) calculated a bullying cost in the order of AUS$0.6–3.6 million per annum for an Australian business with 1000 employees. Similarly, at a national level, Hoel et al. (2003) concluded that costs related to absence and replacement due to bullying alone might account for close to GBP£2 billion annually.

Leymann (1990) estimated the annual costs to be between USD$30 000 and 100 000 per case, assuming that bullying and abusive supervision can go on for years, with the employee getting paid while often being unable to work due to stress in combination with long periods of sick leave, or sometimes even not being assigned proper tasks and responsibilities by their abusive supervisor, who tends to see them as totally incompetent. Thus, bullying in the workplace represents a considerable cost to employees, employers, and society (Di Martino et al., 2003). In sum, abusive and bullying leadership behaviors are detrimental to both employees and the organization. Nonetheless, more empirical research still exists on individual-level than on organizational-level outcomes.





7.4 Antecedents of Bullying and Abusive Supervision


Compared to the rather extensive documentation on the consequences of abusive and ­bullying leadership, research on antecedents of such leadership practices is rather meager (Tepper, 2007). Quite a few studies exist, however, in which the antecedents of and risk factors for becoming a target of bullying and abusive supervision are examined (see Salin & Hoel, 2011 for a review). Obviously, there are limitations concerning knowledge about supervisor misbehavior produced by relying solely on accounts provided by targets. Yet these studies show that abusive leadership and workplace bullying seem to flourish at all organizational levels, affecting males as well as females, and younger as well as older employees. They also seem to prevail in all kinds of work sectors and organizational contexts (see Notelaers et al., 2011). Furthermore, work environments with elevated levels of reported supervisory abuse and bullying seem to be characterized by high levels of stress, particularly being plagued with role-stressors (Bowling and Behr, 2006; Hauge et al., 2007). In this section we will review various empirical studies that have investigated antecedents of bullying and abusive supervision, starting with the most prolific perspective, that of individual characteristics of the leader.





7.4.1 Personality traits—the bad-man approach


Over the last 50 years, research and writings on destructive forms of leadership have strongly emphasized personality traits, exemplified by Adorno et al.’s (1950) seminal study on the authoritarian personality. The 1980s’ and 1990s’ focus on charismatic leadership also embraced the dark sides of leadership, describing and studying the phenomenon of personalized charismatic leadership (O’Connor et al., 1995; House & Howell, 1992). Although these studies often focused on how such leaders might lead an organization astray based on their personal needs, such leaders were also described as portraying abusive behaviors against followers. Based on a literature review, House & Howell (1992) substantiate the claim that such leaders differ from constructive leaders in their excessive need for power, high levels of Machiavellianism, and authoritarianism, as well as in having an external locus of control combined with low self-esteem. In their updated review of the leader-trait paradigm, Judge et al. (2009) present a comparable list of dark traits, which includes narcissism, hubris, social dominance, and Machiavellianism.

The personality construct that has probably most often been linked to unethical and bad leadership is narcissism, which has been defined by numerous characteristics, such as arrogance, feelings of inferiority, an insatiable need for recognition and superiority, hypersensitivity and anger, lack of empathy, amorality, irrationality and inflexibility, and paranoia (Rosenthal & Pittinsky, 2006). In line with this, Goldman (2006) presented a case study describing a highly narcissistic leader according to the diagnostic criteria of DSM IV-TR. However, few studies have systematically studied the relationship between narcissism and indicators of actual misbehavior while employing data from a working population. An exception to this is Blair et al. (2008), who found that leader self-ratings of narcissism were negatively related to supervisor ratings of those leaders’ interpersonal performance and integrity, and that those relationships were insignificant when using subordinate ratings. A plausible explanation for these insignificant results is, according to the authors, that the managers selected the subordinates who were asked to complete the evaluation form. Hence it is quite possible that they chose subordinates who had a positive attitude towards them and thus rated them in a positive direction. In line with this, Kiazad et al. (2010) found that supervisor-rated Machiavellianism was positively associated with subordinates’ perception of abusive supervision. However, there is a need to further scrutinize the relationship between leader narcissism—as well as Machiavellianism—and abusive supervision. As demonstrated in some studies (e.g. Judge et al., 2006), such relationships will vary depending on who is evaluating the personality factors and who is evaluating leader behavior. Because leader assessments have been shown to vary significantly between ­subordinate, supervisor, peer, and self-ratings (see for example Conway & Huffcutt, 1997), which may be a function of those parties having different experiences with the focal leader, different groups of raters should be systematically employed in future studies.

Interestingly, to our knowledge there are no explicit studies on relationships between the Big Five-factor model of personality (McCrae & Costa, 1987), the currently most widely used model of personality of the universal commoner within the normal range, and acts of bullying and abusive supervision. As CWB to a high degree overlaps with definitions of abusive supervision (Tepper, 2000) and workplace bullying (Einarsen et al., 2011)—with the exception that CWB also includes subordinates who may be destructive—we will, in the following, present some studies on the relationship between Big Five factors and CWB. In a recent metastudy on relationships between personality factors and ­organizational deviance (OD) and interpersonal deviance (ID), which reflect the two basic forms of CWB (see for example Berry et al., 2007), relatively strong relationships were found between agreeableness, conscientiousness, and emotional stability and the two behavioral outcomes (ρ between −.23 and −.46), with the strongest relationships being between agreeableness and ID (ρ = −.46) and between conscientiousness and OD (ρ = −.42). Relationships ­between extroversion and openness and the two behavioral outcomes were much weaker (ρ between −.02 and −.09). It may thus be the case that leaders systematically scoring low on emotional stability and on agreeableness and conscientiousness are prone to manifest themselves as destructive leaders. However, Big Five factors as predictors of ­bullying and abusive forms of leadership will have to be studied explicitly to confirm such relationships.

A concept closely related to emotional stability is that of NA, including negative emotions such as anxiety, depression, and hostility. According to Aquino & Thau (2009), NA has shown the most consistent relationship with various victimization measures (see for example Aquino & Bradfield, 2000; Glasø et al., 2007). In line with this, Dalal’s (2005) meta-analysis found a strong association (ρ = .41) between NA and CWB, a result which corresponds with a metastudy by Hershcovis et al. (2007) showing systematic relationships between NA and interpersonal targeted aggression (r (corrected) = .29) and organizational targeted aggression (r (corrected) = .28). Hershcovis et al. (2007) found even stronger relationships between the related concept of trait anger and interpersonal targeted aggression (r (corrected) = .43). In line with this, trait anger has also been found to be a strong predictor of aggression directed towards followers (Innes et al., 2008). Likewise, trait anger has been shown to be associated with being a perpetrator of workplace bullying (Matthiesen & Einarsen, 2007). In a study by Kant et al. (2011), leader self-rated trait anger was associated with follower-rated abusive supervision both of the entire workgroup and within dyads. To conclude, leaders scoring high on NA and trait anger are, probably, also prone to manifest abusive leadership behaviors. In his much-cited review on abusive supervision—including proposals for future research—Tepper (2007) proposes the personality variables of narcissism, agreeableness, and emotional stability (neuroticism) to be promising antecedents of abusive supervision. We propose conscientiousness, NA, and trait anger be added to this list (see also Zapf & Einarsen, 2011 for a theoretical account of such possible antecedents of bullying and abusive supervision).

Theoretically, leader traits may be related to bullying and abusive behavior in many ways (see also Wang et al., 2010). Leaders with certain traits may first of all generally portray behavior that subordinates find abusive. Yet indirect relationships may also exist between traits and such leadership behavior, in that certain traits in the leader may result in increased experience of stress at work, which again may result in more aggressive outbursts. Individual characteristics such as NA, trait anxiety, and attribution styles may for example influence the superior’s perception of organizational justice, a highly likely antecedent of abusive supervision, which we will return to later. For example, Mathisen et al. (2011) found leader agreeableness to moderate the relationship between superior-reported stress and follower-reported bullying, while Aryee and colleagues (2007) found that the relationship between a superior’s experience of interactional injustice and followers’ experiences of abusive supervision was moderated by the superior’s authoritarianism as rated by immediate subordinates. Further, Tepper et al. (2006) found that superior depression mediated the relationship between a supervisor’s procedural justice and subordinate perceptions of abusive supervision. Leaders with certain traits may also be more frequently involved in escalated interpersonal conflicts with their subordinates (see also Glasø et al., 2011), which again may escalate into angry outbursts or ongoing bullying (see also the dual-process model developed by Wang et al., 2010).





7.4.2 Individual characteristics—the bad-thought approach


In addition to personality traits, systematic and enduring cognitive patterns—such as how leaders attribute or explain to themselves why things happen and why they or other people behave as they do—may play an important role as antecedents of leader bullying and abusive supervision. For example, Martinko et al. (2002) have stated that attribution style is one of the most promising individual difference variables when studying CWB in general. Douglas & Martinko (2001) have shown that a hostile attribution style (HAS), where individuals attribute failures to external stable and intentional causes, is associated with self-reported aggression in the workplace. As an extension of this, employing a multisource design, Goh (2006) showed that HAS moderated the relationship between organizational stressors and CWB. Individuals high on HAS engaged in more CWB when stressors were high, whereas individuals low on HAS engaged in less CWB. Likewise, Hoobler & Brass (2006) showed that the relationship between superiors’ experiences of psychological contract violations and subordinates’ perceptions of abusive supervision was moderated by supervisors’ hostile attribution bias.

In their causal-reasoning theory (CRT) of CWB, Martinko and colleagues (2002) substantiate the influence of such cognitive processes, emphasizing (1) how individuals evaluate the quality of their outcomes (e.g. perceived fairness and justice) and (2) how beliefs about the causes of their outcomes (e.g. attributions) influence behavior and emotions. For example, if a leader attributes a follower’s poor performance as intentionally undermining the leader’s position, the leader may retaliate using highly aggressive means. Hence CRT proposes a two-stage process: first a type of experienced disequilibrium between oneself and the environment, and second the attribution processes, wherein one explains how the disequilibrium came about. If the leader decides that the reason for the highly dissatisfactory situation was the subordinate, aggression may result, even as an intended and planned behavior (see also Wang et al., 2010). A parallel approach is found in ­Spector & Fox’s (2005) stressor-emotion model of CWB, which shows how the individual is exposed to the environment through his or her cognitions. The individual first appraises the environment and make attributions for the causes of events and the intentions of actions taken by others, second reacts emotionally to what they see and think, and finally engages in behavior in response, which may be either constructive or counterproductive. In accordance with this line of reasoning, Ferris et al. (1995) emphasize that attributions about the other party’s intentions, be it the leader or the follower, will be strongly related to the quality of the leader–follower relationship. The idea here is that the locus of causality in a supervisor (as well as a follower) predicts his or her affective reactions. While internal attributions for negative events will probably result in negative emotions, including self-deprecation and helplessness, external attributions are related to negative affective reactions, which may be directed towards subordinates—for example, through revenge—particularly when it is felt that there are no mitigating circumstances and when actions are perceived as intentional (Martinko et al., 2002). Although studies have substantiated the idea that individuals with an external locus of control are more likely to show CWB than those with an internal locus of control (Martinko et al., 2002), this notion must to be tested in relation to abusive supervision. Hence we strongly propose that attributions and attributional style should be looked into further in the search for the roots of bullying behaviors and abusive forms of supervision.





7.4.3 Situational and contextual factors and the displacement of aggression


However, restricting our presentation to relationships between individual factors and abusive supervision makes for too limited a scope. The cognitions pointed at in the last subsection may in fact be rooted in actual situational and contextual factors. Certain traits or cognitive processes may also be relevant only when faced with actual stressors in the job situation and the social environment. In the rest of this section we will therefore look at some possible explanations that involve situational and contextual factors. Most studies so far have focused on stress and perceptions of injustice that result in abusive behaviors through a process of displacement of aggression. Studies also exist on distal contextual factors, even as far removed as the financial and climatic conditions of a nation. Yet we will start with the most proximal factor, the subordinate.





7.4.3.1 Subordinates as triggers of bullying and abusive supervision


One of the most apparent situational factors that might affect the perceptions and behaviors of leaders is subordinates. Subordinates may act as both direct and indirect antecedents of bullying and abusive behavior from a leader. According to a social-interactionist perspective, a stressful environment can affect aggression indirectly through its effect on subordinates’ behavior (Felson & Tedeschi, 1993a,b). General stress may cause some employees to violate workplace norms and to perform their work less competently than others. Employees may thus have a tendency to become targets of their superior’s abuse because stressors in their jobs generate affective and behavioral reactions in them that provoke bullying and abuse by the superior (e.g. Felson & Tedeschi, 1993a,b), be it as a hot-blooded reaction or as cold-blooded planned and strategic behavior (see also Fox & Spector, 2010 on instrumental CWB). Accordingly, Aquino & Thau (2009) propose that organizational members perceived as hostile, demanding, and interpersonally difficult will be more probable targets of aggression and victimization.

In a recent study, Tepper et al. (2011) showed that the displacement of aggression may also be related to leader–follower dissimilarities, not just stress and injustice, as will be pinpointed later. Hence leader-perceived deep-level dissimilarity with a subordinate is found to be related to follower-perceived abusive supervision through supervisor perceptions of relationship conflict with the follower. The relationship conflict showed, however, to be a mediator only when followers were perceived to have a low performance. The authors argue that abusive supervision may be a result of moral exclusion and absence of fair treatment by the superior, when he/she experiences the subordinate as different in terms of issues such as their outlook and values. This study illustrates that various factors related to the leader–follower relationship and follower behavior will influence the prevalence of abusive supervision, but more research is definitely needed in this vein.

Yet when moving in this direction, the complexity of interacting variables may be quite large, as illustrated by a study by Tepper et al. (2006) showing that leader-experienced procedural injustice was systematically related to subordinate perception of abusive supervision, through the mediation of the superior’s depression, and that this mediation only emerged when subordinates scored high on NA. The authors argue that leaders exposed to procedural injustice experience depression, which results in their behaving in an abusive way against subordinates perceived as being vulnerable and provocative, as indicated by a high score on NA. Followers may thus take the role of a provocative victim, triggering aggressive responses from their superior, at least under certain situational conditions. In line with this, Aquino & Bradfield (2000) and Aquino et al. (1999) found that employees who scored high on NA more frequently perceived themselves as victims of bullying and abuse.

In a related study, Aquino & Byron (2002) found support for the hypothesis that dominant behavior would show a curvilinear relationship with victimization from leader abuse, indicating that employees low on dominance would be experienced as weak and exploitable and employees high on dominance would break social norms and therefore be more easily exposed to retaliation and abuse. Hence both the notion of the provocative subordinate and the notion of leader-displaced aggression may be fruitful in understanding destructive forms of leadership, while showing the complex interplay between superiors and subordinates at work. For this we now turn to studies in the paradigm of displaced aggression.





7.4.3.2 Stress and perceived injustice as situational antecedents


A leader’s perception of stress and being mistreated by their employer has been shown to be a significant predictor of subordinate-rated abusive leadership (Aryee et al., 2007; Hoobler & Brass, 2006, Tepper et al., 2006). The importance of injustice and fairness in this regard is in accordance with a metastudy by Cohen-Charash & Spector (2001) showing systematic relationships between procedural and distributive justice and CWB in general. In line with this, a study in 70 Norwegian restaurants (Mathisen et al., 2011) showed that subordinate reports of exposure to workplace bullying were related to both superior-reported stress and superior personality—in this case the agreeableness of the chef—but that superior-reported stress was by far the strongest predictor of bullying. In fact, agreeableness only had an effect when leader stress was low. Bullying was lowest when the chef was not stressed and was highly agreeable, while most bullying occurred when the chef was highly stressed, irrespective of their level of agreeableness. Such findings are in line with the contemporary dominant explanation of bullying and abusive supervision; that is, the displaced-aggression perspective, which states that leaders experiencing stress (Hauge et al., 2010) or lack of organizational justice (Aryee et al., 2007; Tepper et al., 2006) expose their followers to abusive treatment. As pointed out by Hoobler & Brass (2006), a leader or manager who experiences unjust treatment in an organization may not necessarily react against the experienced source of injustice, but instead against a third party who is perceived to be more vulnerable, namely a subordinate. That is, they displace their frustration and aggression on an inferior third party.

Regarding alternative situational antecedents of destructive forms of leadership, the number of empirical studies is rather limited, which may be a surprise. Yet in their review of organizational causes of workplace bullying, Salin & Hoel (2011) document work organization and design, organizational culture and climate, and organizational change as being important precursors of bullying, at least as reported by subordinates, substantiating the idea that such contextual factors also predict leader bullying and abuse. Yet of all such factors, role conflict and role ambiguity have been shown to be the most consistent predictors, a conclusion supported by Bowling & Beehr’s (2006) metastudy showing that the role stressors of role conflict (ρ = .44) and role ambiguity (ρ = .30), together with work constraints (ρ = .54), are the strongest predictors of workplace harassment. Hence leader bullying and abuse seems to thrive where expectations are contradictory or unpredictable, and where an individual’s decision authority is limited.





7.4.3.3 Situational and contextual antecedents—power, change, and culture


Power distance, defined as the degree to which employees accept and believe that organizational, institutional, and societal power should be distributed unequally (see for example Hofstede, 1980), has also been shown to influence how leader behavior is perceived. Individuals who score high on power distance are likely to respect and trust their superiors, and it has been suggested that abusive supervision may have less impact for employees who accept a high power distance. Lian et al. (2011) found that power distance moderated the relationship between abusive supervision and interpersonal injustice, most strongly for individuals reporting a low power-distance orientation.

As regards organizational culture, Brodsky (1976) stated that a necessary condition for bullying to occur is a culture which permits and rewards it. Rayner (1998) found that the main reason given by targets of bullying in a UK study was that “Bullies can get away with it” and “workers are too scared to report it” (p. 582). In studies on sexual harassment, tolerant upper management and a lack of clear organizational norms and policies, including safe complaints procedures, are related to a greater number of instances of misbehavior (see Pryor & Fitzgerald, 2003). Namie et al. (2011) claim that today’s business world generally prefers and nourishes an individualistic, aggressive, and abusive response to interpersonal conflicts. Accordingly, Björkqvist et al. (1994) employed the term “danger-ratio effect” to describe the cost–benefit evaluations people do before eventually engaging in aggressive behaviors such as bullying. In line with this Luthans et al. (1998) state that “dark leaders” have a propensity to emerge in cultures that endorse collectivism and the avoidance of uncertainty. In a culture with high uncertainty avoidance, individuals feel threatened by ambiguous situations and appreciate strong leaders who can provide a high degree of structure. In collective cultures characterized by strong loyalty and cooperation, individuals also prefer strong leaders who can guide and unite people around a common goal. In line with such findings, Tepper (2007) calls on researchers to examine the moderating role cultural orientations may have in abusive supervision. As an extension of this, Van de Vliert & Einarsen (2008) and Van de Vliert et al. (2010) have shown how endorsements and reports of exposure to destructive leadership practices, from now on referred to as “abusive supervision,” vary between countries based on climatic and economic conditions, with abusive supervision being particularly accepted and prevalent in countries with harsh climates and general poverty.

Hence contextual factors may be important as distant antecedents. For instance, Padilla et al. (2007) claim that during times of instability, leaders can enhance their power and exploit the situation at the cost of their followers (see also Glad, 2002). In line with this notion, more studies substantiate the idea that organizational changes are associated with bullying. Hoel & Cooper (2000) found that bullied employees reported more cases of “major organizational change,” “budget cuts,” “major technological change,” and “major internal restructuring” than did nonbullied ones. Skogstad et al. (2007a), in their representative study of the Norwegian working force, found a direct relationship between work-environment changes and reductions in staff and pay, and bullying. In a similar study by Baillien & De Witte (2009), the relationship between organizational change and bullying was fully mediated by role conflict and job insecurity. Both studies show rather low direct relationships between organizational changes and bullying, which raises the question of whether organizational changes by themselves trigger bullying. In line with the literature showing organizational justice to be the most consistent predictor of abusive supervision, Barling et al. (2011) argue that it may not be organizational changes per se that result in victimization, but the way that those changes are carried out. Hence the way in which leaders participate in the execution of organizational changes will, probably, influence subordinate perceptions of abusive leadership.





7.5 Conclusion and Directions for Future Research


So far, we certainly know that bullying and supervisory abuse exist in all organizational settings and on all hierarchical levels (see Zapf et al., 2011 for a review), with severe detrimental effects for those targeted as well as for the organization. Yet we know very little about why leaders expose their subordinates to such treatment and even less about how we might prevent it in the first place and manage it when it occurs (see Einarsen et al., 2011 for some suggestions). With reference to the latter option, we also know too little about how some subordinates cope with abusive superiors and prevent them from having such a traumatic effect on their health, performance, and family life (see Hogh et al., 2011; Yagil et al., 2011 for exemptions). Moreover, we do not know much about what subordinates might do to stop supervisors who tend to bully and abuse them. In this regard, we also need to understand better how subordinates might safely complain and blow the whistle on abusive supervisors (see Hoel & Einarsen, 2011) and how organizations can set up safe policies and procedures to protect subordinates against such experiences (see Rayner & Lewis, 2011).

In order to be better able to prevent and intervene in cases of bullying and abusive leadership, we need to know far more about under what circumstances leaders might behave badly towards their subordinates. The personality and thought processes of the leader, and correspondingly of his or her subordinates, seem to play some role in this. Yet stressors in the organizational environment, including perceived injustices from above and conflicting and unclear role expectancies, seem also to play a crucial part, perhaps via a process of displaced aggression. According to Padilla et al. (2007), introducing the concept of the toxic triangle, and as documented in the present chapter, the antecedents of destructive leadership may be found in characteristics of both the leader, their followers, and the organizational context, and in the dynamic interplay between these factors. To make the picture even more complex, the fit between superior and subordinate characteristics (e.g. personality and values) may play an important role (see Kristof-Brown et al., 2005 for an overview). We thus need study designs that integrate these characteristics.

In order to act upon this pertinent social problem in the workplace, it is crucial that we gain a more thorough understanding of the context in which this misbehavior takes place. Regarding the context, many factors at different levels may contribute to explain abusive leadership over and above what is potentially explained by the individual dispositions of the leader and the subordinate, be it the actual work situation of the leader, characteristics of the psychosocial working environment, the organizational culture, or perhaps even the climatic and economic conditions surrounding the given organization.

Regarding the possibility of preventing and managing such misbehavior in leaders and supervisors, we must acknowledge that this matter may have a legal perspective, and that we must open the field to more multidisciplinary work than has been carried out so far, with social-psychology as by far the dominant approach (see Hoel & Einarsen, 2010a,b; Yamada, 2011 for discussion on legal approaches). In the USA, all social movements on interpersonal abuse and mistreatment, be it child abuse, domestic violence, or discriminatory violence, have been able eventually to convince legislators to enforce state or federal legislation to negatively sanction such misconduct (see Namie et al., 2011). Yet what kinds of legislation will work, how they will work, and how to make them work are important questions still awaiting an answer (see Hoel & Einarsen, 2011; Lippel, 2010 for some attempts).

Furthermore, whilst there is most certainly a strong need for more research on bullying and abusive leadership on its own accord, we nevertheless suggest that future research and theory-building should also include variables measuring constructive forms of leadership, in order to provide a comprehensive view of the leadership phenomenon (see for example Einarsen et al., 2007). For example, it is of interest to note that narcissism in leaders may also have its bright sides (see for example Judge et al. 2009; Rosenthal & Pittinsky, 2006). In their study of CEOs, Chatterjee & Hambrick (2007) show that narcissism is positively related to strategic dynamism and the number and size of acquisitions, and that the performance of firms with narcissistic CEOs is generally neither better nor worse than that of firms with non-narcissistic CEOs. In a similar vein, exposing one or more subordinates to bullying and abuse does not preclude someone from being an effective leader or from having good relationships with other subordinates—this is the basic tenet of the leader–member exchange (LMX) approach to leadership. An abusive supervisor may at the same time be a brilliant strategist, a highly knowledgeable professional, a creative genius, or a charismatic front figure. Yet being a bully may instead prevent them from leading effectively. In line with a conglomerated-behavior approach to organizational behavior (see Einarsen et al., 2010; Van de Vliert, 2007), the possibility of abusive supervision taking place in concert with constructive forms of leadership must be put in greater focus, in order to enhance our understanding of leadership in general and effective leadership in particular.

Rafferty, A.E. & Restubog, S.L.D. (2011). The influence of abusive supervisors on followers’ organizational citizenship behaviours: the hidden costs of abusive supervision. British Journal of Management, 22(2), 270–285.

Rayner, C. (1997) The incidence of workplace bullying. Journal of Community & Applied Social Psychology, 7, 199–208.

Rayner, C. (1998). Workplace bullying: Do something! The Journal of Occupational Health and Safety, 14, 581–592.

Rayner, C. & Lewis, D. (2011). Managing Workplace Bullying: The Role of Policies. In S. Einarsen, H. Hoel, D. Zapf, & C.L. Cooper. Bullying and Harassment in the Workplace. Developments in Theory, Research and Practice, 2nd edition. USA: Taylor & Francis.

Rayner, C., Hoel, H., & Cooper, C.L. (2002). Workplace Bullying. What We Know, Who Is to Blame, and What Can We Do? London: Taylor & Francis.

Rosenthal, S.A. & Pittinsky, T.L. (2006). Narcissistic leadership. The Leadership Quarterly, 17(6), 617–633.

Schat, A.C.H., Frone, M.R., & Kelloway, E.K. (2006). Prevalence of workplace aggression in the US workforce: findings from a national study. In E.K. Kelloway, J. Barling, & J.J. Hurrell, editors. Handbook of Workplace Violence. Thousand Oaks: Sage.

Schriesheim, C.A. & Neider, L.L. (2010). Abusive and supportive leadership: Two sides of the same coin. In L.L. Neider & C.A. Shriesheim, editors. The “Dark” Side of Management. USA: Information Age Publishing.

Tepper, B., Moss, S., Lockhart, D., & Carr, J. (2007). Abusive supervision, upward maintenance communication, and subordinates’ psychological distress. The Academy of Management Journal ARCHIVE, 50(5), 1169–1180.

UNISON (1997) Bullying at Work: Bullying Survey Report. London: UNISON.

Van de Vliert, E. (2007). Complex interpersonal conflict behavior: theoretical frontiers. Hove, UK: Psychological Press.

Bullying and Harassment in the Workplace. Developments in Theory, Research and Practice, 2nd edition. USA: Taylor & Francis.
