Yarker, J., Lewis, R., Donaldson-Feilder, E., & Nielsen, K. (2012). Stress Management Competence: The Development of a Measure. In preparation.





9


Transformational Leadership and Psychological Well-being: Effects on Followers and Leaders


Kara A. Arnold and Catherine E. Connelly





9.1 Introduction


Leaders hold a central role in the success of any organization. They are responsible not only for an organization’s strategy and how it is operationalized and implemented but also for the well-being of their followers. In this chapter we therefore describe research focused specifically on the relationship between transformational leadership behaviors and employee psychological well-being. We present a critical analysis of this body of work, focusing on the strengths and limitations of the existing literature, and making suggestions for future research. We also summarize the literature that examines how transformational leadership behaviors affect leaders’ psychological well-being. Currently, the transformational-leadership literature focuses on the impact that transformational leadership in supervisors has on their followers: their performance, their job-related attitudes and their well-being (for a summary see Bass & Riggio, 2006). Much less empirical work has focused on the effects of enacting transformational leadership on leaders themselves, despite its potential importance.





9.1.1 Why transformational leadership?


The relationship between supervisor and employee is an important influence on employee well-being (e.g. Cooper & Payne, 1991; Landeweerd & Boumans, 1994; Skakon et al., 2010; Tepper, 2000). A summary of research related to employee occupational health and well-being at the turn of the century argued that managerial style was one of four main concerns related to employee well-being (Sparks et al., 2001). In essence, managers’ behaviors can either create or reduce stress for employees. When supervisors communicate effectively and provide support to employees, stress is reduced; on the other hand, when they engage in abusive, bullying behaviors, stress is increased (Sparks et al., 2001).

Managers with employees who are less stressed may also find it easier to engage in positive leadership behaviors, resulting in a positive spiral. The importance of psychosocial workplace factors in understanding employee well-being is emphasized by a study which found that incremental variance in the psychological well-being of employees was ­accounted for by supervisory behaviors, after controlling for age, health practices, support from other people at work, support from home, stressful life events, and stressful work events (Gilbreath & Benson, 2004). Indeed, supervisory behaviors are an important influence on employee well-being. Yet, as Gilbreath & Benson (2004) note, they used a newly-developed composite of supervisory behaviors in their study, and “it is likely that particular types of supervisor behaviour … promote employees’ psychological well-being” (p. 263). One example of supervisory behavior that has the potential to influence follower well-being is transformational leadership.

Transformational leadership has been widely hailed, both by academics (e.g. Bass, 1985, 1998; Bass & Riggio, 2006) and practitioners (e.g. Desvaux & Devillard-Hoellinger, 2008; Desvaux et al., 2007), as a way to inspire loyalty and to motivate people to succeed and perform at higher levels than they thought possible. Transformational leadership, as elucidated by Bass, Avolio, and colleagues (e.g. Avolio & Bass, 1995; Avolio et al., 1991, 1999; Bass & Avolio, 1993, 1994; Bass et al., 1996; Hater & Bass, 1988; Yammarino & Bass, 1990), is part of a “full-range” model of leadership. The full-range model includes transactional and laissez-faire aspects of leadership in addition to those that are transformational (Antonakis, 2012; Avolio, 1999). For the purposes of this review, we focus on transformational leadership, as these behaviors add to the positive effects of transactional leadership (Bass & Riggio, 2006).

The first dimension of transformational leadership, idealized influence, consists of ­characteristics that are attributed to the leader by followers, as well as the actual behaviors that the leader engages in. Idealized influence is evident when a leader has strong values and follows these values with appropriate actions (Bass & Riggio, 2006). When followers react positively to their leaders, for example by viewing them as role models, one may attribute this to idealized influence. A related component of transformational leadership is inspirational motivation. A leader who communicates a clear and uplifting vision, who convinces followers to share this vision, and who holds high expectations for followers, is demonstrating this dimension (Bass & Riggio, 2006). These first two dimensions, when combined, are similar to charismatic leadership.

The third dimension of transformational leadership is intellectual stimulation. Leaders with high levels of this cognitive aspect expect and encourage followers to think of different ways of solving problems, are open to new ideas, and encourage creativity (Bass & Riggio, 2006).

The final component of transformational leadership is individual consideration: supporting and developing each follower to his or her full capacity. This involves treating employees as individuals, knowing their needs and capabilities, and mentoring them (Bass & Riggio, 2006; Rafferty & Griffin, 2006).

Transformational leadership can be reliably measured by the Multifactor Leadership Questionnaire (MLQ; Bass & Avolio, 2000). While there are other measures of transformational leadership in the literature (e.g. Alimo-Metcalfe & Alban-Metcalfe, 2001; Carless et al., 2000; Podsakoff et al., 1990; Rafferty & Griffin, 2004), the MLQ is the most widely used (Antonakis, 2012; Eagly et al., 2003). Transformational leadership was the most studied of all leadership theories in the 1990s (Judge & Piccolo, 2004), and the most widely published leadership theory in the Leadership Quarterly journal for the past 2 decades (Gardner et al., 2010; Lowe & Gardner, 2000). As Antonakis (2012) notes, “this research stream dominates the leadership landscape—whether deservingly or not” (p. 257). Given how widely this leadership theory has been advocated, it is important to understand whether transformational leadership can affect the health and well-being of both followers and leaders; and if so, why and under what conditions these relationships occur. In this chapter we focus on summarizing and critiquing the research that answers these questions.





9.2 Well-being Outcomes: Burnout, Affect, and Mental Health


The concept of “well-being” is very broad and can be composed of both physical and psychological health (e.g. Liu et al., 2010). In this chapter we set aside the topic of physical health, and focus exclusively on three aspects of psychological well-being (Keyes et al., 2002): burnout, affect, and mental health.

The study of employee stress has a longstanding tradition in the occupational-health ­literature (e.g. Sulsky & Smith, 2005). While not frequently studied as an outcome of leadership style or behavior (Skakon et al., 2010), the problems associated with stress that arise due to work and other aspects of life have been studied extensively in regards to other working conditions (see Danna & Griffin, 1999 for a review). Within the transformational-leadership literature, burnout is one of the most frequently measured well-being outcomes; our review will therefore focus on burnout as an aspect of stress (Liu et al., 2010). In keeping with a positive psychology approach (Luthans, 2002; Turner et al., 2002) and recently published work, we also consider affective well-being (emotions) and mental health as outcomes. Our review provides more detail on the studies summarized in Skakon et al. (2010). The focus is on context-free psychological well-being, which excludes context-dependent constructs such as job satisfaction (for a summary of the link with job satisfaction, see Bass & Riggio, 2006).





9.2.1 Effects of transformational leadership on follower burnout


9.2.1.1 Direct relationship?


The majority of studies focusing on the link between a transformational leadership style and employee burnout are correlational in nature. The results of these correlational studies have been equivocal. For example, results differ depending on whether the overall measure of transformational leadership is used or whether it is broken down into component behaviors. The relationships can also differ depending on the burnout dimension that is examined.

One of the earliest studies to examine the connection between transformational leadership and employee burnout hypothesized a positive relationship (Seltzer et al., 1989). The authors suggested several reasons for the connection. For example, a leader’s idealized influence might lead employees to put in longer hours at work and more energy into their work roles. Intellectual stimulation and the uncertainty and risk involved in doing things differently were theorized as a potential threat to well-being, and the focus on group orientation might mean that individual employees could not consider their own needs to the extent necessary to maintain their health. A survey of 277 MBA students was used to test the hypotheses. Controlling for gender, age, and marital status, results showed that stress symptoms were negatively correlated with all four transformational dimensions, and comparatively more negatively correlated with burnout. Multiple-regression analysis also showed that stress and burnout were reduced when supervisors were perceived to be charismatic and individually considerate; however, stress and burnout were higher when a supervisor was perceived as intellectually stimulating. Specifically, a factor analysis of the intellectual-stimulation items produced two factors, one of which (“My supervisor requires that we back up our opinions with good reasoning”) was positively correlated with burnout when controlling for charisma. The authors concluded that while “a part of intellectual stimulation, the component requiring backing opinions with reason is positively associated with burnout and the reporting of stress symptoms … overall the data indicate that a transformational style may help to reduce burnout in general” (Seltzer et al., 1989, p. 184).

Other correlational studies support a direct negative relationship between transform­ational leadership and burnout. A study of Canadian teachers in community colleges found that transformational leadership was negatively related to burnout, and that the effect was stronger than personal factors such as demographics, personality, and ­self-esteem, but not as strong as organizational factors such as job demands, social support, and organizational support (Leithwood et al., 1996). Similarly, a study of adult students enrolled in a Masters of Management program (various occupations) found a negative association between transformational leadership behavior in mentors and job stress in mentees (Sosik & Godshalk, 2000). Controlling for age, job level, job tenure, education level, industry, and mentor gender, findings suggested that transformational leadership in mentors was associated with increased receipt of mentoring functions and decreased job-related stress. Another study, of service-sector employees in Canada, confirmed that transformational leadership was negatively associated with job stress, and that job stress was positively ­associated with burnout (Gill et al., 2006).

Other studies have also investigated the components of transformational leadership ­separately. In a sample of employees and leaders in mental-health teams, employee perceptions of leaders’ charisma (idealized influence), inspirational motivation, and individual consideration (i.e. three dimensions of transformational leadership) were negatively related to the emotional-exhaustion dimension of burnout (Corrigan et al., 2002). Intellectual stimulation was not significantly correlated with employee emotional exhaustion, but was positively correlated with the personal-accomplishment component of burnout (as were inspirational motivation and individual consideration).

A study of 480 senior managers in an Australian law-enforcement organization focused on inspirational motivation and links between this and burnout (Densten, 2005). Structural equation-modeling analyses showed that concept-based inspirational motivation (behaviors communicating standards and expectations) was negatively associated with emotional exhaustion but that there was no relationship between image-based inspirational motivation (the items used to measure this focused on creation of vivid ideas and images) and emotional exhaustion. Image-based inspirational motivation did, however, have a “positive direct effect on personal accomplishment and a negative direct effect on depersonalization” (Densten, 2005, p. 113). The author discusses the possibility that visioning behaviors are negatively associated with burnout because they provide a context within which employees can create meaning in their work, reducing frustration and increasing self-esteem and efficacy. While it is interesting to note that different aspects of inspirational motivation may have differential effects on each of the facets of burnout, the study provides little evidence to rationalize the split between image- and concept-based inspirational motivation.





9.2.1.2 Potential moderators?


The fact that not all studies have found negative associations between transformational leadership and burnout suggests that moderators are present. Generally, studies showing no effects or positive effects have focused on links between specific dimensions of transformational leadership and burnout, have considered other workplace stressors, or have been conducted in different cultures.

Employees in a Norwegian information-technology organization completed a survey asking about transformational leadership behaviors of their supervisors, neuroticism, and burnout. Transformational leadership was not significantly associated with emotional exhaustion, was significantly positively associated with professional efficacy, and was negatively associated with cynicism (Hetland et al., 2007). Interestingly, neuroticism showed stronger associations with the dimensions of burnout than did leadership, and was directly and positively related to exhaustion and cynicism, and negatively related to professional efficacy. This suggests that follower personality might be an important moderator of the effects of transformational leadership on burnout dimensions. Followers with different outlooks on work, arising from their different personality characteristics, may interpret and respond differently to the same transformational leadership behavior.

A study of 601 Finnish nurses and nurse managers (Kanste et al., 2007) considered job status as another potential moderator. With age, employment status, work task, and working hours as controls, transformational leadership was positively associated with personal accomplishment. Interestingly, employment status and work task moderated the effect of transformational leadership on emotional exhaustion and depersonalization such that transformational leadership acted as a buffer against emotional exhaustion and depersonalization; more so for temporary workers than for permanent workers. The authors of this study came to a similar conclusion to that of Hetland et al. (2007), suggesting that “leadership behaviour that works well with one subordinate may not be as efficient with others” (Kanste et al., 2007, p. 737).

The question of whether follower personality will moderate the effects of transformational leadership behavior on follower burnout was investigated using samples of 91 ­human-resources advice-firm employees and 95 dyads of leaders and employees (190 participants total) from several firms in the Netherlands (De Hoogh & Den Hartog, 2009). In both samples, locus of control moderated the relationship between charismatic leadership and burnout such that charisma was negatively related to burnout, yet this relationship was stronger for individuals low on internal locus of control and weaker for individuals high on internal locus of control. In the first study, neuroticism also moderated the relationship between charismatic leadership and burnout; the negative relationship between charismatic leadership and burnout was weaker for followers who were more neurotic. Because charisma is but one dimension of transformational leadership, future research should incorporate the other dimensions in studies with similar methodological strengths.

In one experimental study, transformational and transactional leadership styles were ­manipulated to examine their influence on individuals’ performances on a stressful task, and on perceived social support, self-efficacy beliefs, emotions, and stressor appraisals ­(Lyons & Schneider, 2009). All four transformational components were manipulated through the use of videotaped instructions associated with a stressful task that student participants were about to complete. In the video, the actor depicted either a transformational, transactional, or management-by-exception leadership style. Among other findings, the results showed that the transformational leadership condition was associated with lower threat appraisals and lower negative affect (Lyons & Schneider, 2009). This study appears to support the correlational studies showing a negative relationship between burnout and transformational leadership. The strength of the experimental design used in this study is its high internal validity and ability to establish causation, although this is counterbalanced by the lack of external and ­ecological validity.





9.2.1.3 Mediators?


Unfortunately, little empirical research has been conducted on the mechanisms linking transformational leadership to follower burnout and stress. A study of nurses in a Belgian hospital (Stordeur et al., 2001) controlled for work stressors (physical, psychological, and social) when investigating the relationship between transformational leadership in supervisors and emotional exhaustion in employees. The authors speculated that the absence of any effect for transformational leadership could be due to the fact that leadership may have an indirect effect on burnout. This indirect effect was not tested, but correlations suggested that transformational leadership might affect burnout via its effect on role ambiguity. Job control, social support, and team cohesion are also mentioned as potential mediators.

One study has investigated potential explanatory mechanisms. Liu et al. (2010) found that trust in the leader and self-efficacy both mediated the relationship between transformational leadership and stress in a sample of 745 employees from various ­industries in Beijing and Hong Kong. This study adds to knowledge of the relationship between transformational leadership and employee stress by investigating this in the Chinese ­context. As the authors suggest, “scholars and practitioners cannot simply assume that [transformational leadership], which is effective in Western societies, can be automatically transferred to Chinese societies” (Liu et al., 2010, pp. 470–471).





9.2.2 Effects of transformational leadership on follower affect and mental health


9.2.2.1 Direct relationship?


Organizations have begun to be cognizant not only that reducing employee burnout is necessary but also that increasing positive forms of well-being can be of benefit. One of the first studies to investigate the link between transformational leadership and affect was a lab study looking at “emotional contagion,” or when one person mimicks another’s emotions and thereby actually experiences those emotions (Cherulnik et al., 2001). This study approached the research question with a unique method: facial expressions were coded in a lab setting. Participants were recorded while watching a video of student leaders giving a standardized speech (six tapes were used, representing both charismatic and noncharismatic leadership). The viewers’ facial expressions were coded according to four nonverbal responses: smile, smile intensity, looking away, and seconds looking. The differences between the charismatic and noncharismatic conditions were significant for each outcome. The authors concluded that “expressive nonverbal style generally associated with charismatic leaders can evoke similar, relatively strong and positively toned expressions of emotion from those who are exposed to it” (Cherulnik et al., 2001, p. 2156).

Bono & Ilies (2006) investigated the emotional-contagion hypothesis in leader–follower relationships, and reported the results of four studies. While not focused explicitly on transformational leadership, the first two studies showed that charismatic leaders were perceived as expressing more positive emotions, which is an important link in the purported causation chain between transformational leadership in supervisors and employee affective well-being. Two experimental studies then confirmed the link between leaders’ expressions of positive emotion and followers’ positive moods (Bono & Ilies, 2006). In one experiment, participants watched a video depicting a leader who demonstrated either high positive ­emotions or low positive emotions. The authors noted that the results of this study could be due to the positive emotion expressed by the leader in the video, or could be due to other leader behaviors, such as the articulation of a compelling vision or influence tactics. The leader in the high-positive video was also rated as being more attractive than the leader in the low-positive video. A second experiment was therefore designed to manipulate positive mood independent of these other factors. This experiment was also designed to engage participants (174 university students) in a more relevant and realistic leader–follower experience (see Bono & Ilies, 2006 for details). The findings of this experiment confirmed a positive link between positive emotional expression in the leader and positive mood in the participants. While this study did not address transformational leadership per se, it does confirm that charismatic leaders are viewed as expressing more positive emotions, and that leaders who display more positive emotions do transmit these to their followers.

Two studies conducted in the nonprofit sector confirm a positive relationship between transformational leadership and followers’ emotions. In a cross-sectional survey study of 43 Australian employees, transformational leadership (measured using the transformational leadership scale (TLS): Podsakoff et al., 1990) and affective well-being were significantly and positively associated (McMurray et al., 2010). Another study of musicians in nonprofit orchestras tested the mediating effects of emotion on the transformational leadership–performance relationship (Rowold & Rohmann, 2009). This study suggests that transformational leaders have a positive influence on follower affective well-being by ­eliciting more positive emotions and by decreasing negative emotions.

Overcoming the limitations of cross-sectional data, one experience-sampling study confirmed the link between transformational leadership and affective well-being. In this study of 57 health-care workers, the link between transformational leadership in supervisors and employee emotions was examined (Bono et al., 2007). In addition to the leadership effects, the authors were also interested in emotional regulation and stress. Participants reported their momentary positive (happiness, enthusiasm, and optimism) and negative (anxiety, anger, and irritation) emotions four times a day for 2 weeks, and their perceptions of supervisor transformational leadership were measured 1 month prior to the experience-sampling portion of the study through a survey using the MLQ. Employees who had highly transformational supervisors reported having more positive emotions while at work, but transformational leadership was not linked to negative emotions or emotional regulation. The authors concluded that the “results of this study demonstrate the powerful role that managers and supervisor have on employee emotions” (Bono et al., 2007, p. 1364). Studies appear to converge on finding a positive relationship between transformational leadership and follower positive affect. However, there may also be important conditions under which transformational leadership has a larger effect on employee affect.





9.2.2.2 Potential moderators?


The conditions under which a leader is transformational may play a key role in the strength of the relationship between transformational leadership and employee affect. A paper focused on the interactive effects of procedural justice and transformational leadership on the affective responses of followers reported on three studies using self-esteem and emotions as outcomes (De Cremer, 2006). First, an experiment using vignettes and 69 Dutch undergraduate students showed that the effects of procedural justice on negative affect were stronger in the highly-transformational condition. Transformational leadership moderated this relationship. Importantly, the direct effect of transformational leadership on negative emotion was significant and negative. A second laboratory study was then conducted in which 57 Dutch undergraduate students were immersed in the leadership situation. This study corroborated the findings of Study 1. The third study was a field study designed to complement the first two. Human-resources managers in the Netherlands asked civil servants to participate in a questionnaire. A similar moderating effect was found, whereby the effect of procedural justice on organizational-based self-esteem was stronger when transformational leadership was high. The fact that there were significant interactions between procedural justice and transformational leadership corroborates to some extent our observations of the literature on transformational leadership and burnout. Essentially, these results suggest that organizational factors, such as working conditions, need to be incorporated into studies that examine when transformational leadership is more likely to affect employee affect. We were unable to locate any published studies investigating moderators of the relationship between transformational leadership and employee mental health.





9.2.2.3 Potential mediators?


McColl-Kennedy & Anderson (2002) asked whether emotional reactions to transformational leaders mediate the leadership–performance relationship. They found that transformational leadership had a significant influence on frustration and optimism. The negative influence on frustration appeared to affect performance to a larger degree than did the positive optimism emotion. The results suggest that high transformational leadership is associated with higher optimism, lower transformational leadership is associated with higher levels of frustration, and transformational leadership has an impact on affective well-being.

Another study exploring the leadership–performance linkage looked at employee positive mood as a mediator of this relationship (Tsai et al., 2009). A longitudinal study of 282 employee and supervisor dyads in insurance companies in Taiwan found that transformational leadership had both a direct and an indirect effect on performance via positive mood, based on the theory of emotional contagion. The findings indicated that employee perceptions of higher transformational leadership were associated with more positive moods (e.g. happy, pleased, joyful).

Some studies have begun to address the mechanisms linking transformational leadership in supervisors to follower affect and mental health. A summary of the literature on positive leadership theorized four potential mediators of the transformational leadership–employee psychological well-being relationship: self-efficacy, meaning, trust, and identity ­(Sivanathan et al., 2004). Published studies have reported results related to meaning, self-efficacy, and trust as mediators.

The meaning that employees ascribe to their work has been shown to mediate the relationship between transformational leadership and employee affect and mental health ­(Arnold et al., 2007). Two studies (one in health care and one in the service sector) showed that meaning (a) partially mediated the link between transformational leadership and affective well-being and (b) fully mediated the relationship between transformational leadership and employee mental health, after controlling for humanistic work values. Transformational leadership had a positive effect on employee well-being despite different conceptualizations and measurements of employee well-being (affect and mental health), and in both studies employee perceptions that work is meaningful accounted either partially or fully for this relationship. The conceptualization and measurement of psychological health is an issue that we return to in Section 9.2.3.

In one manuscript, three studies investigating the effects of transformational leadership on employee mental health were reported (Barling et al., 2008). The first investigated the lagged effects (measured with the general health questionnaire (GHQ)): in this 6-week longitudinal study of 52 employees of a Canadian home-heating fuel-delivery service, transformational leadership at Time 1 predicted mental health at Time 2 while controlling for mental health at Time 1. In a second study, the mediating effects of trust in the leader were examined in a cross-sectional sample of 436 employees in a large Canadian telecommunications company. Group-level leadership (aggregated ratings of employees for each leader) and individual-level leadership (also labeled “differentiated leadership” in other work: Nielsen & Daniels, in press) were investigated. Findings indicated that trust in the leader fully mediated the relationship between transformational leadership and employee mental health at the individual level but not the group level. The third study replicated this mediating effect after controlling for positive affect, negative affect, neuroticism, and liking of the manager, bolstering confidence that individual characteristics of employees were not responsible for these results. Study 2 corroborates findings from Liu et al. (2010) using a different conceptualization of employee well-being (mental health versus perceived stress) as the outcome. The longitudinal nature of Study 1 allows a degree of determination with regards to the order of the relationship. However, as we will summarize next, another longitudinal study has not confirmed this causal sequence.

A longitudinal study based on a sample drawn from staff members working in ­elder-care roles in a Danish government service has tested several other mediators, publishing both cross-sectional (Nielsen et al., 2008a, 2009) and longitudinal (Nielsen & Munir, 2009; Nielsen et al., 2008) results. One of the cross-sectional studies (Time 1), using 447 Danish elder-care workers, tested involvement, influence, and meaning as mediators of the relationship between transformational leadership and affective well-being (Nielsen et al., 2008a). Structural equation-modeling analyses showed that meaningfulness was the only variable that partially mediated this relationship. In another cross-sectional report (Time 2) on 274 Danish elder-care workers, the mediating mechanisms of self- and team-efficacy were tested (Nielsen et al., 2009). The results showed that both team- and self-efficacy fully mediated the relationship between transformational leadership and employee affective well-being.

In longitudinal reports based on the 188 participants who completed the study at both points (18 months apart), experience of a meaningful work environment, role clarity, and opportunities for development all partially mediated the relationship between transformational leadership and employee affective well-being (Nielsen et al., 2008). No direct relationship was found between transformational leadership at Time 1 and employee affective well-being at Time 2, suggesting that the effects of transformational leadership on affective well-being are indirect and more complex than previous studies had ­suggested. There was a reciprocal relationship between perceptions of transformational leadership and affective well-being, such that affective well-being at Time 1 influenced Time 2 ratings of transformational leadership. This parallels the findings of another study, which utilized a different conceptualization of leadership (van Dierendonck et al., 2004).

Another report on the longitudinal data from these 188 Danish elder-care workers found that followers’ self-efficacy fully mediated the relationship between supervisors’ transformational leadership style and employee affective well-being at Time 2, but not longitudinally (Nielsen & Munir, 2009). This sample was also used to demonstrate a longitudinal link between transformational leadership and depression symptoms ­(Munir et al., 2010); the study spoke to the links between supervisory transformational leadership and employee mental health. Findings showed that transformational leadership was associated with reduced depression in followers both cross-sectionally and prospectively.

Finally, a recent study investigated the impact of group and differentiated transformational leadership on multiple measures of employee well-being (intention to leave, job satisfaction, burnout, sleep quality, and vitality) (Nielsen & Daniels, in press). Mediators ­investigated included group and differentiated working conditions of meaningful work, social support, cohesion, and role clarity. The findings showed that differentiated meaningful work, social support, cohesion, and role conflict all mediated relationships between differentiated transformational leadership and at least one of the well-being outcomes. Meaningful work and social support mediated most relationships. This study confirmed the results of Barling et al. (2008), in that the effect of differentiated transformational leadership (i.e. at the individual level) was stronger than the effect of group-level transformational leadership. The authors suggested that the reason for this finding is that employee well-being outcomes are individual-level variables, so individual perceptions of leadership are more relevant.





9.2.3 Critique and future directions


One of the strengths of the literature on the link between transformational leadership and employee psychological well-being lies in the diversity of samples that have been used in these studies. Although research samples have been drawn from several nations and occupations, the literature is somewhat fragmented through different disciplines and journals (e.g. nursing, hospitality, organizational studies, psychology, psychiatry). This makes it challenging for researchers to focus their efforts and have a complete understanding of the work on this topic.

This review of the research linking transformational leadership to well-being outcomes for employees illustrates four key issues that should be considered in any future work in this area: methods, moderation and mediation, measures, and other factors affecting stress outcomes as controls.

Much of the research on the effects of transformational leadership on employee psychological well-being has been survey-based, used cross-sectional research design, and been subject to monomethod, monosource bias. This is particularly true in studies focused on burnout as the outcome. Experimental and longitudinal work is rare, but it is increasingly necessary if we want to disentangle the nature of this relationship. Because relationships between stress and leadership ratings might well be reciprocal, experimental and longitudinal work is now necessary to overcome the limitations imposed by monomethod bias (Podsakoff et al., 2003). While experimental studies lack ecological validity, this method allows causation to be established and theoretical tenets to be tested. In conjunction with the real-world applicability of longitudinal field studies, our knowledge of the causal links between transformational leadership and employee well-being would be enhanced with more experimental work.

Utilization of “an experimental setting would allow for more definite causal conclusions, [but] a dependent variable such as burnout is hard to capture in a lab context. At best, one can capture stress reactions or more specific aspects of burnout (e.g. emotional states, lowered efficacy) in a lab study” (De Hoogh & Den Hartog, 2009, p. 1065). This is probably why in the stream of research focused on employee affect and mental health there are more experimental (Bono & Ilies, 2006; Cherulnik et al., 2001; De Cremer, 2006), experience-sampling (Bono et al., 2007), and longitudinal (Nielsen & Munir, 2009; Nielsen et al., 2008) studies, adding to the strength of causal conclusions that can be drawn. The empirical research that has been conducted in this regard seems less equivocal in its findings than studies investigating burnout as the outcome, pointing to the conclusion that transformational leadership is linked with better well-being (increased positive affect and better mental health) for employees.

We believe that there may be creative ways to study stress outcomes for employees, by focusing on physiological reactions in the laboratory setting. Investigating stress outcomes by measuring salivary cortisol ratings, blood pressure, or other physical manifestations of psychological stress in an experimental paradigm would be very interesting and would advance knowledge of causation in this relationship.

We also suggest that future research should investigate a wider range of moderators of the relationship between transformational leadership and employee psychological well-being. We need “research … that integrates transformational leadership theory with contingency theories to explore if there are particular conditions under which transformational leaders are more or less effective” (Lyons & Schneider, 2009, p. 747). While many studies have found negative relationships between various components of transformational leadership and burnout (Corrigan et al., 2002; De Hoogh & Den Hartog, 2009; Densten, 2005; Gill et al., 2006; Hetland et al., 2007; Kanste et al., 2007; Leithwood et al., 1996; Lyons & Schneider, 2009; Seltzer et al., 1989; Sosik & Godshalk, 2000), there have been some equivocal ­findings. Several studies have found no relationship or mixed evidence, depending on the component of transformational leadership and the component of burnout being investigated (Corrigan et al., 2002; Densten, 2005; Gill et al., 2010; Hetland et al., 2007; Seltzer et al., 1989; Stordeur et al., 2001). Studies that employed experimental methodologies seem to confirm a negative relationship: employees with transformational supervisors report less burnout (De Hoogh & Den Hartog, 2009). However, this relationship depends upon employee personality to some extent, and burnout is not something that can be easily modeled in an experiment. Hence, the best measure with which to approximate this within a lab might be threat appraisal (Lyons & Schneider, 2009).

Thus far, one study has investigated employee personality (De Hoogh & Den Hartog, 2009) and one study has looked at employment status (Kanste et al., 2007) as a moderator. Other contextual moderators might also be considered, such as type of organization, whether a crisis looms, and team dynamics (Lyons & Schneider, 2009). Individual factors such as employee motivation and career stage could also be theorized to influence this relationship. It would be especially useful for researchers to focus on how specific components of transformational leadership affect specific components of burnout. For example, intellectual stimulation appears to have mixed effects on employee stress levels, which may suggest that the relationship between this particular component and employee stress is more susceptible to the influence of employee characteristics than other component of transformational leadership. For example, an employee experiencing stressors from home as well as work might be more susceptible to negative outcomes of being asked to think of new ways of doing things. In addition, these relationships might be affected by short-term versus long-term measurement issues. Some researchers have suggested that anomalous findings, such as that transformational leadership is negatively associated with sleep quality (Nielsen & Daniels, in press), could be explained by the difficulties inherent in measuring short-term and long-term effects. In other words, some transformational behaviors might initially cause a decrease in certain employee well-being indicators, but in the long term the effect might actually be positive.

Testing of mediators is more often seen in studies of the relationship between transformational leadership and affect or mental health (Arnold et al., 2007; Barling et al., 2008; Nielsen et al., 2008, 2009; Tsai et al., 2009). Fewer studies test potential mediating mechanisms related to stress outcomes (Liu et al., 2010; Stordeur et al., 2001). The focus on mediating mechanisms that link transformational leadership to stress and burnout for employees is another area in which more research could be usefully conducted.

In terms of measurement, utilizing burnout as an outcome measure is potentially ­problematic, because one aspect of burnout could actually be framed as a positive ­well-being outcome (personal accomplishment), while the other two are negative (emotional exhaustion and depersonalization). Studies focusing on the overall measure may obfuscate important differences in the relationship between transformational leadership and each dimension of burnout, particularly when considered in addition to potential moderating effects. There may also be important differences between affect and ­mental-health outcomes. Affect may be more fleeting than mental health, hence less stable in terms of our findings when utilizing cross-sectional methods. Mental health may be subject to longer-term influences from multiple avenues (work, home, friends, finances, etc.) and be less fleeting than affect. Therefore, findings regarding the relationship between transformational leadership and these different conceptualizations of employee psychological well-being may not generalize.

Future research can expand the focus in terms of outcomes, while also using methodologies that improve our ability to establish causation. Studies that incorporate various aspects of well-being in one paper would be useful (e.g. Neilsen & Daniels, in press). Another possibility is to use stress appraisal as an outcome instead of burnout, which would allow researchers to use experimental methods. In regards to the general conceptualization of employee well-being, there is an opportunity to incorporate more positive measures of health (Hofmann & Tetrick, 2003; Snyder & Lopez, 2002). Positive affect and positive mood are two examples that have been incorporated in this literature stream. Other possibilities include the notions of hope, gratitude, optimal functioning, vigor, ­optimism, and psychological capital (Dutton & Glynn, 2008; Luthans, 2002). Detangling how transformational leadership impacts on negative health outcomes versus positive outcomes is another direction that would be useful.

Finally, there is a large body of work investigating other workplace stressors, and future studies on the link between leadership styles and employee stress should control for these where possible. One study that explicitly controlled for these found no relationship between transformational leadership and burnout (Stordeur et al., 2001), and another found that organizational factors were more strongly correlated to burnout than leadership (Leithwood et al., 1996). While findings from these studies must be interpreted with caution due to cross-sectional methodology, we suggest that future research will be able to draw stronger conclusions about the role of transformational leadership in the psychological well-being outcomes of employees if organizational stressors are also considered.





9.3 Effects of Transformational Leadership on Leader Stress and Psychological Well-being


The managerial role is demanding, and individuals filling this role can be “prone to high levels of occupational stress” (Sparks et al., 2001, p. 501). According to one study, 88% of leaders considered work to be their primary source of stress; only 28% of these individuals believed that their employers were providing adequate resources to enable them to cope effectively (Campbell et al., 2007). Workplace factors such as managing change, mergers, and new technologies have been examined in terms of leader well-being, yet the ­importance of enacting positive leadership styles has not garnered much research attention, despite the fact that a number of authors have argued that it would be an interesting question to examine, with important practical implications. De Hoogh & Den Hartog (2009) note that “the potentially exhausting task of leading others also suggests leader burnout as an interesting area of research” (p. 1065). There have been only two studies that we could find examining the link between enacting transformational leadership behaviors and leader psychological well-being. These two studies focused on stress outcomes for the leaders.

First, a study of 236 leaders of 54 mental-health teams looked at the association between leader self-reported transformational leadership and burnout (Corrigan et al., 2002). Transformational leadership was broken down into its four components (charisma, inspirational motivation, intellectual stimulation, and individualized consideration) and burnout into its three components (emotional exhaustion, depersonalization, and personal accomplishment). Correlations between these factors showed that there was a significant positive association between all four transformational dimensions and personal accomplishment; a significant negative relationship between charisma, inspirational motivation, and individual consideration and depersonalization; and a significant negative association between charisma and emotional exhaustion. Correlations between intellectual stimulation and depersonalization and between inspirational motivation, intellectual stimulation, and individual consideration and emotional exhaustion were not significant. One strength of this study was that the researchers investigated differential relationships. This pattern of correlations might suggest that different dimensions of transformational leadership have differential effects on each aspect of burnout for leaders. However, monomethod and monosource bias is an important limitation. For example, there is no way to assess whether the significant correlation between charisma and emotional exhaustion is due to a third unmeasured variable pertaining to emotions. Because perceptions of leader charisma have been shown to be associated with the expression of more positive emotion (Bono & Ilies, 2006), the possibility that an emotional component accounts for this relationship is plausible. The fact that the only cognitively-focused component (intellectual stimulation) did not associate significantly with depersonalization could also be a result of emotional processes. Most importantly, there is no way to disentangle whether transformational leadership enactment is an antecedent of burnout or vice versa. It is highly plausible that those leaders who feel more personal accomplishment naturally rate themselves more positively on any leadership instrument. It would have been interesting if the authors had reported follower ratings of leadership and correlated these with the leader ratings of burnout, especially considering that there was a low correlation between follower and leader ratings of transformational leadership.

A second study looked at the relationship between transformational leadership styles and burnout in a sample of hospitality managers in Cyprus (Zopiatis & Constanti, 2010). These researchers were interested in whether “the style of leadership that one adopts … leaves the leader more vulnerable to burnout” (p. 301). Using the same measurement instruments, a similar pattern of association as in Corrigan et al. (2002) was found in this sample of 131 participants. The overall measure of transformational leadership was significantly and negatively correlated with emotional exhaustion and depersonalization and significantly and positively correlated with personal accomplishment.

These two studies both examined leader stress, but another alternative would be to investigate affective outcomes. It is likely that leaders who behave with integrity, who show care and compassion to employees, and who are effective at leading their teams to achieve goals will generally feel good about their approach to their leadership role (Barling et al., 2008), so increased affective well-being might result when a leader is able to ­successfully enact transformational behaviors. Unfortunately, the cross-sectional research design of the two studies poses a significant limitation to the conclusions that can be drawn; ­managers who are experiencing lower levels of burnout have more resources to spend the time and energy to enact transformational behaviors.





9.3.1 Leader stress and transformational leadership: a reciprocal relationship?


Adaptive cost theory (Cohen, 1978) implies that stress influences the probability that a leader will engage in transformational leadership. According to this theory, cognitive resources are reduced when individuals try to adapt to stressful conditions. As a result, the stressed individual begins to prioritize; most people focus their attention on their own needs and become less sensitive to the needs of others. Therefore, stressed leaders will be less transformational, or may focus solely on aspects of transformational leadership that they consider to be less demanding. Research has also suggested that decision-makers who experience high levels of stress have a tendency to reduce the amount of information that they use when weighing options, and that these individuals also endeavor to control situations (Staw et al., 1981). These behaviors are also incompatible with the precepts of transformational leadership, which require that leaders consider employee input and needs (i.e. individualized consideration) and use novel information and approaches (i.e. intellectual stimulation). As such, stress and transformational leadership may be incompatible.

The question remains: how can organizations encourage leaders who are under stress to engage in transformational leadership? Given the positive outcomes of transformational leadership in terms of employee attitudes and performance outcomes for individuals, groups, and organizations (Bass & Riggio, 2006), doing everything possible to encourage supervisors to be transformational would appear to be a worthwhile goal. Can research identify for which managers enacting transformational leadership is stressful and why? Are there aspects of leader psychological (mental) well-being that make it more likely they will enact transformational leadership?

The job demands–resources theory (Demerouti et al., 2001) may help to explain why enacting transformational leadership behaviors might impact on leaders’ psychological well-being. According to this theory, individuals who face additional demands without possessing sufficient resources will experience heightened levels of stress. Although transformational leaders may benefit from the productivity and attitudinal improvements of their followers, transformational leadership may also be conceptualized as a demand insofar as it requires leaders’ emotional labor and time to enact it. Certain leaders may possess characteristics that make this style easier to enact than it is for others; for example, extroverted leaders may find communicating a vision and providing individual attention to employees easier than introverted leaders. Finally, the environment in which one is leading may also affect whether this leadership style is stressful to enact. While there may be other factors within the individual leader, their followers, and the context that might affect this relationship, here we briefly summarize a few potential variables for future exploration (for a summary, see Connelly & Arnold, 2011).

Emotional labor may help to explain the relationship between enacting transformational leadership and leader well-being. Emotional labor describes the processes required for ­individuals to manage their feelings and expressions (Brotheridge & Grandey, 2002). This behavior occurs when people display or suppress emotions that do not match their actual feelings (Hochschild, 1983). Thus far, there has been little research on the role of ­emotional labor in effective leadership. However, we expect that emotional labor will ­significantly affect a leader’s capacity to engage in transformational leadership behaviors, because they involve such close interpersonal interactions with employees (e.g. treating employees according to thier individual needs and communicating an optimistic strategic vision; ­Gardner et al., 2009; Humphrey et al., 2008).

Time demands are also a potential mediator of this relationship. For example, intellectual stimulation requires time in which to formulate ways of enabling employees to learn from their errors or missteps. Although a leader could intervene and complete tasks quickly by themselves or save some time by simply demonstrating the correct procedure to employees, intellectual stimulation requires that leaders provide a supportive context in which followers can learn independently. Individual consideration also requires a ­substantial time commitment. The leader must get to know each follower as a person in order to understand what developmental opportunities will be appropriate for them, which is ­significantly time-consuming. This is especially true when a leader has a large span of control.

In addition to these mediating mechanisms, we suspect there are several potential moderators of the relationship between enacting transformational leadership and leader psychological well-being. These moderators can be conceptualized as individual leader characteristics, follower characteristics, and context characteristics. In terms of leader characteristics, there are aspects of personality that could make enacting transformational behaviors inherently less stressful. Although many different personality traits exist, there are five that have received considerable research attention (e.g. Digman, 1990; McCrae, 1992). Three of these traits are plausible moderators: extroversion (tendency to be confident and outgoing); agreeableness (tendency to be friendly and caring); and openness to new experience (tendency to try new things and embrace change) (Bakker et al., 2006). Personality has been shown to be related to transformational leadership (Judge & Bono, 2000). For example, leaders with high levels of extroversion may find it easier to talk to employees about their vision (i.e. inspirational motivation). Leaders with high levels of agreeableness may be more likely to interact with employees (i.e. individualized consideration). Leaders with high levels of openness to new experiences may be more able to seek out new ways of approaching problems (i.e. intellectual stimulation). Overall, individuals who are highly extroverted, agreeable, and open to new experience should be less likely to experience stress as a result of the demands of enacting a transformational leadership style.

The reciprocal relationship between perceptions of transformational leadership in supervisors and employee well-being (i.e. the finding that employee affective well-being at Time 1 was associated with ratings of transformational leadership of supervisors at Time 2 in a longitudinal survey study conducted by Nielsen and colleagues) lends empirical support to the suggestion that employee characteristics are an important moderator of the relationship between enacting a transformational leadership style and leader psychological well-being. “Leaders may find it hard to lead the way and make employees to perform ‘beyond the call of duty’ if they [those employees] have no energy and engagement in work. Conversely, employees with high levels of energy and enthusiasm may encourage managers to take the lead, create a vision, and make an effort to develop his or her employees” (Nielsen et al., 2008, p. 27). Hence, we suspect aspects of follower personality will moderator this relationship.

Finally, with regards to context, leadership training is a potential resource (Kelloway & Barling, 2010) and moderator of this relationship. Considering that receiving training on how to enact transformational leadership behaviors increases leaders’ psychological well-being (McKee & Kelley, 2010), and considering that training increases self-efficacy (Bandura, 1978), it is likely that the relationship between enacting transformational ­leadership and leader stress is moderated by the amount of training a leader has received. Leaders who receive transformational leadership training will likely experience less stress when they enact this leadership style than leaders who do not receive training.





9.4 Conclusion


There is great opportunity for further research into the linkages between transformational leadership in leaders and the psychological well-being of followers and leaders. Studies that incorporate moderators and mediators of these relationships are necessary in order to fully understand when and how transformational leadership affects psychological well-being—both for followers and for leaders. Using experimental and longitudinal designs, as well as improved and varied measures tapping into positive measures of well-being, will advance the field. The link between transformational leadership and leader well-being is particularly ripe for exploration. Indeed, studies should investigate the reciprocal links between leader and employee well-being and focus on transformational leadership as both a cause and an effect.

This chapter has provided a critical overview of the existing research on the relationship between transformational leadership and the psychological well-being of followers and leaders. We have presented an agenda for future research that focuses on further investigation of moderators and mediators of these relationships and the effects of enacting this style on leader psychological well-being, and we have addressed methodological issues that relate to this important stream of research. Such further research is necessary for the benefit of leaders, their followers, and the organizations in which they work.
