1 Wilhelm (1960) speculates that the origins of the I Ching go back much further: Fu Hsi (c. 2800 BC) devised the first symbols, and the first of the classic hexagrams emerged in the Hsia dynasty (2205–1766 BC).

2 This is actually an amalgam of different thoughts attributed to fragments of Heraclitus’ writings on the subject of “universal flux” (Wheelwright, 1959, p. 29): (1) everything flows and nothing abides; everything gives way and nothing stays fixed; and (2) you cannot step twice into the same river, for other waters are continually flowing on.

3 The proposition of voluntary change introduces the thorny issue of free will and choice. From a ­psychological/philosophical standpoint, it can be argued that all change is dictated by either an existing condition (the nature or DNA of the organization) or a reaction to external events. In either case, it can be questioned whether the organization is really making a voluntary choice based upon free will. This is not the place for a more in-depth discussion of this issue, but ­cultural assumptions about free will and choice have significant and profound consequences for the way decisions are made within organizations and systems.

4 The quasistationary equilibrium state described by Lewin (1947) is analogous to the punctuated-­equilibrium model described by Tushman & Romanelli (1985).

5 This approach has particular strength within professional management circles, with the Strategic ­Management Society having over 2600 members in 2011.

6 While the influence of North American and European theory and practice is being challenged in some parts of the world, notably Asia, “Western” approaches are still highly influential and are imitated across the globe.

7 Lewin was a student of Carl Stumpf, an early philosopher/psychologist who had a strong influence on the views of leading psychologists of the Gestalt school, such as Max Wertheimer, Wolfgang Köhler, and Kurt Koffka (Boring, 1950).

8 Lewin (1943) further argued and provided evidence that, as a general rule, reducing restraining forces was more effective than increasing driving forces in changing behavior in situations where individuals experienced feelings of threat related to the desired change.

9 There is a long history of field research in disciplines such as cultural anthropology (Burgess, 1984) and sociology (Durkheim, 1895; Nisbet, 1967). In psychology, however, there are few examples before Lewin. The current enthusiasm for qualitative research (Patton, 2002) can be traced back to Lewin.

10 This is a very brief treatment of Bateson and Watzlawick’s discussion of the theory of groups in relation to change. Readers are encouraged to read Watzlawick et al. (1974), pp. 2–5, for a much more in-depth treatment of the subject.

11 This term, apparently, is a direct reference to the familiar nine-dot puzzle “box,” which requires new ways of thinking to solve. The puzzle requires you to connect all nine dots in the box with only four lines (Loyd, 1914).



Another example was observed by the author when his local 76-year-old contractor was ­informed by a neighbor that the roof elevation of his addition to our summer cottage exceeded the local ordinance by 6 inches. The contractor thought about the problem for a few moments and then came up with a very practical solution that was inexpensive and simple and certainly not conventional. Instead of lowering the roofline by reducing the frame of the roof, he simply ordered a load of dirt to be dumped around the foundation of the cottage where the elevation exceeded the standard.

12 Earlier, Freud (1961), on the basis of his clinical work, had posited that “The communal life of human beings had, therefore, a two-fold foundation: the compulsion to work, which was created by external necessity, and the power of love” (p. 48). Lawrence’s list of innate drives can also be compared with Maslow’s (1954) hierarchy of needs, which have gained almost universal currency in explaining ­human motivation. Lawrence assumes that activation of these four drives in coordination with an effective prefrontal-cortical response will ensure the best fit of the individual with the environment, resulting in successful competition of their genes with the genes of others in the propagation of the species. Both Freud’s and Maslow’s observations and theories can be accounted for using Lawrence’s four-drive ­theory. Some might argue that Maslow’s highest-level need, self-actualization, is superordinate, and therefore separate, from the practical need to learn in the pursuit of successful adaptation to the ­environment. The fact that Maslow found so few individuals who were truly self-actualized provides a counter—perhaps cynical—argument for the differentiation of Maslow’s need for self-actualization and Lawrence’s drive to learn.

13


Positive Psychology and Appreciative Inquiry: The Contribution of the Literature to an Understanding of the Nature and Process of Change in Organizations


Stefan P. Cantore and David L. Cooperrider





13.1 Setting the Scene


The development of theory and practice in organizational change is a fast-moving arena in which previous truisms about the nature of organizations and the way they change are being challenged. A well-known example of this is the steady replacement of the century-long dominance of the “organization-as-machine” metaphor, fostered by Fredrick Taylor, among others, in his seminal work The Principles of Scientific Management, with talk of organizations as living human systems (Lewis et al., 2008; Taylor, 1911). Arguably this shift reflects a movement from modernity, where rationality, structure, control, and ­division of labor dominate ideas about organizations, to postmodernity in a post-industrial society, where the capacity to create and manipulate knowledge define an organization’s success or otherwise (Harvey, 1989; Touraine, 1974).

In the latter part of the 19th and for the majority of the 20th, a “modern” or “rational” approach to organizational change can be said to have dominated Western managerial thinking and practice. Some sociologists attribute this approach, in part, to the transition from community self-organizing in pre-industial times to a more formalized, structured, and therefore divided society in the industrial age (Tönnies, 1887). Traditional change efforts, invariably “top-down” in an industrial context, focused on organizational structure, managerial and worker status, differential power between roles, careful delineation of individual responsibilities, and the implementation of new policies and procedures. The aim, to a large extent, was to try and solve apparent problems manifesting themselves in what appeared to be clearly defined and therefore apparently manageable entities called “organizations.”

In recent decades, postmodernity has had an influence. For our purposes, we simply regard postmodernity as an expression that denotes a break from the industrial age. In the case of organizational change, it is a break with the school of thought that can broadly be said to have adopted the metaphor of a machine to represent the organization (or ­separate entity), compared with the more recent metaphor of the organization as a living, socially-constructed human system in which we all participate (Lewis et al., 2008).

So, in contrast to a modernist perspective, with its focus on a rational, ordered, and classically “top-down” approach to organizational change, a postmodern take is likely to include the following assumptions:

There is no one right way of doing things in organizing human activity.

Differences between perspectives and ways of doing things are inevitable and welcome.

There is no definitive history of any organization. All history is composed of stories we tell each other to make sense of our experience of the world.

We construct our selves through a multiplicity of stories told by us and by others.

Knowledge—and organizational change—is created through conversations between us all, regardless of our formal organizational roles or statuses.

Organizational “problems” are a series of beliefs/ideas/feelings people have developed about their experience of being involved with a construct called an “organization.”



A postmodernist perspective is also likely to see the organization as a socially-constructed, ever-changing space that enables human meaning-making in a work context (Weick, 1979). Therefore, rather than a clearly defined “object,” an organization looks and feels like a continually changing set of sometimes ill-defined processes. It is thus no longer ­possible to attribute to an organization a typical modernist “Grand Narrative,” setting out its purpose and steady course through an organizational-change process. Instead, the “here-and-now” perceptions of its members as they co-create and experience change become its multiple and ever-changing story (Lyotard, 1984).

At its most provocative, this perspective no longer necessarily considers an organization as a distinct seperate entity, but rather sees it as people organizing together in ­continually changing patterns of relationships and realities. This may appear a fine distinction but, in our view, it has the effect of redefining the whole modernist notion of “organizational change,” with significant ramifications for the practice of organizational-change ­specialists.

We have also witnessed, in recent decades, a growing interest in systems theory among the majority of organizational-change theorists. The focus is predominantly on an “open systems” perspective, with its underpinning belief that an effective and responsive ­interaction with its external environment is vital to an organization’s continuing success (Amagoh, 2008).

Applying complexity theory to organizational development1 (OD) is yet another area that has recently been of interest to change specialists. This draws upon mathematical modeling, evolutionary biology, and systems theory to answer the question, “How does order emerge?” This theory offers some helpful insights in times of chaos or turbulence in complex systems, when it is not possible to percieve a linear pathway through a change process and it is difficult to determine cause and effect.

One construct emanating from this set of theories is the notion of the complex adaptive system, which, when applied to organizational-change theory, sees an organization becoming “what emerges from the relationships of its members rather than being determined by the choices of individuals” (Stacey et al., 2000 p.123). This leads to: (1) a set of beliefs about how change happens in organizations, with a focus on changing patterns of human relationships; (2) the expectation of both stability and instablity at different points in the process; and (3) the recognition of the importance of continous learning and the need for a willingness to change ways of thinking about how organizations work and develop.

So far we have described some recent developments in our understanding of the nature of organizational change. However, behind the scenes there are some game-changing practices and insights that are only just beginning to gain significant attention from ­practitioners and academics. One of these is appreciative inquiry (AI) and its associated dialogic processes. Bushe & Marshak (2009), in a landmark award-winning article, talk about “a bifurcation in the practice of [OD] that is not fully acknowledged or discussed in OD textbooks or journal articles. Forms of OD practice exist that do not adhere to key assumptions and prescriptions of the founders of OD” (p. 348).

These authors go on to compare and contrast diagnostic OD with dialogic OD. ­Diagnostic OD assumes the existence of an objective reality that can be researched to produce valid data supporting change. Dialogic OD, on the other hand, emphasizes a view of human systems as dialogic systems (Boje & Al Arkoubi, 2005) or meaning-making systems (Bushe, 2009), rather than as biological or open systems.

It may not yet be fully visible or acknowledged, but organizational-change theory and practice is itself subject to significant change. This is reflected in how some organizational-change consultants now understand themselves and their role. For example, one of the authors, when working in an OD capacity with an organization, usually refers to himself as a “Conversational Consultant,” offering to host conversations that matter to the organization or system. The practice of a dialogic-oriented OD consultant will focus more towards creating a “container within which people can safely create new meaning in real-time rather than spending much time, if any, on facilitating project groups undertaking data collection (Bushe & Marshak, 2009; Lewis et al., 2008).

Organizational-change theory has not been the only domain to witness rapid development. Something similar has been occuring in the field of psychology, with a growing body of research literature connecting neuroscience with behavioral and cognitive psychology, such as links between the functioning of certain areas of the brain and how people self-evaluate (Beer & Hughes, 2010). Cacioppo and colleagues (2000) have explored the neurophysiology of emotions, identifying connections between our bodies, brains, and emotional responses, while Rugg & Yonelinas (2003) provide insight into the way the brain works to help us recognize other people. This research is just the tip of a growing iceberg of knowledge that is shaping the practice of psychology across all of its specialisms, including positive psychology (PP).

It is difficult to understate the scale of the revolution that is underway. While we will concentrate on AI and PP, we ask the reader to bear in mind that they are both inextricably connected to a much broader range of developments in organizational change and ­psychology theory.

In this chapter we will explore the theoretical underpinnings of AI and PP, look at how they currently contribute to our understanding of positive organizational change, and consider how they can contribute to the future of what we are calling “innovation-inspired positive organizational development.”

It is worth noting, in passing, that the word “positive” has become very popular in the worlds of psychology and organizational change but means different things to different people. The language of positivity is still some way from a place of shared meaning. This is compounded by professional domains of meaning-making that still separate the individual from the social. A good illustration is the 850-page Handbook of Positive Psychology, which contains not a single reference to OD (Snyder & Lopez, 2005).

However, before we become too negative, it is worth observing that both AI and PP represent a significant splitting off from previous paradigms of thought and practice in the fields of organizational and individual change. This chapter aims to engage both in conversation, with the hope that the strengths of each will complement the other and deepen our shared understanding of positive change.





13.2 Appreciative Inquiry


In this section we consider the various definitions of AI and look at its philosophical and theoretical bases, before concluding with some reflections on the relationship between AI, psychology, and PP.





13.2.1 What is it?


A relatively straightforward definition of AI is that it is “about the search for the best in people, their organizations, and the strengths-enriched world around them” (Whitney et al., 2010, p. 1). However, a number of earlier texts elaborate on this to try and capture something of the philosophy and practices encapsulated by the term. In the original articulation of AI, Cooperrider & Srivastva (1987) argue that root metaphors matter, and they raise the distinction between seeing collective existence as a “problem-to-be-solved” versus a “mystery-and-miracle” of human interaction. They write:

In contrast to a type of research that is lived without a sense of mystery, the appreciative mode awakens the desire to create and discover new social possibilities that can enrich our existence and give it meaning … (AI) is an inquiry process that takes nothing for granted, searching to apprehend the basis of organizational life and working to articulate those possibilities giving witness to a better existence (p. 157).

The focus, in the earliest writings, was not on “positive” or “negative” but on the the question of what gives life to this living system when it is most alive—where appreciation is an experiencing of those things worth valuing. AI is presented in a discovery-oriented spirit of inquiry that draws us into “an experience which lies on the borderline between wonderment and admiration” and is based on a spiritual metaphor of life as a mystery and miracle (p. 91). On this point, again highlighting the idea that our starting assumptions matter, Cooperrrider draws on the words of Albert Einstein: “There are only two ways to live your life. One is as though nothing is a miracle. The other is as though everything is a miracle” (Cooperrider, 1996, p. 4). Appreciative knowledge is something that emerges as we seek to understand the life-giving present. In a more practice-oriented sense, Cooperrider et al. (2008) state that AI is “the co-operative co-evolutionary search for the best in people, their organizations, and the world around them … it involves the art and practice of asking questions that strengthen a system’s capacity to apprehend, anticipate and heighten positive potential” (p. 3).

Bushe (1998a) locates AI in the domain of research methodologies: “AI is a form of Action Research that attempts to help groups, organizations and communities create new, generative images for themselves based on an affirmative understanding of their past” (p. 41). Faure (2006) describes something of the challenge in defining AI when he writes, “In essence it is both a deceptively simple philosophy and a rigourous approach to managing change in human systems” (p. 22). Given this intertwining of a philosophical stance and practical application, it is perhaps not surprising that people struggle with explaining precisely what AI actually is, with some writers resorting to an almost religious definition that encourages people to consider how AI challenges their perspective on the world and their way of “being” in it (Watkins & Mohr, 2001). Others, like Stamps & Lipnack (2004), choose to define AI in terms of what it stands against: “AI arose in the late 1980’s in reaction to the problem orientated logical-positivist science that provided the intellectual foundation for the Industrial Era” (p. 30).

Each of the definitions we have cited gives a flavour of AI as a living research process engaging with human systems and based upon a set of philosophical understandings about the way the social world in which we live is being continually co-constructed between us. You will note that we have avoided emphasizing the term “organization” in our definition; that is because, while AI is often used in the context of organizations, it is also frequently adopted across large-scale human systems, embracing local, city, national, and international communities. This chapter will primarily focus on describing how AI operates as a change process in an organizational context.





13.2.2 Underpinning theories of AI


A core idea supporting AI is the belief that the appreciative process of knowing is socially constructed and “that through our assumptions and choice of method we largely create the world we later discover” (Cooperrider & Srivastva, 1987, p. 129). In other words, inquiry and change are not separate moments, but are simultaneous. Inquiry is intervention. The seeds of change—the things people think about and talk about, the things ­people discover and learn, and the things that inform dialogue and inspire images of the future—are implicit in the very first questions we ask. The questions set the stage for what we find, and what we discover (the data) becomes the linguistic material, the stories, out of which the future is concieved and constructed (Cooperrider & Whitney, 1999, pp. 50–51).

As people interact within a social system, so knowledge is created. Social constructionism, a theory or paradigm to which AI owes much, takes this one step further and argues that this is not separate empirical knowledge but the reality of people’s lived experience. Constructionism is an approach to human science that replaces the individual with ­relationships as the locus of knowledge. Therefore, this approach is built around a keen appreciation of the power of language and discourse of all types (from words to metaphors to narrative forms, and so on) to create our sense of reality—our sense of the true, the good, and the possible.

A key proponent of this perspective, Ken Gergen, has written extensively on this approach, both to explain it (Gergen, 1997a, 2001b) and to apply it to organizational ­science (Gergen & Thatchenkery, 1996) and social psychology (Gergen, 1997b). It is unsurprising that in academic circles social constructionism is hotly debated, given that for some it strikes at the heart of modernist thinking about truth and knowledge, arguing that reality is constructed between people. It has been suggested that it is a truly postmodern philosophic perspective, as Bushe comments: “the dominant theoretical rationale for AI is post-modernist European philosophy” (Bushe, 1998b, p. 2). Trying to get a grip on what social constructionism actually is can be difficult, given that those sympathetic to the idea want to avoid falling into the trap of making it a “thing” that is fixed rather than part of an ongoing dialogue between us about the nature of social reality. A helpful proposition is that both social constructionism and AI can be viewed as “variable social constructions … manifest in many different ways in different local-cultural and local-historical contexts” (Van der Haar & Hosking, 2004, p. 1017). This goes some way to explaining why AI practitioners outside a North American context—where the ideas originated—often need to use different terminology and constructs to engage their audiences.

Based upon the discussion so far, you may be forgiven for thinking that social constructionism is a values-neutral proposition, where people interacting together create worlds that are both good and bad. This misses one of the key elements vital to understanding AI: the notion of generativity. Bushe (2010) picks this up and references Gergen’s (1978) key paper on the topic—“Toward Generative Theory.” He notes that Gergen saw social construction as a means to build what he called “generative capacity,” which, by Gergen’s definition, is the “capacity to challenge the guiding assumptions of the culture, to raise fundamental questions regarding contemporary social life, to foster reconsideration of that which is taken for granted and therby furnish new alternatives for social actions” (p. 1346). This sounds a little like a call to revolution, and, as in all revolutions, there is the implication that what it causes in society will be better (more positive) than the status quo. AI picks up the notion of generative capacity and incorporates it into its methodology and language. While not explicitly positive in its definition, it is implicitly, and often explicitly, positive in its practice.2





13.2.3 AI and psychology


Almost 10 years before the term “positive psychology” was coined by Martin Seligman and Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi in 2000, Cooperrider wrote an integrative review of psychology and the human sciences on the role of the positive in human systems: the role of positive emotions, positive language, positive learning, positive images, positive culture as it relates to human systems development. Recognizing the epistemological differences between empiricist approaches to “a scholarship of the positive,” growing out of the neurosciences and pyschology, and social-constructionist approaches, Cooperrider placed the two fields in juxtaposition, arguing that both converge around one point: “we human beings create our own realities through symbolic and mental processes and … because of this, conscious evolution of the future is a human option.” The purpose of “a scholarship of the positive” was to help constructionist thought move beyond it trajectory of critique and deconstruction “to discover the affirmative task of social theory and to engage in forms of knowing that serve to make collective reality comprehensible from the perspective of human possibility” (Cooperrider, 1990, p. 92).

And how is this done? In constructionist terms, there is a recognition that the limits of language are the limits of our worlds. Vocabularies of the positive—in the world of critical theory—are almost nowhere to be found. This is why Cooperrider et al. (1995) embraced the empirical work of behavioral science, focusing on the positive—for example, the studies on positive emotion, the Pygmalion dynamic, and positive self-monitoring—as a way to accelerate learning, because it was producing new language of what gives life to human systems. This language of “the true, the good, the better and the possible,” they argued, mattered because “words enable worlds” (p. 163).

Cooperrider (1990) provided the first model showing why and how the paradigm ­dialogue between contructionist scholarship and empiricist research could mutually ­reinforce each of its constituents. This reflects the ongoing quest to create a “scholarship of the positive” that elevates topics such as wisdom, courage, integrity, flourishing, excellence, and the like. That article, embracing constructionist meta-theory and the empirical studies of the positive in human systems, significantly bridged the two approaches to create the opportunity for a dialogue across paradigmatic assumptions. Cooperrider and Seligman have given speeches all over the world together, and Cooperrider teaches ­regulary in the Masters Program in Positive Psychology at the University of Pennsylvania.

As Whitney et al. (2010) write:

Today the positive psychology movement is a gathering force in international psychology and is gaining the support of funding sources and eductional institutions. Why? It seems that psychologists like organizational development consultants, have recognized that human systems move in the direction of what they study and that to contribute constructively to human and societal well-being we need to develop a vocabulary of joy, hope and health (p. 85).

Most recently, Cooperidder and Godwin have observed that recent years have seen a huge advancement in what they term the “science and scholarship of the positive.” They cite the development of emotional intelligence (Goleman et al., 2004), advances in AI (Cooperrider et al., 2004), spirals of positive emotions in organizations (Fredrickson, 2003), and the science of happiness (Seligman, 2002) as good examples of a shift taking place. They also argue that the development of academic programs in respected universties that focus on positive OD is good evidence of a growing synergy between the field of OD, including AI, and the emerging field of PP (Cameron & Speitzer, 2011).

Behind this debate, according to Cooperrider and Godwin, a piece of the puzzle, which is truly needed by both AI and PP, is still missing (in Cameron & Speitzer G, 2011). This is a solid understanding of the term “positive change.” Both domains claim ownership of methods that offer hope and happiness—personal and/or organizational. Neither approach, however, is able, at this point in time, to securely support its theories with evidence. They are also not able to fully counter the voices of criticism, which argue that the word “positive” is overused, frequently misapplied, and generally misunderstood. For those in the AI and PP movements, however, these criticisms are a call to inquiry: What gives life to this living system when it is most alive?





13.3 Positive Psychology


In this section we will consider some definitions of PP, reflect on its underpinning ­theories, and locate it in relation to mainstream psychology.





13.3.1 What is it?


“Psychology” is defined in the Oxford English Dictionary as “the scientific study of the human mind and its functions especially those affecting behaviour in a given context” (Pearsall et al., 2003, p. 1420). PP definitions include: “the scientific study of optimal human factors that aims to discover and promote the factors that allow individuals and communities to thrive” (Seligman & Csikszentmihalyi, 2000, p. 5).

Seligman, one of the founding fathers of PP in the late 1990s and early 2000s, recently stated that psychology “is not just the study of disease, weakness and damage; it is also about work, education, insight, love, growth and play … Positive Psychology does not rely on self-deception or hand-waving; instead it tries to adapt what is best in the scientific method to the unique problems that human behaviour presents in all its complexity” (Snyder & Lopez, 2005, p. 5).

Boniwell (2006) states that the aspiration of PP is “to bring solid empirical research into areas such as well-being, flow, personal strengths, wisdom, creativity, psychological health and characteristics of positive groups and institutions” (p. 1).

To differentiate it from positive thinking, Lewis (2011) suggests that PP is subject to the “rigours of scientific experimentation and endorsement suggesting that the phenomena discovered are reliable and repeatable … Positive thinking deals more with the realm of anecdote and exhortation” (p. 3).

Given AI, with its upfront social-constructionist and postmodernistic foundations, and PP, with its positivistic, scientific outlook, you may well be wondering at this point why two so seemingly distant theortical cousins are in the same chapter of a book—perhaps even why they are in the same book. It’s a good question, and we invite you to hold it in your thoughts as you go with us on an inquiry into the similiarities and differences ­between AI and PP, particularly in relation to the notion of organizational change. A temptation is for us to open up a very broad, and still ongoing, debate about the relationship between psychology and social constructionism. We will touch on it, but our intention is to focus on the potential of both AI and PP to support organizational change.





13.3.2 Underpinning theories of PP


We have already alluded to the theoretical location of PP in a positivistic, scientific paradigm with an emphasis on empirical research methods, and to the assertion that through such observations, generalizable truth about effective interventions may be identified and applied to clients/patients by qualified practitioners. For our purposes, Gergen (2001a), in a helpful analysis, identifies some of the thinking that underpins what he calls “psychological science.”

First, he draws attention to the notion held by Descartes and others from the early days of the Enlightment that the mind is self-contained and able to observe and draw rational conclusions without reference to others. The implication is that the more you know about the mind, the more you know about human beings. Second, he notes the distinction ­between the external material world and the internal world of the mind. The mind is objectively able to observe the world and draw rational conclusions about cause and effect. Finally, and of least importance to modernist thinkers according to Gergen, he notes that language is the means by which the mind communicates truth to other people. Collections of words which summarize scientific observations should, by implication, accurately describe the world as it actually is. This is perhaps, as Gergen suggests, one area of mutual interest and overlap which offers the possibility of a fresh dialogue.

Gergen goes on to contrast this perspective on the mind and knowledge with a ­postmodern view concerning socially constructed realities. What is particularly interesting is his observation that psychological science “has long been dedicated to a posture of value neutrality” (Gergen, 2001a, p.808). The implication is that “facts” can be seperated from “values.” He suggests that the reality is that, from a postmodernistic perspective, this ­distiction becomes blurred. What research method you adopt has as much of an impact on society as the application of your findings. All elements have values attached to them. Gergen asserts that to avoid these issues “is not only myopic but irresponsible” (p. 809). So when you put the word “positive” in front of the word “psychology,” you are most definitely implying a whole set of values around what is considered “positive” and what is considered “negative.” Similarly, when you add the word “appreciative” to the word “inquiry” you are are also bringing a set of values to what might be considered a relatively neutral “scientific” term for research. Perhaps a revolution is underway?

Gergen wants to encourage a conversation between psychological science and social constructionism that brings psychology out of its historical empiricist position and enables new generative methodologies to flower.

While there still remains a seemingly wide philosophical gap between the two, both AI and PP share some common features which perhaps explain why they are of interest to those engaged with organizational change. They both:

Hold the belief that human beings can, and indeed deserve to, flourish in organizational or institutional contexts and derive much personal happiness from this (Seligman & Csikszentmihalyi, 2000).

Seek to search out strengths and understand how these can be used to grow new ways of living.

Encourage the development of language that supports the co-construction of a shared understanding of what it is to live and work positively.

Talk about the positive, even though there is perhaps no sense of agreement about what exactly this means in an organizational-change context.

In their own ways have offered challenges to the established way of thinking. AI pushes the field of OD to rethink its historically modernist approach, while PP asks psychology as a science to move out of its alleged value-neutral assumptions about people and become assertive about what makes for a good life.



Both are pioneers of thought and practice in our times and it is no surprise that they feel drawn to one another, even though it may be for comfort at times!

In conclusion, AI and PP claim positive change as their territory of expertise. PP ­however is cautious about entering into the world of organizational change, seeing its role as offering “empirical backing by evaluating both the objective and subjective impact of organizational change practices” (Boniwell, 2006, p. 115). AI similarly seems cautious about entering the domain of PP, viewing its development over the past decade as a perhaps overly optimistic sign that a shared movement towards the positive is taking place, and maybe wanting and willing to see in the field of psychology a redefining and rethinking of its priniciples and practices, even though evidence of a move away from a conventional empiricist approach within PP may be fairly minimal at the present time (Whitney et al., 2010).





13.3.3 PP and its psychological perspectives


In presenting it to the wider psychology community, PP supporters tend to adopt a number of positions. First, that PP in its recent manifestation in the last 2 decades is the consequence of a major eureka moment by Martin Seligman, a clinical psychologist, who after a question from his daughter about why he had been such a grouch for so long, realized that he needed to change and focus on nuturing the best qualities in himself and others (Seligman & Csikszentmihalyi, 2000). Second, that PP is providing a long-overdue counterbalance to mainstream clinical psychology, which has, they say, particularly since World War II, become too focused on curing mental illness and locked into a disease model (Boniwell, 2006).

Linley et al. (2006), in a review paper worth reading in its entirety, suggest that this counterbalancing is the metapsychological function and aspiration of PP. In the years since Seligman shared his personal revelation, academics have suggested that PP was always a part of true psychological understanding and practice. Boniwell argues that PP can be traced back to the thoughts of Aristotle, who “believed that there was a unique daimon or spirit within each of us. Acting in accordance with this daimon leads one to happiness.” She goes on to cite hedonism and utilitarianism as examples of man searching for happiness (Boniwell, 2006, p. 40).

More recent antecendants of PP can arguably be found in the domain of humanistic psychology, whose principle theorists include Clark Moustakas, Carl Rogers, and perhaps most famously, Abraham Maslow. Humanistic psychology broadly concerns itself with human life in its fullness, including meanings, values, freedom, personal responsibility, human potential, and self-actualization. It takes the view that people are essentially good.3

While the topics of interest to humanistic psychologists are similar to those of interest to positive psychologists, they differ in their preferred research paradigms and methods. Whereas those from the humanistic school insist that their research objectives require the use of qualitative research methods, including constructivist approaches, those from the positive school have a preference for quantative and more traditional research methods.

Linley et al. (2006) observe that one of the key successes of PP has been the introduction of a new taxonomy of human strengths into the language of psychology. They suggest the routes that PP may take in the future: either disappearing althogether because it has become fully integated into mainstream psychology or else still existing within the mainstream as a separate area of research but becoming increasingly marginalized. The continued development and influence of PP during the last 5 years suggests that PP is increasingly a part of psychology orthodoxy.





13.4 Understanding Change


We now consider how AI and PP understand the notion of change in an organizational context.





13.4.1 AI and change in an organizational context


Perhaps no other word sparks in us a greater reaction than the word “change.” It connects with us at a deeply personal level, igniting both fear and excitement about the unknown future. Given that most of us have mixed personal experiences of organizations, when the words “change” and “organization” are put together in one sentence we instinctively react with our own set of assumptions about how change happens. Our reactions originate from our understandings of how the world works and who we are in it. This set of beliefs, combined with our interpretation of past experiences and educational and familial influences, forms our individual understanding of “the way things are” and the way we think human systems change. As we interact with people and the wider economic, technological, ecological, and cultural environment, our sense of what change is or is not shifts and itself changes.

The literature on organizational change views it in a similar fashion. It has, since ­organizational management and change was first described in the early years of the 20th century, reflected the assumptions, environmental changes, and human experiences of the times.

The principles of scientific management proposed by Frederick Taylor reflect the views of the early 20th century (Taylor, 1911). He represents the modernistic mindset, ­espousing ideals from the Enlightenment period, including the perfectability of man (and organizations) and the expectation of continual progress, which can be achieved by the self-reliant use of reason or rationality. By adopting a “scientific” rationale for his theories, he argued that the best way to improve production was to measure and track behavior over time, in order to develop the most efficient or “single best way” to do a job. This implied that ­perfect organizations could be designed by rectifying problems in existing ones (­Mautner,1999).

We argue that Taylor’s legacy is embedded in organizational-change theory and manifests itself in a number of key beliefs that are implicit, and often explicit, in modernist change literature:

The power of problem-solving to change organizations.

The power of naming problems to produce change.

The power of instruction to achieve change.

The belief that emotions are problematic.

The power of criticism and fear to motivate change.

The belief in the head and body organizational split.

The belief in the power of separating elements to enhance clarity and so the ability to act efficiently.

The belief in a “right answer” to the problem of design (Lewis et al., 2008a).



Pick any of your preferred theories of organizational change and check them against these beliefs and we think you will be surprised at what you find. For example, Kurt Lewin’s (1943) well-known and linear unfreeze–change–freeze model continues to be a popular recipe for OD. Indeed, it has been said that the whole of classical organizational-change theory is reducible to this one idea of Lewin’s (Weick & Quinn, 1999b). John Kotter’s well-known eight steps on how to successfully tranform your organization continues to be another very popular organizational-change model, elaborating on Lewin’s approach (Kotter, 1996). There are many others.

Since the 1980s, there has been a growing interest in what may be described as ­postmodernist approaches to organizational change. AI is perhaps becoming one of the most well-known and widely-adopted practical expressions of this approach to supporting organizational change.

What are the principles that underpin AI’s approach to organizational change? ­Cooperrider & Srivastvra (1987) envisaged AI as a “conceptual reconfiguration of action research” (p. 129), arguing that for action research to reach its potential as a vehicle for social innovation it needed to move away from its problem focus, which the authors percieved as acting as “a primary constraint on its imagination and contribution to knowledge” (p. 129). They also argued that, in an organizational-change context, it is the question or inquiry that engages with the mystery of life and opens up thinking. The defining of a problem restricts thinking to dealing with what can be said to be a socially-constructed perspective of an apparent event in the past, rather than energizing people to look for future possibilities. As an aside, we can see here an early link to the role ­Seligman & ­Csikszentmihalyi (2000) percieved PP to fulfill with regard to the discipline of psychology, bringing it from a problem or illness focus to a life-giving focus.

The term “action research” was first used by Kurt Lewin in the 1940s to describe a method in which theory and pratice are combined in an interventionist type of research to produce results that are both rigourous and relevant (Hajnal, 2005)—in other words, research undertaken in a social context that leads to specific social action. Action research, with its original emphasis on inquiry leading to action, can therefore be considered an early predecessor of AI. What AI offers is a combination of social-constructionist perspectives on the nature of organizational change with an action-research type of framework grounded in the desire to grow strengths rather than resolve problems. So, while modernist approaches to organizational change might aim implicitly or explicitly for managed deliberate change in pattern, order, hierachy, and control, a postmodern approach like AI will expect instability, chaos, uncertainty, and full involvement by all parties in change. We have deliberately exagerated the dicotomy between the two positions to emphasize the substantial nature of the diference ­between them.

AI is constructed on five principles:

(1) The constructionist principle—Reality as we know it is a subjective state, rather than an objective one.

(2) The principle of simultaniety—Inquiry is intervention and creates change. The moment we begin to ask a question, we begin to create change.

(3) The poetic principle—Human organizations are an open book and their stories are constantly being coauthored. Pasts, presents, and futures are endless sources of learning, inspiration, and interpretation. We have a choice about what we study and what we study changes organizations.

(4) The anticipatory principle—Human systems move in the direction of their images of the future. The more positive and hopeful the image of the future, the more positive the present-day action.

(5) The positive principle—Momentum for large-scale change requires large amounts of positive affect and social bonding. This momentum is best generated through positive questions that amplify the positive core (adapted from Cooperrider et al., 2008 and Whitney et al., 2010).



How these principles play out in the practice of AI will be explored in Section 13.5.1.





13.4.2 PP and change in an organizational context


We have previously referred to the ambiguity and confusion caused by the overuse of the words “positive” and “change.” A way of reframing this is to understand that we are engaged in a conversation that is opening up new questions about the process of change and the values and beliefs we bring to change in our lives, both as individuals and as groups of individuals in organizations. As in any vibrant conversation, new themes emerge, connections are made, assumptions go unspoken, and misunderstandings happen. Space does not permit a full elaboration of the metaconversation, so we will focus on the essentials of PP as it was originally elaborated by Seligman and Csikszentmihalyi in 2000 and identify any specific connections with organizational change.

Seligman & Csikszentmihalyi (2000) suggest that PP operates at three levels. The first is the subjective level, with its study of positive experiences such as joy, well-being, and happiness. The second is the level of the individual, with the aim of identifying the ­constituents of the positive, fufilling life and the positive personal traits associated with being able to live positively. The third focuses on the level of institutions—what makes for positive institutions (note that organizations per se are not mentioned) and what moves individuals towards altruism, civility, better citzenship, and work ethic (Seligman & ­Csikszentmihalyi, 2000).

One criticism of PP is that very little research has been undertaken into the nature and development of positive institutions. Traditional science-based psychological researchers have instead largely focused on identifying methods for measuring personal strengths and responding to the call to adapt what is best in the scientific method to the unique problems that human nature presents to those who wish to understand it in all its complexity (Seligman & Csikszentmihalyi, 2000).

In the past decade, a whole literature has developed around positive organizational behavior, which Luthans (2003) defines as “the study and application of positively orientated human resource strengths and psychological capacities that can be measured, developed and effectively managed for performance improvement in todays workplace” (p. 178). The focus has been on research that considers how positive personality traits relate to work performance, core self-evaluations (self-esteem, generalizized self-efficacy, locus of control, and emotional stability), and positive psychological traits (Luthans & Youssef, 2007).

What all of this suggests is that PP, particularly the field of positive organizational behavior, has contributed much to emerging thinking about individual development and performance within an organizational context. It constitutes the “what” that needs to be done to help individuals perform positively. However, it has not added much knowledge per se to our understanding of the “how” of organizational change.





13.4.3 Commonalities and differences between how AI and PP understand change in an organizational context


13.4.3.1 Commonalities


Both AI and PP make humanistic assumptions about the essential goodness of people and the innate desire of groups of people to create better life experiences for themselves as individuals and as groups. For Fineman (2006), this means that “an optimistic route to optimistic outcomes” pervades both domains and acts as a backcloth to all the literature and theories (p. 271). As a result, both AI and PP proponents have been described as evangelical in the propogation of the positive “gospel.” Both AI and PP believe change can happen in organizations that are positively orientated. AI, with its notion of the “positive core,” emphasises the need to tap into the core strengths of an organization in order to create a new future (Cooperrider et al., 2008). This is complemented by PP’s focus on measuring personal strengths that support effective perfomance in an organizational context. It is also echoed in the PP notion of positive institutions (Seligman & Csikszentmihalyi, 2000). PP brings an understanding of the nature of well-being and ­happiness in work contexts that echoes some of the assumptions AI makes about the engagement of people in change processes. Arguably, both support organizational change through their use of affirmative language and the belief that the language of the positive is in itself a support for growth and development. It is worth mentioning that AI and PP are rooted in a North American culture and worldview. It is in that context that both have developed and become popular.





13.4.3.2 Differences


Perhaps the most fundamental difference is the sense that PP strongly supports the need to provide scientific evidence to support its methods and practices, while AI offers an opportunity for all people involved in an organization to create a new future based on their own inquiry, which does not necessarily involve the scientific method nor hypothesis testing. A PP perspective talks about interventions (often it is not made explicit who makes these interventions or how), while AI talks of a co-created process that supports co-construction of a new organization alongside a new way of positively valuing one another.

Essentially, they are answering two different, but allied, questions, from two different change paradigms. AI answers the question of what people can do to create new ways of working by suggesting the use of a co-designed relational-inquiry process that will enable them to make the future happen as they inquire together. PP answers the question of what makes for the greatest satisfaction, happiness, and effectiveness in the workplace by offering the use of formal scientific methods to test theories on sample groups. The aim is to create knowledge that ultimately can be applied in all organizational contexts to enhance well-­being and performance.





13.5 The Change Process


We now consider how, in practice, AI and PP aim to deliver change in an organization context.





13.5.1 How does the AI literature describe the change process?


Proponents of AI tend to begin any description of an AI change process with some consideration of the principles and practices of AI. They are aware that they are engaged in a dialogue with change managers whose training has typically focused on change as a linear process of key steps directed from outside the system under consideration. Such managers have tended to adopt the machine metaphor, believing that change can be planned and directed in a rational scientific manner. The literature positions AI as a ­contrast to this and defines it as:

A relational process of inquiry, grounded in affirmation and appreciation (Whitney et al., 2010).

A form of transformational inquiry that selectively seeks to locate, highlight, and illuminate the “life-giving” forces of an organization’s existence (Cooperrider et al., 2008).

A new way of engaging with one another—it meets a deep-seated need in each of us to be respected, to be listened to, and to have the opportunity to shape the future (Lewis et al., 2008).



AI proposes that the combination of appreciation (an attitude of mind and heart) and inquiry (a set of practices and processes) produces “a vital and powerful catalytic effect on leadership and organizational change. By tapping into accounts of organizations that are functioning at their best AI unleashes information and commitment that together create energy for positive change” (Whitney et al., 2010, p. 4).

It is not surprising therefore that efforts to use AI as a change process try to balance explanations and encouragements with efforts to develop in change managers an ­appreciation of the value of an inquiry process within the organizational or community context. The theory states that if you get both, positive change will happen.

In order for change managers to develop an appreciative mindset, Cooperrider et al. (2008) suggest they need to understand the five principles outlined in Section 13.4.1 and internalize them as the basis for the inquiry process. At one level, this sounds straightforward, but for those who bring a scientific understanding of organizational change to the process it requires a revolution in thought and a major change in the lens through which they view the world. So, for example, if you believe there is definite truth about what is a good outcome for a specific organizational change, you will find yourself challenged by the AI principles, which ask you to acknowledge not only that such “truth” is not externally verifiable but also that the truth can only be constructed by discussions and dialogue between the change agent and the organization. It is the multiple relationships with others in which conversations and inquiries take place that not only lead to change but are the change in themselves.

Perhaps the greatest hurdle to overcome for many is the difficulty of leaving behind a mindset that begins a change process by defining “problems” and shifting to one that begins by exploring strengths and successes.4

If the AI mindset is present in an organization, or available to it through an external consultancy, what are the practices and processes that make AI successful? Some suggest that using AI is “like sitting down to dinner in a fine restaurant. The choices seem unlimited and there are so many possible combinations that no two dinners—even in the same restaurant—are ever the same” (Whitney et al., 2010, p. 23). This metaphor illustrates the desire of theorists and practitioners to encourage flexibility and the adaptation of an AI process to the particular situation of an organization or community.

However, that is not to say that a clear set of steps is not offered. AI offers what has been termed the “4-D Cycle.” The aim of this cycle is to help the organization understand its positive core, which is the life-giving forces of the organization’s existence (Cooperrider et al., 2008). The authors explain that behind an AI process is the desire to understand when an organization is most healthy and alive, and based on this knowledge to understand what possibilities this creates for the future.

The cycle is usually described in four steps, although, in reality, there are five. The first is a preparatory step to agree upon the affirmative topic that serves as the initial focus of the inquiry (Cooperrider et al., 2008). The subsequent four are:

(1) Discovery—This phase focuses on helping people tell their stories about when they experienced the topic of the inquiry at its best. This may range from an aspect of customer service through to the whole organization. The approach used is often one of appreciative interviews, where people within the organization interview one another to elicit stories of great perfomance.

(2) Dream—The “data” collected in the first stage are then used to help people, in conversation with one another, to build on what they have discovered about the organization at its best and project this into their wishes, hopes, and aspirations for the organization’s future. Such dreams are not abstract, but rather grounded in people’s past experiences.

(3) Design—This stage is concerned with shared decision-making about how to make the dream a reality in the context. The design phase defines the basic structure that will allow the dream to be realised. Like the other phases, the design phase requires widespread dialogue about the nature of the structure and the processes. This is what is meant by co-constructing the organization’s future.

(4) Destiny—Finally, groups or individuals are encouraged to take forward the actions identified during the previous phases and make them a tangible reality alongside the shifts of mindset and attitude that have taken place in the conversations ­during the process. A closing celebration is held (Cooperrider et al., 2008; Lewis et al., 2008).



For further information about the “how-to” of AI, please read any of the books referenced in this chapter. Each contains case studies that bring the process to life and illustrate the diverse ways in which AI has been crafted to meet particular circumstances.

In its early days, the “how” of AI in an organizational context required the hiring of organizational-change consultants. This is changing as more people have become aware of the principles and practices and are using them in a wide variety of contexts, including coaching, team development, and organizational and whole-system transformation. There is no certification process that attests to the qualifications of AI practitioners. In contrast, a positive psychologist will be able to reference their professional credentials—usually licensure or chartering—as evidence of their expertise.





13.5.2 How does the PP literature describe the change process?


PP does not directly specify a process for organizational change. Lewis (2011) has, ­however, indicated how AI can be can be added to PP to create inspiring organizations. This approach is interesting because of the energy and mental discipline that is necessary to use both approaches at once, since they are firmly based in contrasting and at times contradictory philosophical paradigms.

In practice, someone who considers themselves a positive psychologist working with organizations may offer some or all of the ideas and interventions listed in Table 13.1 to those leading change, based on the assumption that in combination, positive change will happen. This is not a comprehensive list and more opportunities to adapt PP approaches in organizations are continually being developed. For example, Lewis (2011) identifies the following domains to which PP can be applied:

Positive engagement and performance.

Positive communication and decision-making.

Positive leadership and change.

Positive relationships at work.

Positive sustainable growth.

Positive transformation.



In the later two categories, Lewis weaves in the value and complementarity of AI alongside PP in the development and transformation of the organization.

There is relatively little in the PP literature concerning the “how” of organizational change. The assumption is that PP will provide a variety of approaches to achieve a positive result: one-to-one coaching, team facilitation, occupational health advice, external ­diagnostic assessments of organizational well-being, expert organizational consultancy activities, input to organizational policies and strategies, training activities, and leadership development. While this is not an exhaustive list, it gives the reader some idea of what activities can be involved.

Table 13.1 PP-based interventions to promote positive change in an organization.

Adapted from Boniwell, I. (2006). Positive psychology in a nutshell: A balanced introduction to the science of optimal functioning London: Personal Well-Being Centre.

Topic Activity

Job variety Redesigning jobs to make work more interesting

Intrinisic motivation Helping employees find satisfaction in the value of the work they do

Confidence Supporting employees in practices that build personal self-confidence

Creativity Helping create organizational environments that support people using creative instincts and abilities

Strengths work Encouraging organizations to identify and support the strengths of the people working for them

Team-building Facilitating the effective functioning of teams

Meta-perspective Enabling people to see the benefits and also downsides of strengths; this leads to a more balanced approach to the development of the whole person

Flow Finding ways of helping people experience work as entirely fulfilling and all-absorbing

Participatory working practices Supporting workers to have more control over what they do and when they do it

Open climate, empowerment, and self-organization Giving employees control and even ownership of the company; one step on from participation





13.5.3 Commonalities and differences between how AI and PP approach change in an organizational context


13.5.3.1 Commonalities


We have already discussed the sense of optimism and the possibility of positive change that both AI and PP espouse. You will also have a sense that both view language as important, although AI tends to focus on the co-creation of shared meaning, while PP takes a more traditional problem-solving approach, which includes a taxonomy that is to be understood by everyone involved. Both approaches to change tend to assume a degree of expertise and understanding, although AI is perhaps more open to being adapted and adopted by those from any profession, while PP is most often practiced by professionals trained in psychology. Both AI and PP provide a linear-stage model for organizational change. AI provides the 4-D Cycle and PP a linear diagnosis-and-“treament” model.





13.5.3.2 Differences


PP, with its “fine-grain” diagnostic approach to behaviors and attitudes, contrasts with AI’s interest in supporting large-scale processes of change. AI does not explicitly consider behaviors and PP does not explicitly consider organizational-change processes. AI assumes that everyone together will make new meaning and create the future, while PP takes the “expert” position, offering advice on what actions others need to take to improve or change. AI seeks unity of action and purpose in the change process, while PP makes no attempt to offer a unifying theory but is content with specific change interventions to address particular topics. Someone using the AI perspective might describe organizational change as having everyone coming together to improve their situation, while someone using the PP approach might prefer to concentrate on measuring specific outcomes in specific aspects of organizational well-being.





13.6 What is Emerging for PP and AI in the Field of Organizational Change?


Just as we began by considering the range of past and present influences on the field of organizational-change theory and practice, so we conclude with a few pointers to what we believe the future will hold.

Both PP and AI find themselves together under the umbrella of a number of developing schools of thought. For example, positive organizational scholarship (POS), based at the Center for Positive Organizational Scholarship at the University of Michigan, focuses on “dynamics in human organizations that lead to developing human strength, producing restoration and resilience, fostering vitality and cultivating extraordinary individuals” (http://www.centerforpos.org). The center aims for a breadth and inclusivity in ­supporting research on the importance of a positive perspective. It make frequent reference to both AI and PP in its literature. Earlier we referred to Luthans’ work in defining a new field of positive organizational behavior that incorporates PP as part of its theoretical basis (Luthans & Youssef, 2007).

There is also a growing interest in the field of conversational approaches to change, which is manifesting itself in the adoption by positive organizational change practitioners of world-café, open-space, future-search, and circle-conversational methods (Lewis et al., 2008). We can expect to see further scholarship in the years ahead exploring the contribution that conversation, as a change process, can make to positive-change efforts.

These developments have not been without their critics, and in the future we can ­continue to expect criticism of the lack of support in the research literature for the benefits of taking a “positive-change” approach. Fineman (2006) highlights what he sees as the sidelining of negative feelings—“sources of disruption or destruction” in the “positive project” (p. 274). He argues that so-called negative emotions can hold the key to improved organizational performance, rather than the opposite. Fineman also challenges positive practitioners about their negative attitudes towards those who express negative feelings in the context of a change process. He descibes this as an “unarticulated dark-side to ­positiveness” (p. 281).

A perception that the positive-change school of thought reflects North American cultural norms rather than a more universal approach has also been commented upon. Recently Boniwell, a British academic proponent of PP, has critiqued some of PP’s weaknesses, citing the lack of a guiding cohesive theory, a reductionist scientific methodology, a proneness to drawing big conclusions out of weak findings, one-sidedness, and lack of balance (Boniwell, 2006).

In a review of 20 AI case studies, Bushe & Kassam (2005) found that 35% reported that the use of AI resulted in transformational change, including new knowledge and a new generative metaphor that changed the accepted thinking of system members. The study concluded that further research is warranted into the nature of transformational change and the role, or otherwise, of AI in specific contexts.

Stepping into the creative space that is being opened up through the dialogue around the topic of positive organizational change, Cooperrider & Godwin (2011) propose a ­re-envisioning of the field of OD by merging the streams of positive human science (including PP), AI, learning from nature (biomimicry), and design approaches. They call this new domain of theory and practice “innovation-inspired positive organizational development” (IPOD) and suggest that it involves three steps:

(1) elevating strengths;

(2) magnifiying strengths;

(3) refraction of strengths.



What these trends in theorizing and emerging practice suggest is that while differences in philosophy and practice differentiate AI and PP, these alone will not be sufficient to keep them separate, since each is a part of a wider movement of positive organizational change that continues to build energy as time passes. We live in exciting times.





Notes


1 We use the term “organizational development” to mean the body of knowledge and associated ­practices that support organisational change.

2 As a post-script it is worth noting the difference between social constructionism, which focuses on the creation of knowledge between people through their interactions, and social ­constructivism, which focuses on the learning an individual receives through their interactions. The two are, of course, ­intimately related (Kim, 2001).

3 For more information about humanistic psychology, the Association of Humanistic Psychologists Web site contains helpful material: http://www.ahpweb.org/.

4 AI does not deny people’s stories about organizational “problems” but encourages them to ­inquire into what these stories are telling them about the “other side of the coin,” when the ­experience of organizing is at its best. At the heart of people’s problem focus is a deep, heartfelt knowledge that life could be so much better. AI encourages people to access this yearning for what is not yet known and to work ­towards it collaboratively. AI does not prejudge what this future may be or assert what is, or is not, “positive” about it. That is the work to be done by those involved.
