15


Developmental Approaches for Enhancing Organizational Creativity and Innovation


Jane Henry





15.1 Introduction


In the last 30 years there has been increasing interest in ways of changing organizations in a manner that enhances creativity and innovation. One of the principle reasons for this is the increased pressure brought about by global competition. In high-wage economies, maintaining a high level of creativity and innovation is an obvious way of adding value. In developing economies, creativity and innovation offer ways of moving up the value chain. The organizational-change approaches used to achieve this end are many and ­various, ranging from structural and cultural changes through idea and people development to the management of innovation, process improvement, and organizational renewal.

In organizations it is common for creativity to be associated with the front end of the creative-process research and development (R&D) and ideas. Innovation tends to be associated with the development phase, where creative ideas are developed into working models, and when used in conjunction with creativity and innovation, entrepreneurship is often associated with the business of bringing new products and services to market. However, use of these terms varies and “creativity” is also associated with management approaches that aim to draw out the creativity in all employees. “Innovation” is sometimes used in a broader sense to cover the R&D, development, and marketing of new products. “Entrepreneurship” is also particularly associated with setting up small businesses and the wider process of bringing creative ideas and innovative products into being.





15.2 Perspective


The assumptions people make about the causes of creativity and innovation influence the choice of change and development approach adopted (Henry, 1994). For example, ­traditionally, creativity was seen as the provenance of a single individual, and it was assumed that while some people had creative ability, others did not. This led to research on the nature of the characteristics of creative people (e.g. Guildford, 1959) and a stress on recruiting the best staff. Evidence suggests that the caliber of individual staff members is especially important at the front end of the creative process, for people like research scientists who come up with the initial ideas. However, creative endeavor in organizations ­generally involves teamwork, so, more recently, stress has been placed on the means of drawing out the creativity in staff members across the organization, rather than focusing solely on the privileged few.

A number of studies show that creative people are able to tolerate uncertainty for longer than their less-creative counterparts and spend more time exploring questions than are the less-creative, who are more likely to jump to premature conclusions (e.g. Perkins, 1981). To achieve success within organizations, intrapreneurs generally also need persistence. People are more likely to persist in areas they care about; hence, intrinsic motivation is closely associated with creative output (Amabile, 1983). Organizations that grant members of staff the opportunity to follow up some of their own work interests generally have that trust amply rewarded.

The popular conception of creativity (De Bono, 1984; Koestler, 1969) is typically associated with mental flexibility and divergent thinking—“thinking out of the box,” as it is often referred to in organizations. The logic here is that more-creative, open-minded people are able to see connections that lead to new ideas which more task-oriented people would miss. Many large European organizations have some process to facilitate thinking out of the box. This ranges from away days through focus groups to programs that train staff members to think creatively. Unilever, for example, has routinely put large numbers of staff members through creative problem-solving training. Other companies have a companywide problem-solving process that employees are expected to use.

Creative-thinking programs often encourage divergent thinking. These frequently work on the principle that quantity breeds quality, and they aim to gather as many ideas as possible through procedures such as brainstorming, morphological analysis, and the use of analogy (Van Gundy, 1988). In my experience, these procedures are best placed within a creative problem-solving process, where time is spent considering which question to devote onself to, what angle on the question is likely to be most productive, which ideas seem most promising, and the acceptability, feasibility, and ownership of plans for the implementation of the preferred way(s) forward. This type of approach generates ideas, enables people to check whether they have anticipated possible ways forward, gives people a common language for discussing problems, and often helps breed tolerance of different working styles within the group. The ethos of creative thinking and brainstorming tends to encourage the acceptance of all ideas and the transmutation of crazy ideas into useful options. This allows participants to see that those with a different style and set of values to themselves offer very different ideas and that the favored solution often benefits from this mix of perspectives.

Studies of creativity in various domains, including science and business, suggest that mental flexibility is not enough. Rather, experience appears to be a critical factor for ­outstanding creativity (Simon, 1988). It is known that experts think differently from novices in that they chunk information differently and hold it in more sophisticated maps (Kaufmann, 1988). It seems that this facility enables them to spot unusual patterns more readily and leaves them better able to determine which are critical. Hence, on this reading, after Fleming had examined numerous growths on Petri dishes, he was better placed to realize the possible importance of the unusual one that led to the discovery of penicillin (Henry, 2001).

The importance of sector-relevant expertise is more recognized in some countries than in others. German culture tends to place more value on expertise than do its counterparts in the USA and UK, and German managers are much more likely to have sector-relevant ­backgrounds, for example in engineering, than US or UK managers (Jones, 1988). ­Recognition of the importance of experience in developing tacit knowledge has been better appreciated in the East than the West (Nonaka & Takeuchi, 1995). In Japan, for example, companies regularly exchange members of staff. This offers the first-hand experience and practice in context that is central to the development of tacit knowledge. In the late 20th century, knowledge management became fashionable, partly in an attempt to capture and share knowledge across organizations, so staff members could learn good practice and ideas from others.

The perspectives on creativity discussed so far intervene at the level of the individual—seeking to develop the creativity within a person. But within organizations, bringing a creative idea to fruition is very much a process of co-creation. It is important that people feel comfortable and trust their colleagues enough to be willing to share ideas and try new things (Handy, 1993). We know people are more likely to explore new possibilities when they feel safe, so the culture in which creativity takes place is important. In open and creative organizational cultures, people feel freer to try new things and to challenge the status quo than they do in more closed and bureaucratic cultures (Ekvall, 1991). West (2002) elaborates on ways of enhancing performance in creative and innovative work teams, while Henry (2001, 2006) and Rickards (1999) elaborate on ways of enhancing organizational creativity.

Csikszentmihalyi (1996) has elucidated the importance of the field within which the creativity takes place. No matter how brilliant any given individual is, in order for their creativity to be recognized the organizational culture and the sector that the innovation addresses need to be supportive of the endeavor if the innovation is to have a chance of being developed and accepted (Csikszentmihalyi, 1996). This means that for the innovation to be taken up, attention must be given to the politics of acceptance within and outside the organization.





15.3 Culture


15.3.1 Open cultures


Creativity is associated with more-open cultures that exhibit a higher level of trust. Open cultures afford employees elements of freedom and control as to how they go about their tasks. A number of writers have found this to be important for creative endeavor; Amabile & Gryskiewicz (1988) found it important for industrial research scientists, for example.

At the start of a new creative approach, people are often very enthusiastic. A number of successful new industries seem to have benefitted from a core of enthusiasts who were able to meet informally. This appears to hold true for the flourishing of art in Florence in the 15th century, of information and communications technology (ICT) in Silicon Valley, and of biotechnology in and around Cambridge, UK (Csikszentmihalyi, 1996). ­Fukuyama (1999) suggests that face-to-face contact helps breeds social capital, which helps generate trust, enabling people in an informal network to be prepared to share ideas and so build on each others’ knowledge. Jelinek & Schoonhaven (1991) found that open, high-trust cultures were a necessary feature of innovative organizations. They found that Intel, Motorola, and Hewlett Packard were distinguished by openness and informality. Open cultures are not without cost, however. When more people can have a say, it often takes more time to discuss things prior to reaching agreement, and middle management may lose power.

The organizational culture and climate can have an impact on motivation and the capacity for innovation. A creative climate is one in which people feel free to challenge ideas, mistakes are tolerated, and staff members are supported in creative endeavors. In creative cultures, managers walk the talk so that people know that it is OK to question accepted practice (Ekvall, 1991). It also helps if the chain of command is not too long, so that creative decisions do not get lost as they wend their way through bureaucracy and associated committees. This form of management suits some people but not others. ­Culture-change programs may lose a percentage of managers who cannot adapt to the loss of power entailed in a more open way of working (Henry, 1992).

Culture-change programs need to be sensitive to the subculture they are operating in. Staff members who are more task-oriented seem to favor more conservative climates than do interest-driven and creative employees. Ekvall (1997), for example, found that production departments tend to include staff members who favor more conservative climates than their R&D colleagues.

There appear to be some differences in favored cultures across sectors. West & Farr (1990, West, 2002) found that for public-sector employees in UK samples, clarity about the goal to be reached was an important characteristic of the climate they deemed most likely to be ­successfully creative. This perception may be especially important in environments that ­feature successive initiatives (as many UK public-sector employees have experienced) and may also be characteristic of a workforce for whom security is particularly important.

It is not surprising that the idea that allowing greater openness, local empowerment, decentralization, and less management control will enhance organizational creativity (e.g. Peters, 1988) has been championed in Western, individualistic, Anglo-Saxon cultures, since a participative approach to management fits with their cultural values. The application of these ideas to more hierarchical cultures would seem to be problematic, though managers in such cultures often concede that some moves towards opening up the organizational culture to capitalize on employee ideas would be desirable (Keong, 2000).

Tom Peters has been a high-profile exponent of the benefits of an entrepreneurial culture. On the basis of an examination of the characteristics of organizations perceived to be excellent in the early 1980s, Peters & Waterman (1982) advocated a culture that: had a bias for action; stayed close to customers; encouraged autonomy and entrepreneurship; aimed for productivity through people; favored “hands-on” management; strived for value; stuck to what it was good at; used simple forms; and had a lean ­staffing structure. Unfortunately, as is often the case with organizations paraded as examples of good ­practice, many of Peters’ firms have not fared so well over time (e.g. Pascale, 1990).





15.3.2 Changing culture


In the 1990s, many companies attempted to change their organizational culture in the direction of greater participation and flexibility in order to draw out the creativity of their staff, as a means of enhancing responsiveness and as a path to continuous innovation. Government initiatives such as the UK government’s “Winning Company” program endorsed this approach by advocating practices that unlocked people’s potential, empowered employees, provided good communications, increased knowledge of customers, offered training, and made use of teamwork. For example, Fogden (1999) described an attempt to open up culture in the UK government employment service that adopted principles being used in many creative-change programs. These involved identifying and sharing good practice, using an organizational-culture inventory to monitor opinion, offering workshops that looked at creativity and change, supporting managers taking innovative initiatives, establishing and monitoring the progress of pilots, and identifying lessons that could be learned.

A serious problem with some change programs of this type occurs when the change is in the rhetoric rather than the reality. This happens when managers are not “walking the talk” and employee cynicism sets in. A number of culture-change programs in the 1990s coincided with a need for cost-cutting. Keenoy & Anthony (1992) described the difficulty of trying to engage employees in an achievement-oriented culture-change program when, for example, TSB had just announced that 5000 staff members were to be made redundant. Equally, Watson (1994) describes the failure of aspects of a participative culture-change effort at ZTC Ryland, a British telecommunications plant, that had been welcomed by many managers. The proclaimed merits of autonomy, freedom, and respect came at a time of financial stringency and cost constraints, which the workers thought conflicted with the values espoused by the change program, and as a consequence they felt they were not being treated with respect. In her substantive review, Legge (1994) came to the conclusion that the benefits of establishing an enterprise culture did not have the impact and were not as lasting as senior managers claimed.

Another mistake some companies make is to expect progress too quickly. An early BP culture-change program aimed to shift from a “yes but” to a “yes and” culture by stressing the importance of open thinking, personal impact, empowerment, and networking. However, key personnel involved admitted that the 2 years allowed for the process was much too short (Henry, 1992).

Successful culture change is usually a long-term process. Semler (1996) noted that it took 15 years to transform his father’s traditional and hierarchical organization from a bureaucratic top-heavy firm to one that was largely self-organizing. John Harvey-Jones (1988) also took many years to change ICI, one of the largest chemical companies in the world. Karmoy, the largest aluminum plant in Europe—based in Norway—built a successful creative-change program around a gardening metaphor and workshops aimed at all staff. Following this program, the number of employee suggestions rose, absenteeism was reduced, and production and satisfaction levels increased. The initiative followed a series of earlier change programs in the company, which the lead consultant felt laid important groundwork for its acceptability (Parker, 1991).





15.4 Developing People


Many change efforts aim to push responsibility down and empower staff members to enable the organization to demonstrate greater flexibility and faster response and incorporate valuable new processes more rapidly. The rationale is that a workforce that has more flexibility and is empowered to take local decisions is likely to be not only more motivated but also generally more creative, cooperative, and committed. Of course, the degree of employee empowerment varies considerably, from call-center employees being allowed to vary their greetings slightly and make use of a suggestion box, through communication and consultation about management decisions, to staff at Jaguar being allowed to select their own training courses or companies that allow their staff to self-organize.





15.4.1 Organizational learning


A popular approach to organizational change through the development of people is encapsulated in the idea of the learning organization (Pedlar et al., 1991; Senge, 1991). In this approach, more attention is paid to the development of individuals, and teams and systems are developed to support staff development. Focus groups may be set up. Staff members are encouraged to engage in both single-loop learning, where they learn new skills and are empowered to adjust and refine existing processes, and double-loop learning, where they advance their understanding so they can engage with, challenge, and change existing systems. The learning-organization approach draws on earlier work in action learning (Revans, 1969), reflective action (Argyris & Schön, 1978), and systems thinking (Beer, 1981).

Different proponents have had slightly different emphases. Senge (1991) stressed the importance of systems thinking, along with personal and professional development, expectation and assumption, fostering a shared vision, and the centrality of team learning in organizational transformation. Pedlar et al. (1991) focused more on developing employees. Garvin (1993) recommended encouraging an environment where people systematically attempt to solve problems, experiment with new approaches, and learn from their own experience and the practice of others and transfer the knowledge gained. He also suggested training people in problem-solving skills, giving them opportunities to apply them as boundaries opened up both within the organization and across the supply chain.

Education and training is often a key part of people-development programs. ­Companies such as ICI, Ford, Rover, Bosch, and Baxi have all offered to pay for employees to take a course of their choosing. In many cases, these include non-work-related courses. The idea is that the participation will help build trust in the company, increase the ­employee’s self-confidence and enthusiasm, and perhaps make them more flexible at work (Holden, 1996).

Attention is also often given to interpersonal relations, acculturation, and professional development. Giving 360-degree feedback may help people understand how they are perceived at various levels of the organization. Junior members of staff may be assigned a learning buddy to discuss issues with, and increasingly middle and senior managers are being given the option of coaching to assist them with individual issues (Bartlett & Ghosal, 1995).





15.4.2 Self-organization


Another perspective on creativity, drawn from work on complexity, sees it as a property that emerges naturally from complex adaptive systems (such as organizations) in which the active “agents” are free to interact, learn, and communicate (Stacy, 1996; see Chapter 12 of this volume for a discussion of complexity). From this perspective, creativity is seen as a property that is natural but very hard to predict and control. This leads to a focus on the community of practice (Wenger, 1998) within which creative people operate. It draws attention to the importance of co-creation and leads consultants such as Wheatley (1994) to stress the need to facilitate interactions among employees and across the networks they engage in (Stacy, 1996 and Chapter 12).

Some organizations see management control as such a serious dampener on the natural creativity of employees that they empower their staffs to a much greater degree than is normal; effectively these companies are self-organized by the workers. This approach is found among professionals whose work is complex, such as doctors, lawyers, software engineers, and academics. It has also been tried in other kinds of small- and medium-sized firms but is less common in large firms. Ricardo Semler’s (1996) transformation of Semco in Brazil offers a well-known example of this kind of approach. He began by ending clocking in and out and moved on to discarding manuals and reducing layers of management. By the 1990s, the workers had an active voice in corporate decisions, the accounts were transparent and open to inspection, company rules had been curtailed to a series of ­cartoons, and staff members were free to choose how they wanted to run their sections. Most opted for a more participative approach, but the cultures developed varied across different sections. Teams selected employees locally, appraised their bosses, and had a say in their salaries and bonus levels (Semler, 1996).

Ken Lewis of Dutton Engineering achieved high levels of creativity, quality, and productivity by allowing employees to self-organize in his small sheet-metal-working company. There were no set hours and decision-making was pushed down to the lowest level. Like Semco, he embraced open-book accounting, profit-sharing, sponsoring of training, and self-managed teams who dealt directly with the customer, handling design, cost, and ­delivery (Lewis & Lytton, 1995).

Some successful creative companies aim to support a loose creative atmosphere through internal design, such as open-plan offices and pleasant environments (e.g. Hallmark), and/or the provision of goodies such as free pastries to keep staff happy (e.g. Google).

Oticon, a medium-sized well-established Danish hearing-aid manufacturer, dispensed with the traditional organizational hierarchy. Its 1500 staff members were free to attach themselves to any of the company’s projects. The office was completely open-plan and staff members had movable desks and phones, which they could relocate according to the project they were working on. This type of arrangement aims to capitalize on the idea that important breakthroughs happen in informal corridor conversations, not office memos. Employers are often keen on the cost savings afforded by open-plan offices, but the arrangement does not suit all personality types. Introverts often work better in environments that afford them more privacy and quiet.





15.5 Structure


Over the last 50 years, many organizations have radically changed their structures. They have delayed, decentralized, divisionalized, and in many cases downsized as they outsourced non-core elements, such as IT, manufacturing, customer enquiries, billing, and/or cleaning. Many innovative large companies have followed Hewlett-Packard in organizing divisions round product types rather than basing their structures on functional specializations. Pioneers like Volvo based their manufacturing around cell structures. The drivers for these changes have been both an a desire for an increase in flexibility and a reduction of costs. The increased flexibility and shorter decision chains afforded by leaner, flatter structures and the increased use of multidisciplinary teams help facilitate organizational creativity.





15.5.1 Innovative projects


Creative and innovative projects do not always fit happily in traditional organizational structures with separate R&D, design, development, production, sales, and service departments. In such settings, innovation typically operates as a relay structure, moving from department to department. This approach can be relatively slow and there is considerable potential for the creative impetus to be lost en route. Alternative ways of organizing creative and innovative projects include the matrix organization, where staff members retain departmental duties but are also assigned to special projects. The downside is that dual reporting can be problematic. Instead, staff members may be relieved of ordinary duties and placed on an independent project team for the duration of the project. Top management sometimes supports a venture-team approach, where a small group working on creative and innovative projects operates outside standard reporting procedures (Holt, 1991). Nonaka & Takeuchi (1995) champion an approach where routine work is handled through a traditional structure and parallel project teams pursue creative and innovative goals.

Many creative projects are of a limited duration. The construction and media industries have a long history of specialist subcontractors who come together for the period of a project. The highly creative film and advertising production industry works this way very successfully. Kanter (1988) found that rehiring depends partly on reputation but also on whether those being considered have successful working relationships with people already hired to the project. This practice is based upon the premise that people work better with those they know and trust.





15.5.2 Organizational boundaries


The boundary of the organization itself may need to change in order to enable it to be more creative in boosting efficiency, meeting deadlines, and mitigating risk. This usually entails collaboration and alliances based on partnerships, mergers, or outsourcing, whether for development, manufacture, or marketing.

For example, Sony and Philips formed a partnership to develop the CD with a view to setting an accepted international standard—a goal they achieved. Earlier they had each failed when going it alone (with the introduction of the laser disc and Betamax video).

The high cost of innovation encourages some organizations to undertake joint ventures. For example, car manufacturers like Ford, SEAT, and VW use common components over a variety of brands and use their particular logo to market essentially the same car in order to save on development costs.

In industries requiring a very large capital base, such as aerospace or stock trading, a logical way of providing the liquidity necessary for new creative endeavors is to merge with larger companies. Many financial companies have been part of successive mergers.

In contrast, less capital-intensive industries such as retailing have found vertical disaggregation advantageous. Benetton, for example, subcontracts 95% of its manufacturing activities. Marks & Spencer and Nike have tried to maintain a close relationship with their suppliers. Ken Lewis of Dutton argues that it is better to develop a long-term relationship with suppliers where possible, and that it is more important to pick a supplier with whom you share values than the cheapest tender if the relationship is to work long-term.

Developments in IT have enabled new forms of organization. Recently, some of the most successful organizations, such as Visa, Amazon, eBay and Google, have flourished by providing interorganizational networks. With Visa, for example, it is the connectivity to many other organizations that is important (Mulgan & Briscoe, 1996). Typically such businesses operate with a relatively small central staff. Visa also has distributed ownership.

A lot of creativity and innovation happens across organizational networks, especially informal networks. More flexible structures and open cultures help people take advantage of such networks, both within and outside the organization (Nohria & Eccles, 1992).





15.6 Developing Ideas


Organizational-change processes like scenario-planning, benchmarking, and suggestion schemes are commonly employed to help organizations develop more creative ideas.





15.6.1 Foresight


Scenario-planning offers ways of anticipating different possible futures. The effect of such exercises is that managers tend to become more flexible as their perception of possible future realities is expanded and altered. Shell famously used scenario-planning to help it better prepare for the unexpected oil price hike in the 1970s (Wack, 1985). I have used scenario planning successfully to help public-sector workers realize the changed nature of the external context in which they work. Following scenario-planning, participants are often more open to change and more likely to proffer creative ideas.

Not all good ideas need to come from inside an organization. Indeed, history teaches us that the strategy of being first to market is expensive and high-risk (e.g. Kay, 1998). There are many examples of fast followers winning out: VHS’s triumph over Betamax, for example. Checking out the competition and copying it, or “creative swiping,” as Peters (1988) termed it, is a common route to organizational change. Benchmarking offers a way of formally analyzing what the competition is doing (Zairi, 1996). Camp (1989) claims this enables companies to use ideas from proven practice, consider many options, and check market reality.





15.6.2 Nurturing ideas


The evidence suggests that employees across an organization have many innovative ideas that are very valuable to their employers (Tidd et al., 1997). Many organizational-change programs aim to capitalize on such locally-informed knowledge and ideas. Developing suggestion schemes, giving free time, and providing idea screening processes are ways of doing so.

Suggestions schemes tend to quickly pay for themselves, but their success is dependent on the organizational culture in which they are launched (Schroeder & Robinson, 1991). Not surprisingly, where trust is higher, the number of suggestions per employee tends also to be higher. Honda, for example, has had eight suggestions per employee per year, and Toyota has claimed that it implements over 90% of the suggestions it receives (Toyoda, 1987). Opinion is mixed as to whether or not it is a good idea to offer financial incentives for suggestions. Some companies believe in financial inducements, while others, such as Semco, are strongly against them, believing they mitigate against a culture in which ideas are shared freely. Companies may also offer formal recognition of an idea’s worth through awards (Semler, 1994).

A high level of employee freedom as to how to go about one’s work helps companies like Google and 3M sustain a high level of creativity year after year (Mitchell, 1989). One practical way to encourage creativity is to allow members of staff a percentage of time to work on their own projects. Google grants its employees 20% of their time for such activities. Staff members can attract further resources by interesting colleagues in employing their 20% free time towards their own project. Sometimes staff members can also bid for resources to support their pet projects. People are generally more creative and put in much more effort when working on things that interest them, so many successful innovations have started life as a free-time project: Post-it Notes are a well-known example (Nayak & Ketteringham, 1991).

Kodak has used an internal innovation screening system, where innovation facilitators help employees develop their ideas through a process that allows them to bid for supporting resources. This is a five-stage process: idea generation, screening, group review, sponsorship-seeking, and sponsorship. Companies need some such screening mechanism, both to help employees nurture creative ideas and to help the company determine which ideas to fund and for how long, recognizing that only a small percentage of the population of ideas will reach the market. Kodak, for example, found that between 1979 and 1988 only 4% of ideas became commercial products (Rosenfeld & Servo, 1991).

Of course, not all creative ideas are developed in-house. In some sectors, new ideas are regularly bought in. For example, in creative industries such as pop music and medicine, smaller creative start-ups often come up with new approaches, while larger established players undertake more of the manufacture and marketing. Large pharmaceutical ­companies often buy up small biotech companies with promising new approaches and take their products forward through expensive development, testing, production, and sales.





15.7 Managing Innovation


The type of organizational-development (OD) process needed to develop and/or sustain innovation varies with the size of the organization, the sector, the stage of the creative endeavor, and the culture within which the change is to be undertaken.





15.7.1 Stage


Small organizations often have open cultures and their main challenge for sustaining creativity may have most to do with issues around gaining funding for expansion and putting procedures in place that enable them to capitalize on their initial success. A known problem for many creative small businesses is that expansion generally involves the creative CEO, who started the company, being willing to let go of control of all aspects of their creative endeavor; a difficult move for many who have put their heart and soul into getting a business up and running (Bolton, 1971).

The UK and US governments often see small business as their economic salvation and assume that new jobs and areas of growth will come from ideas within this sector. In the UK, successive government initiatives have aimed to help small businesses set up, develop business plans, and take on employees. A weakness in this policy is that many small-­business owners are attracted to the sector because of a preference for control and a consequent dislike of working for large organizations. Such people have no interest in expanding their small business as they like the control small size offers them (Gray, 1993).

Many creative people and inventors are not good business managers, though there are exceptions like James Dyson, the inventor of the bagless vacuum and MD of Dyson. In larger organizations, creative individuals need to be able to handle politics if their good idea is to reach the market. Many find they need champions and sponsors within the organization to help support them through the development and marketing procedures (Schon, 1963). Even in 3M, a company famed for producing a high percentage of new products each year, it took 10 years for the inventor of the not-very-sticky glue in Post-it pads to find a manager who understood the value of this substance and who was willing to champion this invention to market (Nayak & Ketteringham, 1991). Organizations can help develop creativity by acknowledging the importance of the different roles that are typically needed to bring a creative idea to market and to support associated activities—for example, giving time for creative scientists to explore and attend conferences and allowing technological gatekeepers to keep abreast of new developments (Maidique, 1990).

Creativity and innovation in organizations often follows a phased entrepreneurial process: first a creative exploratory phase, where someone comes up with an idea; then a development phase, where a prototype is worked up to a working model; and finally a phase in which the new product or service is marketed and distributed to the public.

There is a case for taking account of people’s motivation when rewarding them for successful creative endeavors. Creative people are often motivated by job satisfaction and so work well when given the maximum autonomy possible. However, when it comes to innovative development, multidisciplinary teams are often involved in taking an initial idea forward and developing an economic working prototype. In this stage, a group-level reward structure is considered more effective. In contrast, entrepreneurs who are more focused on reaching the maximum market often value financial recompense for the risks they take in achieving this end.





15.7.2 Sector


Innovation is often associated with radical change, but even in areas such as technological innovation, most new products and services emerge from evolutionary change (Henry et al., 2002a). Even a radical change such as the light bulb emerges after incremental improvement in associated technologies, such as vacuum pumping, electricity distribution, and glass-blowing. The sources of innovation tend to vary by industry.

Governments often implicitly subscribe to a model of innovation in which innovation in science emerges from universities and/or R&D departments. This seems to hold true for scientific inventions and industrial innovations like nylon and lasers (Rothwell, 1994). However, commercial applications from basic science are by no means guaranteed. Government records in identifying winners have not always been encouraging, and other factors are more important in many industries.

In consumer products, market demand appears to have been the key trigger for the development of ever-more-sophisticated refrigerators, airbags, and televisions, for example. The importance of market-led innovation has caused some organizations to make a point of working with consumers to find out how they use their products. Apple famously uses this type of process to help design very user-friendly and successful computers.

Entrepreneurs can also be critical in certain high-risk innovations. For example, Morita, the chairman of Sony, famously championed the very successful Walkman against engineering advice and tested the prototype with friends on the golf course (Morita, 1988).

Others factors are also important: technological development has been critical in electronics and chemicals, design is particularly important for machinery, production for automobiles, and software for finance and travel (Tidd et al., 1997). By the 1980s, integrated development, parallel processes, networking, and attention to quality appear to have given the Japanese and other Eastern manufacturers an edge over many of their Western counterparts in a number of fields. Nowadays, enhanced ICT, flexibility, attention to continuous improvement, and ongoing learning all seem to be critical in getting innovations out quickly enough with sufficient quality (Henry et al., 2002a). See Tidd & Bessant (2009) for elaboration on ways of managing innovation.

Creativity entails doing something new, so it is often very hard to predict the outcome of any given innovation. This is particularly true for development-engineering projects that are also very expensive. Organizations sometimes task more than one team to find a way of developing a particular innovation, such as the tilting train. Usually such an organization can only afford to take one team’s design to completion. In this type of development-engineering setting it is very important that the management decision is seen to be fair by all parties, as it is very dispiriting to lose out after working hard on an innovative project for months or years.

Innovations often follow a cycle, where the introduction of a radical innovation is followed by a series of incremental improvements (Foster, 1986). Essentially, a radically new product emerges (e.g. refrigerators instead of ice) and a number of competing products enter the market. After a while, a dominant design tends to emerge and competitors attempt to gain an advantage by reducing costs and/or improving quality (Abernathy & Utterback, 1988). These efforts foster many innovative organizational-change programs.





15.8 Improving Processes


Organizational change has been characterized by a succession of management fads, from managerial grids and decision trees in the 1960s through management by objectives in the 1970s to an emphasis on corporate culture in the early 1990s (Pascale, 1990). Organizational-change processes designed to improve the quality, cost, functionality, and/or reliability of creative and innovative products and services have exhibited similar successive fashions (Henry et al., 2002a). Recently we have seen continuous improvement, total quality management (TQM), lean management, reengineering, Six Sigma, the learning organization, and knowledge management, for example.





15.8.1 Incremental change


In large organizations, there are two key competing trends with an impact on creativity: first, increased participation, and second, enhanced monitoring. While the former is conducive to creativity, the latter is not necessarily so (e.g. Cummings et al., 1975; Zhou, 2003). Nevertheless, in many sectors, from call centers to manufacturing, surveillance of day-to-day work has been increasing (Ball, 2010).

One of the most influential change approaches aimed at improving innovation processes is the quality movement. This came to the fore in the West partly as a response to the superior reliability of a number of Japanese products (notably cars and electronic consumer goods) in the latter part of the 20th century. W. Edwards Deming (1982) is credited with inspiring Japanese companies like Toyota, Honda, Sony, and Panasonic to improve the reliability and quality of their products through a series of measures that drew on local knowledge to improve procedures. These included looking for faults in the system rather than blaming individuals, developing a culture that drives out fear, thinking long-term, choosing the most reliable rather than the cheapest suppliers, and training people on the job. These ideas were subsequently taken up by South East Asian, US, and European companies.

Quality approaches have developed over time. Quality departments that inspected the quality of other people’s goods morphed into quality control, which encouraged feedback on the causes of defects and necessary corrective action. Quality-assurance programs introduced quality management systems to manage product manufacture and service provision. Various procedural standards, such as ISO 9000, aimed to certify that firms were adopting best practices for quality improvement (Dale & Cooper, 1982). TQM focused less on doing things by the book and more on engaging hearts and minds and empowering workers. Furthermore, TQM worked with suppliers, employees, and customers to improve quality continuously (Oakland, 1989).

The advantages of TQM are widely recognized and it has been instituted in many ­companies, but its success has been debated. Some employers, notably those in small- and ­medium-sized firms, remain suspicious of the bureaucratic and resource-intensive nature of implementing quality standards like ISO 9000 (Briscoe et al., 2005). Zairi (1996) concluded that quality programs had worked for the majority of people in his survey, as most reported higher product turnover and higher profit per employee than the industry average. However, after a review of successes and failures, Hill & Wilkinson (1995) concluded that most UK TQM initiatives failed. They suggested that a number of organizations were going through the motions but were not transforming sufficiently or granting sufficient resources to the exercise to derive meaningful long-term benefits. In addition, not all employees adopt TQM procedures; Garrahan & Stewart (1992) argue that Nissan’s Neighbor Check code, for example, introduced a form of scrutiny and social control that workers found stressful.

Notions of continuous and incremental improvement are central to TQM and to other recent OD methodologies like lean management and Six Sigma (Bhuiyan & Baghel, 1999). Six Sigma is a quality-engineering process that is popular in manufacturing. Focusing on human errors, this methodology is linked to continuous improvement and organizational change (Dale et al., 2000). Lean manufacturing draws inspiration from Japanese manufacturing techniques. It focuses on removing waste, getting things right the first time, and just-in-time inventory management (Womack et al., 1990). Hines et al. (2004) offer a review.

Writers such as Harding (1995) place greater emphasis on learning as a central process in positive quality initiatives. For example, the European Foundation for Quality Management’s Business Excellence model offers a self-assessment tool. This is based on a results-oriented, customer-focused model that encourages involvement, innovation, partnership, and learning. It involves collecting data on a number of processes and identifying strengths, areas for improvement, key opportunities, plans for implementing actions that arise from the exercise, and periodic reviews. Advocates like Oakland et al. (2002) argue that the model provides a framework that helps organizations pursue comprehensively excellent performance. Critics argue that this type of assessment process misses the more critical factors of engaging staff and honing processes (Dale et al., 2006).





15.8.2 Radical change


Organizational-change methods that come into prominence often reflect a development of the time. Knowledge engineering, for example, came to the fore as computer access and storage took a major leap forward.

Business process reengineering (BPR) emerged with the introduction of new ICT capabilities that became central to business endeavor. It offers a radical approach to organizational change, focused on fundamentally redesigning existing processes around outcomes by simplifying them and removing redundant stages, personnel, and even departments (Ventrakamen, 1994). It has become associated with downsizing, but Hammer (1990) argued for its potential to enhance value, and gives examples such as substantial savings on Ford’s accounts-payment department. However, Davenport & Stockard (1994) point out how difficult it is to treat an existing company as if it were a clean slate and redesign processes from scratch. Even Champy, one of the originators of BPR, admits only a small proportion of reengineering processes work well. On the other hand, Pascale (in Henry et al., 2002b) contends that 80% of all organizational-change programs fail.

Those commissioning organizational-change programs sometimes construe successful innovation as a matter of removing blocks. This approach to innovation is rarely sufficient. While it is possible to tackle many blocks to efficient production and it is prudent to investigate the likely market for potential innovations, it is difficult to anticipate with any degree of certainty whether radical innovations will be successful. Rather, organizations gain value more reliably by creating an environment that is more conducive to the emergence of ­creativity and innovation.





15.9 Conclusion


In order to develop and sustain creativity, an organization generally needs a relatively open climate, empowered staff, a responsive and flexible structure, integrated procedures, idea-development systems, and good external partnerships. Some of the main ways of achieving these goals include opening up the organizational culture, developing a responsive structure, pushing responsibility down, nurturing ideas, integrating procedures, continuously improving processes, and communicating internally and with like-minded partners and customers.

There are has been quite a lot of research on technological innovation and product-development processes in manufacturing, and a fair amount of attention has been given to small business. The application of organizational change for creativity and innovation in other areas, such as the public or not-for-profit sectors and the service industries, has ­received less attention and deserves more. Equally, it would be useful to conduct studies focused on how creative change works in more hierarchical cultures. Procedures and methods developed in Anglo-Saxon and individualistic cultures characterized by openness, high employee participation in decision-making, and an emphasis on engaging intrinsic motivation and encouraging empowerment may not be as effective in work cultures that are less sympathetic to these features.
