16


Individual Readiness for Organizational Change


Myungweon Choi and Wendy E.A. Ruona





16.1 Introduction


No organization today exists in a stable environment. Current scholars, especially the ­proponents of complexity theories, consider that all organizations are under the influence of multiple changes (Brown & Eisenhardt, 1997; Burnes, 2004a; Stacey et al., 2002; Styhre, 2002; Tetenbaum, 1998). According to these scholars, change is inherent in human action and therefore necessarily occurs in any context of human social interactions (Ford & Ford, 1995). As organizations are sites of continuously evolving human action, they are in a continuous state of change and, in order to survive, must develop the ability to continuously change themselves (Burnes, 2004b; Tsoukas & Chia, 2002).

Organizational leaders are thus continually charged with introducing and implementing various initiatives to change their organizations. The problem is that in reality many change efforts do not result in their intended aims. Specifically, researchers have estimated that at least two-thirds of change projects fail (Beer & Nohria, 2000a, 2000b; Burke & Biggart, 1997; Burnes, 2004c). The cause of many organizations’ inability to achieve the intended aims of their change efforts is often considered an implementation failure, rather than a flaw innate in the change initiative itself (Klein & Sorra, 1996). In particular, failures are often attributed to an organization’s inability to provide for an effective unfreezing process (Lewin, 1997) before attempting a change induction (Kotter, 1995, 1996; Schein, 1987, 1999b).

Individual readiness for organizational change—which is defined as the extent to which an individual believes that a change is needed and that he or she has the capacity for the change (Armenakis et al., 1993; Eby et al., 2000; Jansen, 2000; Jones et al., 2005)—is increasingly important in this context. The purpose of this chapter is to review the idea underlying the construct of individual readiness for organizational change (henceforth, “readiness for change”) and discuss its implications for organizational change research. For this purpose, this chapter evaluates the common use of the term “resistance to change” and reconceptualizes organizational members’ attitudes towards organizational change, focusing on the concept of individual readiness for organizational change.





16.2 Challenges to the Concept of Resistance to Change


Traditionally, individuals’ attitudes towards organizational change have often been ­conceptualized as resistance to change, which has long been considered a barrier to ­organizational change attempts (Dent & Goldberg, 1999a; Jermier et al., 1994). However, some recent researchers have begun to criticize the prevalent view on resistance to change and suggest a need for a multifaceted perspective on individuals’ attitudes towards organizational change. In this section, the three most researched topics on this issue are reviewed: the positive aspect of resistance, the situational causes of resistance, and resistance created by change agents.





16.2.1 Positive aspect of resistance


Resistance to change has been viewed as an obstacle that change recipients (usually employees) present to change initiatives and as something that must be overcome. What is implicit in this view is that the change agent (usually management) is right and change recipients are wrong when it comes to change.

However, some researchers have argued that individuals’ reactions to change are not necessarily dysfunctional obstacles or liabilities to successful change. Rather, according to the researchers, resistance can raise awareness and add momentum for change (Ford et al., 2008), provide a feedback mechanism vital to the change process (Klein, 1985), and serve as a source of information for the development of more effective change efforts (Knowles & Linn, 2004; Piderit, 2000; Waddell & Sohal, 1998). From this point of view, resistance to change can serve as an asset and a resource in the implementation and successful accomplishment of change, and we need to carefully examine it, rather than take it as given or ignore it.





16.2.2 Situational causes of resistance


As Knowles & Linn (2004) noted, depending on the context, the source of psychological resistance can be attributed either to the person or to the situation. Organizational researchers have shown that resistance in the context of organizational change is more often attributed to the situation than to the person. For example, Kotter (1995) showed that employees often understand the new vision and want to make it happen when organizations attempt a major change. However, according to Kotter, obstacles to change often reside in the organization’s structure and in its system—for example, performance evaluation, compensation, and succession planning—that are not aligned with the desired new behavior. These force people to “choose between the new vision and their own self-interest” (p. 64).

At least two lines of research have supported the idea that resistance in the context of organizational change is often attributed to the situation specific to a change. As Burke (2008) noted, some researchers stress that resistance to change comes from ­experiencing a lack of choice (i.e. the imposition of change) or from being forced to move to some new state of being and acting. The concept of reactance helps to explain this human phenomenon. Psychological reactance theory says that when a person senses that someone else is limiting his or her freedom to choose or act, an uncomfortable state of reactance arises, creating motivation to reassert that freedom (Brehm & Brehm, 1981). The more important the freedoms threatened or the more arbitrary (i.e. not legitimate), ­blatant, direct, and demanding the threat, the greater the reactance (Fuegen & Brehm, 2004; Knowles & Linn, 2004). According to these researchers, individuals are not simply and naturally resistant to change. Rather, they resist the imposition of change, or the way change is imposed upon them.

Another view, based on empirical studies of various constructs related to individuals’ attitudes towards organizational change—such as readiness for change (e.g. Eby et al., 2000), commitment to change (e.g. Herscovitch & Meyer, 2002), openness to change (e.g. Wanberg & Banas, 2000), and cynicism about organizational change (e.g. Wanous et al., 2000)—is that the formation of these constructs is influenced by various factors specific to a given situation. In particular, researchers have reported that contextual factors—for example, justice perception (Bernerth et al., 2007; Caldwell et al., 2004; ­Fedor et al., 2006), participation in the change process (Brown & Cregan, 2008; Devos et al., 2007; Reichers et al., 1997; Wanberg & Banas, 2000; Wanous et al., 2000), ­information-sharing (Brown & Cregan, 2008; Wanberg & Banas, 2000), trust in management (Devos et al., 2007), and experience with previous change projects (Devos et al., 2007; Wanous et al., 2000)—greatly influence attitudinal reactions to organizational change. These studies indicate that individuals’ attitudes towards organizational change are not a personality-based predisposition. Rather, they are a learned response, shaped by the specific context (Choi, 2011).





16.2.3 Resistance created by change agents


Some researchers with a social constructionist viewpoint challenge the idea that resistance represents objective phenomena that exist independent of change agents. Rather, they contend that change agents create resistance by expecting resistance (Dent & Goldberg, 1999a; Ford et al., 2002, 2008). According to these authors, change requires both change agents and change recipients to engage in sense-making (Weick, 1995). When change is attempted, change agents try to determine, “How will this get accomplished?”, while change recipients try to determine, “What will happen to me?” (Ford et al., 2008; Gioia et al., 1994). If change agents expect that resistance to change is natural and inevitable, a self-fulfilling prophecy may emerge. By shaping the very phenomenon to which they are paying attention, change agents’ expectation predisposes them to look for resistance and to make sense of others’ actions in such a way as to confirm that expectation, thereby ­reaffirming their belief that change recipients resist change (Dent & Goldberg, 1999a; Ford et al., 2008). When change agents go into a change situation expecting resistance, they are likely to act in a way that promotes resistance, much as teachers who expect students to underperform convey this lack of confidence in their ability, so that the students do indeed perform poorly (Rosenthal & Jacobson, 1992).





16.2.4 Evaluation of the concept of resistance


Kurt Lewin’s idea of resistance is helpful in evaluating the common use of the term ­“resistance to change.” Lewin (1997) conceived of resistance as a restraining force moving in the direction of maintaining the status quo in opposition to a force pushing for change. According to this perspective, resistance is a systemic phenomenon rather than an individual predisposition. However, since Lewin’s conceptualization, resistance has come to be seen largely as a psychological phenomenon that a change recipient experiences. It is often used to justify organizations’ failings, through blaming individuals for the unsatisfactory results of change efforts (Dent & Goldberg, 1999a, 1999b; Ford et al., 2002, 2008; Krantz, 1999; Maurer, 2006).

As has been noted, resistance is not a personality-based predisposition. When confronted with the possibility of change, most of us are likely to be ambivalent (Burke, 2008; Piderit, 2000), and our attitudes are shaped based on the contextual factors specific to a given situation. These attitudes may contain rich information concerning the change implementation. Furthermore, as stated in Section 16.2.3, recent research even urges us to reconsider the role change agents play in creating resistance (Dent & Goldberg, 1999a; Ford et al., 2008). Therefore, it is necessary for us to shift our attention to the overall system of change and to make a more valid conceptualization of individuals’ attitudes towards change.





16.3 Reconceptualizing Individuals’ Attitudes Towards Organizational Change


After reviewing the criticism of the traditional view of resistance to change, the following question arose: Is there another way to understand individuals’ attitudes towards organizational change? While the tenets of resistance to change have been challenged, some researchers have begun stressing other constructs, such as readiness for change (e.g. Eby et al., 2000), commitment to change (e.g. Herscovitch & Meyer, 2002), openness to change (e.g. Wanberg & Banas, 2000), and cynicism about organizational change (e.g. Wanous et al., 2000). This chapter focuses on one of these constructs, readiness for change, because it is comprehensive and it captures Lewin’s (1997) idea of unfreezing well (Armenakis et al., 1993; Choi & Ruona, 2011; Eby et al., 2000).





16.3.1 Lewin’s idea of the unfreezing step


According to Lewin (1997), a particular set of behaviors is the result of two groups of forces: those striving to maintain the status quo and those pushing for change. When both sets of forces are equal, current behaviors are maintained in a state Lewin called “quasi-­stationary equilibrium,” supported by a large force field of driving and restraining forces. To change the behaviors—to successfully discard an old behavior and adopt a new one—one needs to unfreeze the equilibrium between the two sets of forces, by increasing the forces pushing for change and/or by decreasing those forces maintaining the current state.

Building on Lewin’s idea of unfreezing, Schein (1999a) identified three conditions that are necessary to achieve unfreezing: disconfirmation of the validity of the status quo, induction of survival anxiety, and creation of psychological safety. Disconfirmation of the validity of the status quo functions as a primary driving force and is a prerequisite for the acceptance of new environmental circumstances requiring creative and generative learning. However, one can ignore the disconfirming information or deny its validity, unless it arouses survival anxiety—the feeling that if we do not change, we will fail to meet our needs or fail to achieve some goals or ideals that we have set for ourselves. In addition, the need to create psychological safety is even more critical because “unless sufficient psychological safety is created, the disconfirming information will be denied or in other ways defended against, no survival anxiety will be felt, and consequently, no change will take place” (Schein, 1999a, p. 61). In fact, the goal of various activities for change management is to create psychological safety (Schein, 1999a). In sum, in order to unfreeze the equilibrium, it is necessary to balance the amount of threat produced by disconfirming data with enough psychological safety to allow the change recipient to accept the information, feel the survival anxiety, and become motivated to change. Through these processes, organizational members’ beliefs and attitudes about a change can be altered, and they may perceive the change as necessary and as likely to be successful (Armenakis et al., 1993).

While Lewin (1997) thought that all three of his steps—unfreezing, moving, and ­refreezing—were critical to a successful change project, the reason so many change efforts fail is often attributed to an organization’s inability to provide for an effective unfreezing process before attempting a change induction (Kotter, 1995, 1996; Schein, 1987, 1999b). In fact, most organizational change models suggest that efforts to build momentum, to “warm-up” or “defrost” organizational members to the need for change, or to gain buy-in for the change initiative (Armenakis et al., 1993; Kotter, 1996; Schein, 1987, 1999a) are necessary to increase driving forces and/or decrease restraining forces and, therefore, are critical components for any successful change initiative.





16.3.2 The unfreezing step and individuals’ attitudes towards organizational change


“Attitude” refers to a person’s overall evaluation of people, objects, and issues. More ­specifically, it refers to how favorably or unfavorably, or how positively or negatively in general one views some object of judgment (Petty & Wegener, 1998). Therefore, ­attitudes towards an organizational change can be defined as an individual’s overall favorable/positive or unfavorable/negative evaluative judgment of a change initiative implemented by an organization (Lines, 2005).

As is widely acknowledged, it is important to separate general attitudes from specific ones (Fisher, 1980). In organizations, a person may have a general attitude towards change but at the same time possess different attitudes about particular changes. While the former may be more related to personality, the latter tends to be determined largely by the context (Katz & Kahn, 1978; Lau & Woodman, 1995). In other words, even though a person is supportive of and open to organizational change in general, his or her attitudes about a specific change being undertaken may vary depending on how he or she evaluates the contexts and the issues involved. Schein (1999a) suggested that individuals will form their attitudes towards a change based on the disconfirming information, survival anxiety, and psychological safety created in a situation specific to the change. In this respect, individuals’ attitudes towards a specific organizational change can be a measure of how effectively the organization has achieved the unfreezing process.

Recently, researchers have proposed several constructs reflecting this notion of unfreezing, including commitment to change, openness to change, cynicism about organizational change, and readiness for change (Choi, 2011). Commitment to change is defined as “a force (mind-set) that binds an individual to a course of action deemed necessary for the successful implementation of a change initiative” (Herscovitch & Meyer, 2002, p. 475). Openness to change has been defined as both willingness to support the change and positive affect about the potential consequences of the change (Miller et al., 1994; ­Wanberg & Banas, 2000). Cynicism about organizational change is defined as “a ­pessimistic viewpoint about change efforts being successful because those responsible for making changes are blamed for being unmotivated, incompetent, or both” (Wanous et al., 2000, p. 133). Finally, readiness for change is defined as organizational members’ belief in the organization’s capacity for making a successful change, the extent to which the change is needed, and the benefits the organization and its members can gain from the change ­(Armenakis et al., 1993; Holt et al., 2007). These constructs are similar in that they all reflect an individual’s overall positive or negative evaluative judgment of a specific change initiative (Choi, 2011). In addition, they are defined as the cognitive precursors to the behavioral support for a change effort.

Despite their commonalities, however, these four constructs focus on slightly different aspects of individuals’ attitudes towards organizational change and differ in the range of phenomena they capture (Choi, 2011). For example, the subconstructs of commitment to change include a belief in the inherent benefits of the change, a sense of obligation to ­provide support for the change, and a recognition of the costs associated with failure to support the change (Herscovitch & Meyer, 2002; Meyer et al., 2007). On the other hand, cynicism about organizational change focuses particularly on management capability and support for the change (Bernerth et al., 2007; Bommer et al., 2005; Reichers et al., 1997; Wanous et al., 2000). Among the aforementioned constructs, readiness for change is the most comprehensive, in that it captures various aspects of individuals’ attitudes towards organizational change, including their belief in the change-specific efficacy, appropriateness of the change, management support for the change, and personal benefit from the change (Armenakis et al., 1993; Choi & Ruona, 2011; Eby et al., 2000; Holt et al., 2007).





16.3.3 Defining individual readiness for organizational change


Initially, studies on individual readiness for change were published primarily in the health and medical literature (e.g. Block & Keller, 1998; Joe et al., 1998; Mayer, 1975; Morera et al., 1998; Prochaska et al., 1997). These studies usually focused on ceasing harmful health behaviors such as smoking and drug abuse and starting positive ones such as exercise, weight management, and eating nutritional meals. Individual readiness in this context is concerned with the extent to which an individual perceives a change as needed and whether he or she has the capacity for the change (Choi & Ruona, 2011).

Although individual readiness is clearly an individual-level construct, it requires a consideration of the organizational context when it is applied to organizational settings (Jansen, 2000). Organizational change is a situation that interrupts the normal patterns of an organization (Ford et al., 2008). In such a situation, in order to make sense of the new environment and to draw conclusions about its possible outcomes, individuals are actively involved in information-seeking, meaning-ascription, and assumption-making about the change process (Ford et al., 2008; Gioia et al., 1994). As a result, individuals form assumptions, expectations, and impressions about the change, which make up individual readiness for change in the organizational change context.

Researchers have defined individual readiness for organizational change in slightly different ways (Choi & Ruona, 2011). For example, Armenakis et al. (1993) and Jansen (2000) defined it in terms of the necessity of a specific change initiative and the ­organizational capacity to implement it successfully. Specifically, Armenakis et al. (1993) defined it as ­individuals’ beliefs, attitudes, and intentions regarding the extent to which changes are needed and the organization’s capacity to successfully make those changes. Jansen (2000) defined it as the extent to which individuals perceive an organization as being capable of making the change and the change as needed. Similarly, Eby et al. (2000) contended that individual readiness in the context of organizational change is related to the belief that the change is “both necessary and likely to be successful” (p. 422). On the other hand, Jones et al. (2005) additionally emphasized an individual’s belief in the benefits of the change. Nevertheless, these researchers all agree that individual readiness for organizational change involves an evaluation of the individual and organizational capacity for making a successful change, the need for the change, and the benefits the organization and its members might gain from the change (Armenakis et al., 1993; Eby et al., 2000; Holt et al., 2007; Jansen, 2000).

As some researchers have noted, the definitions of individual readiness for organizational change are conceptually similar to Lewin’s (1997) notion of the unfreezing step (Armenakis et al., 1993; Choi & Ruona, 2011; Eby et al., 2000). The unfreezing step in the organizational change context includes the process by which organizational members’ attitudes about a change initiative are altered in such a way that they perceive the change as necessary and as likely to be successful (Armenakis et al., 1993; Eby et al., 2000; Schein, 1999a). In this respect, when individuals become ready for a change initiative, this indicates that the unfreezing step has been successful.

In a similar vein, Rogers (2003) endorsed the importance of individual readiness for change in the innovation–decision process model. According to this model, individuals develop a favorable or unfavorable attitude towards an innovation in the persuasion stage based on prior conditions (previous practice, felt needs/problems, innovativeness, norms of the social systems) as well as on the knowledge they gained through the previous stage (knowledge stage). Rogers stressed that individuals’ attitudes towards an innovation formed in the persuasion stage affect the decision, implementation, and confirmation of the adoption of an innovation. By explaining the formation of ­individuals’ attitudes towards an innovation and emphasizing their role in innovation adoption, Rogers’ model also highlights the importance and relevance of individual readiness in the context of organizational change. In addition, Hall & Hord (1987) dealt with the core ideas of individual readiness for organizational change in their concern-based adoption model. The main premise underlying this is that a change initiative can be more successful if the concerns of those affected by it are considered. Concerns in their model are defined as “the composite representation of the feelings, preoccupation, thought, and consideration given to a particular issue or task” (p. 59) and develop through the stages including awareness, informational, personal, and consequence. These stages in Hall & Hord’s model are similar to the subconstructs of individual ­readiness for organizational change proposed by researchers (Armenakis et al., 1993; Eby et al., 2000; Holt et al., 2007).





16.3.4 Research on individual readiness for organizational change


Some researchers have conducted empirical studies on individual readiness for organizational change. These studies are usually concerned with the factors that contribute to increasing the level of individual readiness for change. Specifically, they show that individual readiness for organizational change increases when: (1) individuals believe in organizational ability to accommodate changing situations (Eby et al., 2000; Jones et al., 2005); (2) policies supporting change exist (Eby et al., 2000; McNabb & Sepic, 1995; Rafferty & Simons, 2006); (3) social relationships exist within the organization (Hanpachern et al., 1998; Jones et al., 2005; Madsen et al., 2005); (4) trust exists ­between peers and with leaders (Rafferty & Simons, 2006); and (5) organizational members participate at work (Eby et al., 2000; Jones et al., 2005). In addition, ­relationships have been reported between readiness for change and individual-level ­variables such as change self-efficacy (Cunningham et al., 2002; Kwahk & Lee, 2008; Rafferty & Simons, 2006), organizational commitment (Kwahk & Kim, 2008; Kwahk & Lee, 2008; Madsen et al., 2005), perceived personal competence (Kwahk & Kim, 2008), and job satisfaction (McNabb & Sepic, 1995). These studies also demonstrate that job characteristics such as decision latitude and control over challenging tasks (Cunningham et al., 2002) are related to readiness for change. As the studies illustrate, individual readiness for organizational change is shaped by various ­contextual factors in a given situation.

Furthermore, a few studies have shown that individual readiness for change is actually related to support for or participation in change implementation (e.g. Cunningham et al., 2002; Jones et al., 2005; Meyer et al., 2007; Weeks et al., 2004). For example, ­Cunningham et al. (2002) showed that individuals who felt more committed to change at the early stage of a change initiative participated in more activities at later stages than those who were less committed to change. Similarly, Jones et al. (2005) showed that individuals who ­reported higher levels of readiness for change at the pre-implementation stage ­contributed more to the success of change implementation at the implementation and post-implementation stages.

Some research studies have focused on the effects of personality on one’s attitude towards organizational change. These studies have reported mixed results regarding the influence of personality variables on attitudes toward organizational change. For example, some studies have suggested that personality variables such as locus of control, work ­motivation, self-esteem, and optimism are significantly related to attitudes towards organizational change (e.g. Chen & Wang, 2007; Elias, 2009; Judge et al., 1999; Wanberg & Banas, 2000), while others have indicated that these variables do not have a significant relationship with readiness for change (e.g. Devos et al., 2001; Wanous et al., 2000).

As has been explained, it is important to separate general attitudes from specific ones (Eagly & Chaiken, 1993; Fisher, 1980; Katz & Kahn, 1978). While the former may depend on personal needs and values, the latter are determined largely by one’s experience within the organizational context (Katz & Kahn, 1978; Lau & Woodman, 1995). Individual readiness for organizational change, as defined in this chapter, should be understood as an attitude about a specific change, and not as general attitudes towards change. It is directed at a specific change initiative and has been conceptualized as an attitude towards a specific change (Armenakis & Bedeian, 1999; Holt et al., 2007; Jansen, 2000). Therefore, negative attitudes toward particular organizational change—or low levels of readiness for change—are not simply the feelings that negative people bring into the organization. While personality may have an effect on attitudes towards change in general, its effect may become irrelevant in a specific change ­context, due to the decisive effects of the situational variables (Devos et al., 2001; Wanous et al., 2000). In sum, individual readiness for organizational change is not a personality-based predisposition. It is “shaped by experiences in the work context” (Johnson & ­O’Leary-Kelly, 2003, pp. 640–641) and, therefore, is likely to change as individuals’ experiences change.





16.4 Concluding Remarks


Researchers with a strategic management perspective regard organizational change as a process of implementing corporate strategy devised by organizational leaders and ­decision-makers (Child, 1972; Dunphy, 2000). On the other hand, those who take the organizational development (OD) perspective regard change as a series of intentional efforts to make differences in the organizational work setting in order to enhance individual development and improve organizational performance (Porras & Robertson, 1992). According to Beer & Nohria (2000a, 2000b), these two perspectives are the most fundamental in the organizational change literature and are based on totally different assumptions about the purpose of and means for change. Nevertheless, regardless of the perspective adopted, it cannot be denied that organizations today are required to change themselves incrementally, and in many cases in a fundamental manner.

As Porras & Robertson (1992) contended it is no exaggeration to say that “change in the individual organizational member’s behavior is at the core of organizational change” (p. 724). Organizations only change and act through their members, and successful change will persist over the long term only when individuals alter their on-the-job behaviors in appropriate ways (George & Jones, 2001; Porras & Robertson, 1992). Supporting this idea, many researchers have empirically demonstrated that individuals are not passive recipients of the organizational-level change but rather actors who actively interpret and respond to what is happening in their environments (e.g. Ford & Ford, 1995; Hall & Hord, 1987; Isabella, 1990). Furthermore, a few recent research studies (reviewed in this chapter) have shown that individuals’ attitudes towards an organizational change initiative influence their behavioral support for that change (e.g. Cunningham et al., 2002; Jones et al., 2005; Meyer et al., 2007; Weeks et al., 2004). Individuals’ attitudes towards organizational change have a real impact on change implementation and, therefore, are certainly critical for any organizational change’s success.

As has been noted, the concept of individual readiness for organizational change is found in influential works including those of Lewin (1997), Rogers (2003), and Hall & Hord (1987). Furthermore, the importance of individual readiness for organizational change has been stressed by most change models, ranging from Lewin’s three-step change model, which provides the basis for almost all the OD change models (Cummings & ­Worley, 2005), to Kotter’s change process model (1995, 1996), which exemplifies the strategic management approach to organizational change.





16.4.1 Implications for organizational change research and practice


As researchers with a social constructionist viewpoint contend, organizational change is a situation that interrupts normal patterns of organization (Ford et al., 2008). When faced with organizational change, individuals try to make sense of their new environment and to draw conclusions about its possible outcomes and the implications for them. In order to do this, they extract particular behaviors from leaders and communications specific to the organizational change from streams of ongoing events, interpret them, and act on their interpretation (Ford et al., 2008; Gioia et al., 1994). The research studies reviewed in this chapter show that individual readiness for organizational change is shaped by a range of situational variables. For example, the more individuals regard an organization as having the ability to accommodate changing situations and the more they trust their peers and leaders, the more likely they are to be ready for a change initiative. As individual readiness for organizational change is based on specific organizational experiences, it is likely to change over time as individuals’ experiences change. In this respect, the concept of individual readiness for change can be better conceptualized as states, rather than personality traits (Choi, 2011).

The use of the construct of individual readiness for organizational change gives us advantages over the common use of resistance to change. As discussed earlier, by using the term “resistance to change,” leaders and change agents commonly fail to notice the potentially positive intentions that can motivate negative responses to change (Piderit, 2000). On the other hand, the concept of individual readiness for organizational change assumes that ­individuals’ concerns over change are natural and that there must be reasons for them. ­Furthermore, it is also assumed that change can be more successful if the concerns of change recipients are considered. Therefore, the concept of individual readiness for organizational change helps us pay attention to the key components of such concerns—for example, individuals’ evaluation of management support for and the personal benefit of a specific change initiative—and to their situational causes. In this regard, individual readiness for organizational change is a more practical concept with which to understand individuals’ attitudes towards organizational change than is resistance to change (Choi & Ruona, 2011).

In addition, the use of the construct of individual readiness for organizational change makes a difference to the role of organizational change and development practitioners. The question of “how to foster readiness for change” can bring about different perspectives on the situation and produce different outcomes from the question of “how to ­overcome resistance to change” (Choi & Ruona, 2011). While the latter question assumes that the role of change agents is to act as monitors who react to signs of resistance, the former can enable change agents to take the role of coaches and champions for change, who proactively design environments conducive to individual readiness for change within organizations (Dent & Goldberg, 1999a; Ford & Ford, 2008; Jansen, 2000). With precise understanding of the concept of readiness for change, organizational change and ­development practitioners can accurately capture the meanings and information attached to employees’ attitudes towards organizational change and reveal problems with the change implementation. They will also be better able to guide the unfreezing phase (Lewin, 1997) and make more appropriate interventions for the organizational change implementation.





16.4.2 Suggestions for future research


Even though individual readiness for organizational change is an appealing construct, so far only a small number of empirical studies have dealt with this phenomenon (e.g. Cunningham et al., 2002; Eby et al., 2000; Hanpachern et al., 1998; Jones et al., 2005; Madsen et al., 2005; McNabb & Sepic, 1995; Weeks et al., 2004). Moreover, when assessing individual readiness for change, previous research studies have tended to focus only on a single aspect, such as one’s perception of personal benefit from the change (Jones et al., 2005) or one’s evaluation of the organization’s capacity for making successful changes (Weeks et al., 2004). As a result, the studies have failed to capture the multifaceted nature of individual readiness for organizational change. Considering the importance of the construct, we need more studies that investigate how to foster it in organizations and which are conducted with measures that effectively capture its nature (e.g. Holt et al., 2007).

In addition, future research needs to be conducted to determine the effects of individual readiness for organizational change. Even though some of the previous research studies have shown a positive association between individuals’ attitudes towards organizational change and their actual behavioral support for change initiatives (e.g. Herscovitch & Meyer, 2002; Jones et al., 2005; Kwahk & Lee, 2008; ­Meyer et al., 2007; Michaelis et al., 2009; Parish et al., 2008; Rubin et al., 2009; Shum et al., 2008; Stanley et al., 2005), more empirical work needs to be done to explain this link with confidence. Research in this line will eventually help organizational change and development researchers and practitioners find better ways to improve organizations’ ability to increase organizational members’ acceptance of or support for change ­initiatives.

17


Towards an Integration of Stage Theories of Planned Organizational Change


Janice M. Prochaska, James O. Prochaska, and Dustin Bailey





17.1 Introduction


The management of planned change has been reported as CEOs’ most central concern in managing their companies (Conference Board, 2010; Van de Ven et al., 1989). Scholars and consultants have jumped to provide prescriptions with little or no supporting evidence. Innovation requires more than creative capacities to invent new ideas; it requires managerial skills to transform good ideas into practice. The specific skills required to manage the process of innovation remain underdeveloped (Burke, 2004). A road map indicating how and why planned change unfolds and what paths are likely to lead to success or failure is vital in today’s rapidly-changing world. Micklethwait & Wooldridge (1996) sharply criticized management theory for being incapable of self-criticism, for having confusing terminology, for rarely rising above common sense, and for being faddish and bedeviled by contradictions that would not be allowed in more-rigorous disciplines.





17.2 Stage Theories of Change


Since the second half of the 20th century, a series of scholars and researchers in organizational-change theory have used the stage paradigm. Lewin (1951) was the first to develop core notions about the dynamics of the change process within organizations and put forth a stage model of planned change. Lewin theorized that in an organization, any given stable state is a quasistationary equilibrium that can be unfrozen, moved, and refrozen by a change agent. By providing information that things are no longer working, unfreezing can take place. As a result, an organization will become less committed to the current mode of operation. The unfreezing creates motivation and readiness to change. The issue is then reframed so that new perspectives, attitudes, and feelings are created and the organization is moved through cognitive redefinition. Then freezing or stabilization of the change occurs through integration into the social system of the organization. Here discomfort with the change needs to be reduced. Throughout the process of unfreezing, movement, and refreezing, Lewin describes driving forces that promote new directions or change and restraining forces that reinforce the status quo or support the organization’s resistance to change. The likelihood of success depends on the balance between the scope and complexity of the proposed goal and the strength of the change-producing forces (Lauffer, 1982).

Another leading model of stages of individual and organizational change is E.M. Rogers’ diffusion of innovation theory. Rogers (1971) wrote extensively on collective innovative decisions and found them to be considerably more complex than individual innovation. These collective decisions are to initiate a new idea in an organization, to adapt the new proposal to local conditions, to sanction the idea, and to support the change. Each of these behaviors may be carried out by different individuals in the organization.

Rogers concentrated more on the power-holders or influential persons who make the decisions and less on the new idea and the way it is adopted within an organization. The focus on power-holders or influential persons is consistent with Rogers’ diffusion work, which attempts to analyze the innovation decision-making process and to determine the roles played by various innovators, resisters, and leaders as the change process unfolds. Innovators are the inventors of the new practice technology, or, by extension, the people who pass the new idea through an organization. Resisters, or critics of the innovation, are the defenders of the system the way it is. Leaders are those within an organization who influence the methods used by others. By virtue of professional or personal prestige, they are seen as models of practice and are often recruited as champions of the planned change (Smale, 1994). These three groups of people play significant roles in generating group acceptance or rejection of an innovation.

Zaltman et al.’s (1973) theory of the innovation process is one of the best-known ­stage-based models at the level of the organization. It is subdivided into initiation and implementation, sequentially ordered in time, and linked with transaction routines to make adjustments between stages.

Knowledge of the initiation is the crucial first substage of the innovation. Thus before any innovation can take place or be adopted, potential adopters must be aware that the innovation exists and that there is an opportunity to utilize it in the organization. In the second substage, organizational members form attitudes towards the innovation along the dimensions of their openness to it and its perceived potential. Here it is important to develop some commitment on the part of organizational members to work for the innovation and to deal with some of the potential problems that might arise as its implementation is attempted. In the third substage, the information concerning the potential innovation is evaluated and a decision is made.

The second major stage of the innovation process is concerned with the actual utilization of the innovation by organizational members as they perform their tasks. In the initial implementation substage, the organization makes the first attempt to utilize the particular innovation, often on a trial basis. If the initial implementation is successful, there is a greater likelihood that the innovation will continue to be implemented and that the continued–sustained implementation substage will be entered. In this last substage the innovation is fully implemented and is considered a routine part of organizational life.

Table 17.1 Comparison of stages of the innovation process.

Lewin Zaltman, Duncan, Hollbeck Kotter

2. Formation of attitudes towards innovation substage

2. Continued-sustained implementation

2. Form guiding coalition

3. Create a vision

4. Communicate the vision

5. Empower others to act on the vision

6. Plan for short-term wins

7. Consolidate and improve on changes

8. Institutionalize new approaches





In contrast to Zaltman et al. (1973), March & Simon’s (1958) organizational-change theory focuses just on problem-solving and so describes the initiation phase of the innovation process but not the implementation phase. March and Simon see initiation occurring when a given program of activity no longer satisfies performance goals; organization performance is then perceived as unsatisfactory and the search is on for innovation.

In more recent years, Kotter (2007) has put forth the idea that leaders who successfully transform businesses do right things right and in the right order. Kotter’s (1995) eight steps or stages are outlined in Table 17.1, where they are compared to the stages of previously-mentioned theorists. The review provided here demonstrates that ­stage-modeling has a long history in the organizational-change literature.





17.3 Anti-stage Theories of Planned Organizational Change


Rather than viewing the organizational-change process as a series of discrete stages sequentially ordered in time and linked with transition routines to make adjustments between stages, Schroeder et al. (1989) believe the process to be fluid, without a fixed sequence of stages. Taking a grounded theory approach, Schroeder et al. analyzed eight historical innovations. They noted that six displayed a common pattern or sequence. While some of these six stages logically must precede others, they are not presented as a fixed sequence. Schroeder et al. worked to capture significant process patterns in the development of innovations. They recognized the untidy nature of the innovative process and likened the pattern to a fireworks display. Although some of the streams of activity are interdependent, many appear to be disjunctive and to occur independently of their streams of activity. There is a great deal of back-tracking and overlap as well. Stages are not seen, but instead there are tracks of activities involving ideas, people, transactions, context, and outcomes.

The process Schroeder and his colleagues observed includes: an initial shock to propel the innovation idea into being; proliferation of the original idea; setbacks and surprises along the way, which provide numerous opportunities for trial-and-error learning; a blending of old and new ideas as the innovation is implemented and diffused; ­restructuring of the organization; and the involvement of top management in the process.

Cohen et al.’s (1972) “garbage can” model follows the fluidity of Schroeder and his colleagues. They describe many stakeholders engaging and disengaging in the innovation process over time, as their needs for inclusion and identity dictate. Reorganization or change is described as a choice opportunity that collects an assortment of ­loosely-­connected problems, solutions, and participants, and which allows the organization to arrive at an interpretation of what it has become, what it is becoming, and what it is going to become. Organizations are viewed as collections of choices looking for problems, issues and ­feelings looking for decision situations in which they might be aired, solutions looking for issues to which they might be an answer, and decision-makers looking for work (Cohen et al., 1972).

Deming (1982) and Katzenbach & Smith (1993) provide management rather than stage guidance on how to convert innovations to implementation. Deming advocates for a different style of management that is focused on constantly and continually improving the quality of a product or a service and which recognizes the power of teamwork to do so. He calls for a complete shakeup of Western styles of management. According to ­Deming, management is obligated to improve continually through the use of statistical analysis and the breaking down of barriers between management and hourly workers. Everyone and every department must subscribe to constant improvement, and managers must be good role models (Walton, 1986).

Katzenbach & Smith (1993) believe that teams represent one of the best ways to support the broad-based changes necessary for high-performing organizations. Executives who want innovation should give top priority to the development of team performance. Katzenbach and Smith also see a link between teams, individual behavioral change, and high organizational performance. The same team dynamics that promote high performance also support learning and behavioral change. These authors argue that change is more effectively implemented at the team level than at either the larger organizational or the individual level. Because of these advantages, these authors suggest that the focus of change should be at the team level.





17.4 Transtheoretical Model


Over the past 35 years, the transtheoretical model of behavior change (TTM) has emerged as one of the leading integrative approaches to individual and organizational change. The process of change has been the central focus of the transtheoretical framework developed by Prochaska & DiClemente (1984). They propose interrelated dimensions that can adequately assess intentional modification of behavior. The stage of change is the central organizing construct of the model. It represents the temporal, motivational, and constancy aspects of change. The second dimension, processes of change, focuses on activities and events that create successful modification of behavior. The model dictates different intervention strategies for the different stages of change: precontemplation (not intending to take action within the next 6 months), contemplation (intending to take action within 6 months), preparation (intending to take action in the next 30 days and making small changes), action (actively engaging in a new behavior), and maintenance (sustaining the new behavior over time). Activation of the appropriate processes results in acceleration through these stages of change. Processes include such interventions as consciousness-raising, dramatic relief, reinforcement management, counter conditioning, helping relationships, and stimulus control. The model also defines a set of dynamic variables that includes decisional balance—the pros and cons of behavior change (Velicer et al., 1985). Decisional balance was originally based on the theoretical model of decision-making developed by Janis & Mann (1977). Decisional balance involves comparison of the perceived positive aspects (pros) and negative aspects (cons) of making a behavior change.

Comparing across studies of 12 different behaviors, Prochaska et al. (1994) discovered some remarkably consistent patterns across stages of change and pros and cons of changing. In precontemplation, the cons of changing were always higher than the pros. From precontemplation to contemplation, the pros always increased, but there was no consistent pattern in the cons. From contemplation to action, the cons always decreased, but there was no consistent pattern in the pros. These findings were replicated with 48 behaviors by Hall & Rossi (2008). It was discovered that from precontemplation to action, there was approximately 1 standard deviation peak increase in the pros of changing. From contemplation to action, there was approximately a .5 standard deviation decrease in the cons of changing. These basic patterns have implications for applied interventions. To produce progress with populations or organizations in the precontemplation stage, programs must increase employees’ perceptions of the pros of changing. To produce progress with populations or organizations in contemplation, programs must decrease employees’ perceptions of the cons of changing.

Systematic relationships between the stage and the pros and cons of change have been found across each organizational-change behavior and population we have examined, including: (1) readiness to participate in collaborative service delivery (university employees) (Levesque et al., 1999); (2) readiness for a merger or acquisition (food-brokerage-company employees); (3) readiness to participate in high-performance teams (university employees); (4) readiness for managed care (Prochaska, 2000); (5) readiness to purchase systems, applications, and products in data-processing (trade-conference attendees); (6) readiness to use a stage approach to organizational change (change-management consultants); (7) advancing female scientists (Prochaska et al., 2006); and (8) readiness for continuous quality improvement (Levesque et al., 2001). No other theory of organizational change has generated such consistent results across such a range of issues, populations, and organizations. As with 48 health behaviors in individuals, it was discovered that from precontemplation to action there was approximately 1 standard deviation peak increase in the pros of changing. From contemplation to action, there was approximately a .5 standard deviation decrease in the cons of changing.

Longitudinal studies of change have found decisional balance measures to be a powerful predictor of future change. Pros and cons predict whether or not employees classified as precontemplators progress to more advanced stages of change (e.g. Blissmer et al., 2010; Velicer et al., 1985). Organizational-change programs based on the TTM facilitate change through interactions with employees that are designed to increase the pros and decrease the cons of changing. Such interventions help employees and stakeholders understand the benefits of a new work behavior, and thus help create conditions more conducive to the change.

Stage effects involve the ability to predict performance over time depending on the stage at baseline. Over an 18-month period, those in precontemplation make the least successful changes; those in contemplation make significantly more progress; and those in the preparation stage make the most progress. Helping those in precontemplation progress to contemplation with a brief intervention should allow them to show the increased progress of contemplation over time. In fact, in one study those who progressed one stage in 1 month following intervention took twice as much effective action over the next 6 months (Prochaska et al., 1985). From a stage paradigm, it is a much more realistic immediate or short-term goal to help precontemplators to progress to contemplation than it is to use an action-paradigm goal of trying to move them all the way to maintenance quickly. An action goal for precontemplators early in intervention is likely to lead to low participation rates, low retention rates, and high resistance.

A fourth construct of the TTM is self-efficacy. According to Bandura (1977), self-efficacy is a belief in one’s ability to carry out a particular action, as distinct from one’s actual capability. Belief in the ability to carry out specific actions plays a large role in whether goals are achieved. Beliefs influence whether individuals initiate and persist in actions directed towards these goals (Bandura, 1982).

The TTM provides: a framework for understanding the mechanisms that are hypothesized to mediate intentional change (processes); a temporal ordering of sequences of events in changing an established pattern (stages); measures that are sensitive to the earliest signs of change (decisional balance and self-efficacy); a means of tailoring education and intervention approaches to the level of readiness for change.





17.5 The TTM and Organizational Change


Specialists are trained to see what is unique in their area of study. Integrationists are trained to find patterns common across specific areas of inquiry and practice. Eldredge & Gould (1972), for example, have suggested that there are common patterns of change across many levels of the universe, including cellular changes, evolution of life, individual development, organizational development, and development of the universe.

Van de Ven & Poole (1995), leaders in the study of organizational change, also point out the type of theoretical pluralism that has uncovered novel ways to explain some organizational change. There is now a diversity of theories and concepts, borrowed from different disciplines ranging from child development to evolutionary biology to teleology. Gioia & Pitre (1990) warn against compartmentalization of perspectives that produce isolated lines of research and encourage comparative analyses of change dynamics across different units of analysis.

In her seminal article in the Academy of Management Review, Gersick (1991) compared models of change and found that:

(1) There are important commonalities in how change occurs across such diverse domains as organizations, groups, individuals, species, and physical matter.

(2) Comparing research findings from disparate areas is beneficial because different facets of kindred processes may come into focus as the methodology and levels of analysis vary. Gersick states that “models from different fields have much to offer each other [beginning] with the premise that they reflect common processes” (p. 11).



Baranowski (1990) and Winett (1995) present excellent analyses of how TTM can be used at individual and institutional levels. These leading theorists are enthusiastic about the applicability of TTM to planned change in organizations and institutions. Baranowski, in his 1990 article, displays some of the types of interventions that can be applied at each stage across different levels, including the physical, social, and institutional environment, as well as the individual.

Paskett et al. (1992) state that the stage model of behavior change could be applied to the organizational level in terms of identifying readiness to change. Intervention programs could then be tailored to correspond to the stage of change. Abrams (1991) describes the workplace as a complex social system intermediate between individual and population levels of social structure. He applied TTM to the worksite and began to theoretically integrate individual and organizational interventions, and he states that TTM can be applied, iteratively, to any size of aggregate unit of social structure. Worksites are to communities what individuals are to worksites. A “systems” perspective simply adjusts the perspective and the theory to match the aggregate unit of analysis.

Armenakis et al. (1993) offer Bandura’s (1982) strategies for influencing individual cognitions as appropriate for creating readiness for organizational change. Bowen et al. (1997) expanded the TTM to study communities’ readiness to change. Using the TTM as a guide, they found that a community can be assigned a stage of readiness.

Rogers (1995) included “Prochaska’s Stages of Change” in his fourth edition of Diffusion in Innovations and compares TTM to his stages. Rogers put forth the notion of a hierarchy of communication effects, where an individual usually must pass from knowledge change to overt behavior change in a cumulative sequence of stages that are generally parallel to the TTM stages of change. TTM has distinct research advantages in that it can go beyond the case-study form of qualitative research carried out by Rogers; TTM can analyze the dynamic process and make it quantitative by first focusing on change, one stage at a time, and then integrating each of the stages into a dynamic process.

There are six steps to applying the TTM stage approach to the study of change in organizations:

(1) Identify and define target behavior changes—Review relevant documentation and conduct interviews with key informants to identify and define in very concrete terms the employee and organizational behaviors that need to change to accomplish the desired change objectives.

(2) Customize TTM measures—Develop tailored surveys that precisely match the target behavior changes identified. These surveys assess employees’ readiness to change (individual-level assessments) and what the organization is doing to facilitate change (organization-level assessments).

(3) Administer TTM measures—Distribute surveys to all employees who have been or will be asked to participate in the change effort. Encourage employee participation and ensure confidentiality. Assessments should take 30–40 minutes to complete.

(4) Data analysis and feedback—On the basis of the data, provide a detailed profile of employees’ readiness to change and recommend stage-matched individual-level and organization-level interventions that will maximize progress.

(5) Provide stage-matched individual- and organization-level interventions—Work with change leaders to deliver the individual- and organization-level interventions best suited to employees’ readiness to change target behaviors.

(6) Re-administer stage-of-change measures—Re-administer assessments 3–6 months later to chart progress (e.g. “How many employees have progressed at least one stage and now have progressed to the action stage?” “How do employees now perceive the pros and cons of the organization’s change-management initiative?”).



These steps were followed in a study to assess a university’s readiness for integrated service delivery and to guide the development of a stage-matched management program that would minimize resistance and maximize the likelihood of successful change. Thirty-one members of the university staff and administrators completed an assessment containing the stage and decisional-balance measures, as well as measures of behaviors representing the goals of integrated service delivery. Data provided preliminary evidence of the applicability of the model to integrated service delivery. For example, stage of change displayed characteristic relationships to behavior and the pros and cons of integrated service delivery. Based on the pattern of findings, a stage-matched change-management program was developed, tailored to the organization’s readiness to change (Levesque et al., 1999).

Berry et al. (2007) have criticized the organizational stages-of-change construct of the TTM. They collected data on organizational and individual stages of change from service providers, senior management, and board members at 17 provincial health authorities across three data-collection periods. Participants were asked for their organization’s and their individual readiness to promote tobacco reduction, physical activity, and healthy eating. The authors stated that the organizational stage-of-change construct was not valid because inconsistent relationships were found for service providers and there were no significant correlations for board respondents.

Such variability does not refute a model, but it can refute a measure that is too vague. A fundamental tenet of using the TTM is that one needs to be explicit regarding the behaviors one must carry out in order to be in action. The “promotion of health behaviors” can too easily be interpreted in different ways by providers, management, and the board. With high variability and poor correlation, the researchers concluded that the stages-of-change construct was not valid. However, we do not believe that these authors applied the TTM staging measure properly and therefore they were not able to generate valid assessments.

Lehman et al. (2002) developed a 115-item survey with 18 content domains to comprehensively assess an organization’s readiness for change. This survey focused on motivation and the personality attributes of program leaders and members of staff, institutional resources, and the organizational climate as a first step in understanding organizational factors related to implementing new technologies. The concern here is that the measure is generic, focusing on an organization’s general readiness to change, and is not specific to what technology is being asked to be accepted. Leaders and staff members can vary markedly depending on what is being asked, with different sets of barriers when different behavior changes are being sought. Because more-stable attributes are assessed, rather than readiness to carry out a new behavior, the instrument is likely to be better suited to understand why changes do not occur than how changes can occur.

The next steps towards an integration of stage theories of planned organizational change would be for scholars and theoreticians to develop a hierarchical model for combining the partial integrations presented in Table 17.1. Then scientists would be able to empirically test how well this conceptual integration can be supported by basic and applied research. Particularly promising is the integration of Rogers’ stages of diffusion of innovation with Prochaska’s stages of change, where stages of diffusion at the population level can be complemented and accelerated by stages of intentional change at the individual level.
