Part III


Organizational Development





19


A Critical Review of Organization Development


Bernard Burnes





19.1 Introduction


Organization development is the name given to the emerging applied behavioral science ­discipline that seeks to improve organizations through planned, systematic, long-range efforts focused on the organization’s culture and its human and social processes. The goals of organization development are to make the organization more effective, more viable, and better able to achieve both the goals of the organization as an entity and the goals of the individuals within the organization (French & Bell, 1973, p. xiv).



Organization development is a systemwide application and transfer of behavioral science knowledge to the planned development, improvement, and reinforcement of the strategies, structures, and processes that lead to organization effectiveness (Cummings & Worley, 2005, p. 1).

This chapter undertakes a critical review of OD by chronicling the major stages, disjunctures, and controversies in its history. Though there has been a tendency in recent years to write of “classical OD” and “new OD” (Marshak & Grant, 2008; Oswick, 2009), this tends to obscure its continuously-evolving nature. Throughout its history, OD has been driven by the twin forces of academic rigor and practical relevance. There has been much debate in recent years concerning the need to achieve both rigor and relevance in organizational theory and practice (Bartunek, 2007; Polzer et al., 2009). However, it was the founding father of OD, Kurt Lewin (1943/1944, p. 169), who first made the case for this in his famous dictum, “There is nothing so practical as a good theory,” which presaged the current debate by 5 decades (Gulati, 2007). As new theories, perspectives, and organizational needs have emerged, and as OD has sought to respond to these, sometimes its theoretical wing has been in the ascendancy, and sometimes its practitioner wing has come to the fore. Though this clash of rigor and relevance has made Lewin’s dictum difficult to achieve, it has provided the driving force that has propelled OD through successive periods of change.

Though the various manifestations of OD produced by this clash do not always fit into neatly-packaged time frames, a chronological perspective makes it easier to understand its development and allows it to be seen as part of a wider context. This chapter begins with the origins of OD in the 1940s and 1950s, and examines the contribution of Kurt Lewin and the growth of T-groups. In the 1960s, driven by the popularity of T-groups, OD expanded rapidly and established itself as the major approach to change in the USA; ­however, by the end of this period OD found itself under attack, and enthusiasm for T-groups began to decline. Though OD continued to expand in the 1970s and 1980s, the criticisms of it increased: in the 1970s, it was criticized for putting individual needs before organization priorities, and in the 1980s, an era of rapid organizational transformation, for being too slow, too incremental, and too participative. In response to this, in the 1990s and 2000s, there was a great deal of soul-searching in the OD community, which led to an intellectual renaissance. This created the potential for OD to reunite its academic and practitioner wings and rebuff its critics. The chapter closes by arguing that OD is the ­dominant and most widely-applicable approach to organizational change, but that there are significant issues which it must address if it is to achieve both rigor and relevance.





19.2 The 1940s and 1950s—Kurt Lewin, NTL,and the Origins of OD


It is not entirely clear where the name “organization development” came from. According to Ron Lippitt, the term was coined, independently but simultaneously, in 1959 by two different teams of National Training Laboratories Institute (NTL) members that were consulting in two different organizations: Herb Shepard and Robert Blake at Esso’s Bayway Refinery and Richard Beckhard and Douglas McGregor at General Mills. Richard Beckhard offers one of several explanations:

… we wanted to put a label on the program at General Mills … We clearly didn’t want to call it management development because it was total organization-wide, nor was it human ­relations training although there was a component of that in it. We didn’t want to call it ­organization improvement because that’s a static term, so we labeled the program “Organization Development,” meaning a system-wide change effort (French, 1982, p. 268).

Therefore, though OD is sometimes characterized as only addressing small-group problems in organizations (Dawson, 1994; Hatch, 1997; Kanter et al., 1992), from the outset it was conceived of as a dynamic and organization-wide approach to change.

Though there is some fuzziness about the origins of the name, there seems to be ­general agreement that the original core components of OD were: T-groups and the creation of the NTL; action research; and participative management (Cummings & ­Worley, 2001; French, 1982; French & Bell, 1999; Harrison, 1995). There is also general agreement that all of these have strong connections with the work of Kurt Lewin (Benne, 1976; Burnes, 2007; Dent, 2002; Freedman, 1999). As Schein (1988) stated:

There is little question that the intellectual father of contemporary theories of applied behavioral science, action research and planned change is Kurt Lewin. His seminal work … launched a whole generation of research in group dynamics and the implementation of change programs (p. 238).

Any critical review of OD must not only begin with Lewin’s contribution, but must also recognize that his work is the yardstick against which later forms of OD are measured. This is because Lewin provided both the core tools and approaches of OD, and its ­rationale and values (Bargal & Barr, 1992; Conner, 1977; Gold, 1999; Marrow, 1969). Lewin’s three major contributions to OD were:

Planned change, comprising four interrelated elements: field theory, group dynamics, action research, and the three-step model of change (Burnes, 2004b, 2007).

Showing how psychological theories and techniques developed and used in laboratory experiments to study group behavior could be applied to studying and changing group behavior in the real world (Dent, 2002; Highhouse, 2007).

A set of values based on openness and participation. Lewin believed that only through the spread of democratic values and participation throughout society could social conflict be effectively tackled, whether it took the form of war, racism, religious intolerance, or industrial unrest (Lewin, 1943b, 1948; Marrow, 1969). The Lewin et al. (1939) autocracy–democracy study showed that leaders who promoted democratic participation obtained far better outcomes than autocratic leaders. Consequently, if autocratic leaders wanted to improve the performance of their followers, they first had to change their own behavior.



Between the autocracy–democracy studies in 1939 and his death in 1947, Lewin demonstrated in a wide range of real-world situations how planned change and participative management could be successfully applied (Burnes, 2007; Marrow, 1969). In 1945, Lewin established the Research Center for Group Dynamics at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology. Its aim was to investigate all aspects of group behavior, especially the ­benefits of democratic participation. At the same time, he was also chief architect of the Commission on Community Interrelations (CCI). Founded and funded by the American Jewish Congress, its aim was the eradication of discrimination against all minority groups. In 1946, Lewin was asked by the Connecticut Interracial Commission to organize a training workshop for community leaders to enable them to deal more effectively with racial and religious prejudice (Marrow, 1969). Lewin saw this as an opportunity to put his democratic-humanist values into practice and also to conduct field research on group dynamics (Bradford, 1967).

The resultant workshop, which took place in New Britain, Connecticut, has become famous in the annals of behavioral science and can claim to have laid the foundations for OD (Burnes, 2007; Highhouse, 2007; Rogers, 1970). It was led by Kurt Lewin, Kenneth Benne, Leland Bradford, and Ronald Lippitt (French, 1982). Benne (1976, p. 28) described it as “a project in cooperative action research” that was based on Lewin’s previous research. What emerged from New Britain was both an approach to change—­T-groups—and an organization for promoting that approach—the NTL (Freedman, 1999). The creation of T-groups, where the “T” stands for “training,” has been described as one of the most important, but also the most contentious, social inventions of the 20th century (Back, 1972; Campbell & Dunnette, 1968; Harrison, 1995; Highhouse, 2002; ­Kaplan, 1986).

The original T-groups were unstructured, agendaless groups of strangers who, working with a trained facilitator, would learn from their own interactions and the evolving dynamics of the group (French, 1982). The original idea was to use such groups to explore the feelings and attitudes of the participants in order that they could understand and reflect on their behavior in the “here and now” and so improve their interpersonal skills in order to bring about change in their “back-home” situations (Bradford et al., 1964). T-groups emerged from the New Britain workshop when the group members, almost ­accidentally, started to provide feedback to each other on their attitudes and behaviors (Benne, 1964; French & Bell, 1990). This evolved into a method by which individuals could gain personal insight into themselves and how their behavior impacted on others (French, 1982). In essence, this was an extension of Lewin’s earlier work which showed that leaders needed to change their behavior before they could change other people’s. In passing, we can also note that it shows some remarkable similarities with the modern concept of emotional intelligence (Sosik & Megerian, 1999).

The T-group approach is often referred to as “sensitivity training,” because it sensitizes the participants to their own behavior. Also, because the approach originally brought together groups of strangers in a controlled environment outside their workplaces and communities, it is referred to as “laboratory training” (Cooke, 2011; Highhouse, 2007), hence the NTL’s name. After Lewin’s death, the NTL was founded and led by Benne, Bradford, and Lippitt, the surviving leaders of the New Britain workshop, and it was originally funded by the Office of Naval Research and the National Education Association (Cummings & Huse, 1989; French, 1982).

Highhouse (2002) maintains that the entire field of OD was founded and built by T-group trainers. However, this ignores the groundwork done before the development of T-groups by Lewin and his associates (Burnes, 2007; Cooke, 2011; Freedman, 1999). It also ignores the continuing work on action research and participative management undertaken at this time by the Research Center for Group Dynamics and the CCI, which were staffed by Lewin’s collaborators and students (Cartwright & Zander, 1953; ­Friedlander, 1976; Marrow, 1969).

Nevertheless, in the late 1940s and the 1950s it was the zealot-like commitment to the promotion of T-groups which led to the creation of the OD movement, and certainly most of those who became leading figures in the movement were involved in the development of T-groups (Freedman, 1999; French, 1982; Harrison, 1995; Highhouse, 2002, 2007; Kleiner, 1996). It has to be recognized that for most of this period, OD had been more a debate amongst academics—an “intellectual crusade”—than a business from which people might make a living (Greiner & Cummings, 2004). When the research grants ran out, the NTL began to move into executive education in order to fund itself and the nature of T-groups and the NTL began to change, with the generation and free exchange of ideas giving way to a more business-oriented mindset (Dent, 2002; ­Highhouse, 2002).





19.3 The 1960s—OD Comes to Maturity


Such was the drive of the T-group pioneers that by the early 1960s, OD was emerging as a major approach to organizational change in the USA and was influencing theory and practice elsewhere in the world (Beckhard, 1969; French & Bell, 1973). T-groups were spreading like wildfire, and the other two core components of OD, action research and participative management, were gaining much support (Cummings & Worley, 2001; French, 1982; Highhouse, 2002).

OD was taken up by major Fortune 500 companies, including Esso, Dow Chemicals, Union Carbide, and Proctor & Gamble, who wanted to change the style of their managers and renew their organizations (Bradford & Burke, 2004; Greiner & Cummings, 2004). In 1964, the OD Network was founded by a small group of enthusiasts, and within a decade it had 3000 members; in 1968, the American Society of Training and Development established an OD Division, and in 1971 the Academy of Management ­created a Division of Organization Development and Change (Bradford & Burke, 2004; Cummings & Worley, 2001).

The dramatic growth of OD in the 1960s has to be seen in the light of the major social upheavals in the USA and around the world. The rise of the antiauthoritarian counterculture, especially the civil-rights movement and the flower-power/peace movement, and the emergence of new-age religion all led to a questioning of the established order and established ways of doing things (Isserman & Kazin, 2007; Lattin, 2011). This was as true for organizational life as for other aspects of society. New, heretical ideas were abroad and their promoters:

… saw a truth that ran against … prevailing attitudes. They saw how, despite the power of corporate practice, something desperately desirable had been lost in everyday corporate life: a sense of the value of human relationships and community. They saw how, without that human spirit, corporations could not perform (Kleiner, 1966, p. X).

Consequently, OD saw itself not as a set of tools and techniques to make organizations more efficient, nor any longer as just a small cult evangelizing the merits of T-groups and driven by intellectuals from leading American universities. Instead it was broadening out “into a popular ‘movement’ intended to ‘democratize’ life in organizations” (Greiner & Cummings, 2004, p. 375). In their work, OD practitioners were addressing fundamental questions, such as:

Does man exist for the benefit of organizations such as industry, the state, or the market? Or, do organizations exist for the benefit of man? (Zand, 2010, p. 424)

How do I help create a better society? (Porras & Bradford, 2004, p. 393)

Porras said:

I think the basic belief at that time was that OD processes could really transform people, make them psychologically healthier … Then, through the use of these improved interpersonal skills, people in the organization would develop more powerful ways to solve problems together, increase their participation, share power and decision making, get things done more effectively and efficiently, and so on (Porras & Bradford, 2004, p. 394).

An indication of the impact of OD is that while in 1960 no MBA, management, or business degree program in the USA had a required course on organizational behavior and development, by 1970 all the major degree programs had one (Dent, 2002).

In the mid-1960s, OD began to embrace quality-of-working-life initiatives and strategic change (Cummings & Worley, 2001), but it was action research, participative management, and, above all, T-groups and the NTL that were at the forefront of the growth in OD (French & Bell, 1973). In their original form, the purpose of T-groups was to allow ­people to learn about themselves and in so doing to see how their action affected others in their “back-home” situations (French, 1982; Highhouse, 2007; Marrow, 1969). ­T-groups were built on Lewin’s (1946, 1947b) work on the social psychology of groups and, as such, were not about individual therapy. However, as the demand for T-groups grew in the 1950s, many of those recruited by the NTL as trainers came from a psychotherapy background rather than a social psychology one (Benne, 1964; Freedman, 1996; ­Highhouse, 2002). As a consequence, in the 1960s T-groups became more like ­psychotherapy sessions for their participants, and began to lose touch with the aim of changing the behavior of the groups to which they belonged and for which they had responsibility (Kaplan, 1986).

As Porras observed:

… in the early ’60s, groups [T-groups, sensitivity training groups, encounter groups] were being used primarily to transform people without much concern for the setting the group participant came from. The idea was to make everyone more interpersonally competent so that they could interact more effectively with each other in whatever situation they found themselves. At UCLA, they were running groups mainly for all types of people with only a relatively minor emphasis on people specifically in organizations (Porras & Bradford, 2004, p. 393).

By 1966, some 20 000 business men and women had passed through the NTL’s doors (Friendly, 1966). Such was the demand for T-groups that many T-group trainers, and even people who had only attended a few T-group sessions, began to develop their own ­products, such as in-company or “family” T-groups and Blake’s Managerial Grid (Bradford, 1974; Cummings & Huse, 1989; Dent, 2002; Harrison, 1995). This unregulated ­expansion of T-groups meant that, in many cases, therapy-like interventions were being conducted by people who had little or no training in the use of psychotherapy, and as a result T-groups began to be criticized for damaging the careers and psychological health of participants (Harrison, 1995; Highhouse, 2002; Kaplan, 1986). Even when the most experienced T-group trainers were involved, some organizations proved culturally unsuited to the approach, as a well-documented exercise with the US State Department showed (Argyris, 1967; Freedman, 1999; Marrow, 1974). Another telling criticism was that whatever individuals might learn about themselves in T-groups, it had very little positive effect on their performance at work (Campbell & Dunnette, 1968; Dunnette & Campbell, 1970; Kleiner, 1996; Odiorne, 1963). Consequently, by the end of the 1960s the influence of the NTL and the use of T-groups dramatically declined (Highhouse, 2002; Joure et al., 1971; Kleiner, 1996).

The use of action research and participative management also grew considerably in the 1960s, and as the influence of T-groups waned, these took a more dominant role in the OD movement (Cummings & Huse, 1989; French, 1982; French & Bell, 1999). Though there are a number of variants of action research (Cooke, 2011; Cummings & Worley, 2001; French & Bell, 1999; McArdle & Reason, 2008), it is Lewin’s (1946) participative, or cooperative, action-research model that is most used by OD practitioners (Chein et al., 1948; French, 1982). Though mostly used in the 1940s to tackle societal problems, such as religious and racial prejudice, Lewin also used it extensively in the Harwood Manufacturing Corporation, where it continued to be used for many years after his death (Burnes, 2007; Marrow, 1967, 1969). As Marrow 1972, the company’s president, commented, Harwood was the first real test of Lewin’s action-research approach to change:

We agreed that the emphasis was to be on action, but action as a function of research. Each step taken was to be studied. Continuous evaluation of all steps would be made as they ­followed one another. The rule would be: No research without action, no action without research (p. 90).

By the 1960s, action research was seen as:

… an emergent inquiry process in which applied behavioral science knowledge is integrated with existing organizational knowledge and applied to solve real organizational problems. It is simultaneously concerned with bringing about change in organizations, in developing self-help competencies in organizational members and in adding to scientific knowledge. Finally it is an evolving process that is undertaken in a spirit of collaboration and co-inquiry (Shani & Pasmore, 1985, p. 439).

It was based on the assumption that each organization is unique and that a change process has to be designed with that uniqueness in mind and adapted in the light of ongoing ­experience and emergent learning (Coghlan, 2011). It incorporated Lewin’s (1947b) belief that for change to be effective, it must take place at the group level and must be a participative and collaborative process of discussion, debate, and experiment which involves all of those concerned (Allport, 1948; Bargal et al., 1992; French & Bell, 1990). Lewin maintained that the understanding and learning produced by this process was more important for the individuals and groups concerned than any resulting change as such (Lewin, 1946). Though action research is often characterized as a diagnostic process (French & Bell, 1999), for Lewin it was much more about creating a dialogue amongst the participants that would allow them to reach a common understanding of the context in which their behaviors took place (Burnes, 2004b).

Though OD practitioners have developed a number of variants of action research, they all tend to stem from Lewin’s three-step model (unfreezing, moving, refreezing) (Bullock & Batten, 1985; Clark, 1972; Coghlan, 2011; Cummings & Huse, 1989; Cunningham, 1993; Foster, 1972; Frohman et al., 1976; Lippitt et al., 1958). Lewin laid particular stress on the need to refreeze the new behavior—make it self-sustaining (Lewin, 1947a). Such has been the impact of the three-step model that:

Scratch any account of creating and managing change and the idea that change is a three-stage process which necessarily begins with a process of unfreezing will not be far below the surface. Indeed, it has been said that the whole theory of change is reducible to this one idea of Kurt Lewin’s … (Hendry, 1996, p. 624).

The third core component of OD in the 1960s was participative management, or “group dynamics” as it was sometimes called (Bennis et al., 1969; Greiner & Cummings, 2004; Marrow, 1969). Even today, the concept that groups of workers should have a role in decision-making is a controversial one, yet it underpinned all aspects of OD from its outset (Cummings & Worley, 2001; Huczynski & Buchanan, 2001; Marrow, 1969, 1972b). Modern concepts of participative management began with the publication of Lewin’s study on the impact of different leadership styles on children’s behavior, which gave credibility to the concept (Burnes, 2011b; Lewin et al., 1939; Zimmerman, 1978). Lewin then went on to show that participative management could be effective in a wide range of social and organizational settings, and was the most effective way of managing groups and changing group behavior (Burnes, 2007; Lewin, 1943a, 1943b). The benefits claimed for participative management are that it leads to better decision-making, increases group cooperation, performance, and commitment, and reduces resistance to change (Bennis et al., 1969; Cartwright & Zander, 1953; Marrow et al., 1967).

Participative-management interventions grew rapidly in the 1950s and 1960s (French & Bell, 1999). Studies showed that most employees welcomed the opportunity to participate in decision-making, and that such initiatives led to higher productivity and more satisfied workers (Cartwright, 1951; Cartwright & Zander, 1953; Likert, 1967; Mosely, 1987; Seashore & Bower, 1970). The philosophy of participative management led to the development of tools and systems for analyzing and changing management styles. The main ones were as follows:

(1) Rensis Likert’s “Systems 1, 2, 3 and 4” with 1 being based on extreme authoritarian ­principles and 4 on the greatest degree of participation; (2) Robert Blake’s “Managerial Grid Scale,” 9 : 9 to 9 : 1, with 9 : 9 corresponding to equally strong concern for production and people and 9 : 1 a concern for production only; (3) Douglas McGregor’s “Theory X” for the authoritarian approach and “Theory Y” for the participative approach (Marrow, 1967, p. 31).



One of the most wide-ranging participative-management interventions in the 1960s was carried out by the Harwood Manufacturing Corporation (Marrow et al., 1967). Some 20 years after its first encounter with Lewin and participative management, Harwood was still committed to this approach. When it took over another company, it embarked on an extensive and successful 3-year OD effort to inculcate the Harwood culture and approach to management into the newly-acquired organization.

By the end of the 1960s, the OD movement had been transformed in terms of the ­number of practitioners and the range of products. Though the popularity of T-groups was in decline, the action research–participative management approaches and their underlying values appeared to be growing in strength, and OD dominated the field of organizational change and development (Dunphy, 2000; French & Bell, 1973; Greiner, 1967; Krell, 1981). Though small-scale, local OD initiatives were still the norm, a number of organization-wide OD interventions were emerging (French & Bell, 1999). These ­strategic-change initiatives were recognition that smaller-scale interventions were often hindered by ­divisional and company-wide structures and large-scale production and process tech­nologies (Burnes, 2009a). OD was also growing internationally. While the USA was the ­powerhouse for OD, similar developments were happening elsewhere (French, 1982): the UK’s Tavistock Institute’s sociotechnical-systems approach complemented the growing interest in the USA in job redesign and quality of working life (Davis, 1979; Guest, 1957; Herzberg, 1968; Trist et al., 1963), while the workplace-democracy movement in ­Scandinavia paralleled and informed the participative-management dimension of US OD (Bradbury et al., 2008; Pasmore, 2001).





19.4 The 1970s and 1980s—Red Flags and Rivals


After the heady days of the 1960s, the next 2 decades were to prove a much less comfortable time for OD. Flower power was dead, Western companies were facing increasing competition from the Japanese, the oil price rose dramatically, and inflation and recession were rife (Freedman, 1999; Johnson & Ouchi, 1974; Morgan, 1986; Sheldrake, 1996). Companies no longer wanted to experiment with novel ideas; they wanted value-for-money products that worked. Therefore, if the growing number of practitioners were to earn a living, OD had to unleash its entrepreneurial spirit and become a much more hard-headed, results-focused, commercial activity that catered to what companies wanted (Burnes, 2009a; Dent, 2002; Greiner & Cummings, 2004; Herzberg, 1968; Krell, 1981).

This pressure for OD to change found form in Greiner’s (1972) influential article “Red Flags in Organization Development,” in which he warned that:

… disturbing symptoms of the failure of current brands of OD have been appearing frequently in research studies, in anecdotal evidence by managers, and in the literature of organizations (p. 18).

Greiner (1972) went on to say that “I wish to raise six red warning flags by questioning six trends that may be preventing the very changes being sought” (p. 19):

Flag 1—Putting the individual before the organization.

Flag 2—Putting the informal before the formal organization.

Flag 3—Putting behavior before diagnosis.

Flag 4—Putting process before task.

Flag 5—Putting experts before the manager.

Flag 6—Putting the package before the situation.



In response to the pressure for change, there was a major shift of focus, from small-group behavioral interventions to a more system-wide perspective that embraced strategy and structure, work processes, organizational effectiveness, and the needs of all organizational stakeholders (Armenakis & Bedeian, 1999; Greiner & Cummings, 2004; Pasmore & Fagans, 1992; Sashkin & Burke, 1987; Woodman, 1989). Burnes (2009a) observed that these changes were characterized by four main developments:

An open-systems perspective, building on sociotechnical-systems theory, which viewed organizations in their totality and within their environments.

A change of focus from group norms and values to organizational culture.

A shift of emphasis from group learning to organizational learning.

Increasing focus on organizational transformation rather than just group change.



These developments, however, were not without their problems and critics. By the end of the 1970s, OD had assembled a widely-used range of tried-and-tested approaches, but these still tended to focus on the group level rather than on the wider organization (Beer & Walton, 1987; Krell, 1981). Organization-wide transformational approaches were seen as crucial to maintaining OD’s relevance. However, the more ambiguous and vague they were, the less well they were developed, understood, accepted, and supported (Cummings & Worley, 1997; French & Bell, 1995; Golembiewski, 1979; Porras & Robertson, 1987; Sashkin & Burke, 1987). In addition, the more that OD focused on large-scale change interventions, the less collaborative the change process became and the less able it was to promote its core humanist and democratic values (Burnes, 2009a). The dilemma for OD was that the more it attempted to provide what its customers and critics asked for, the less it appeared to be recognizably OD (Bradford & Burke, 2004; Greiner & Cummings, 2004; Worley & Feyerhern, 2003).

At issue was a fundamental question: Is change most effective when it is driven from the bottom of an organization or from the top (Conger, 2000)? In the 1950s and 1960s, inspired by the work of Lewin and the early proponents of OD, the loudest voices were in favor of bottom-up, collaborative change. However, by the 1980s, led by writers such as Tom Peters, the loudest voices were in favor of swift and decisive top-down change, often involving unpalatable actions such as closing factories and sacking large numbers of people.

Peters has been, arguably, the most influential management consultant of the last 30 years (Collins, 2008; Huczynski, 1993). He catapulted to international fame when he ­co-authored In Search of Excellence: Lessons from America’s Best-run Companies (Peters & Waterman, 1982). At a time when Western economies were on the rocks and Japanese companies appeared to be sweeping all before them, In Search of Excellence seemed to offer a way for Western companies to regain their competitiveness (Crainer, 1995).

Peters launched an attack on corporate America, which he saw as a collection of ­outmoded and uncompetitive dinosaurs, doomed to extinction unless they transformed themselves rapidly and irreversibly (Burnes, 2009a). He argued that this could not be achieved by continuing with past OD-type approaches to managing and changing organizations (Peters, 1989, 1993; Peters & Waterman, 1982). Many others took a similar view (Handy, 1984; Kanter, 1983, 1989; Pascale & Athos, 1982)—they were not opposed to empowerment, but believed its effectiveness was limited. Their attitude towards Lewin and OD is best summed up in Kanter et al.’s (1992) trenchant comment that:

Lewin’s model was a simple one, with organizational change involving three stages; unfreezing, changing and refreezing … This quaintly linear and static conception—the organization as an ice cube—is so wildly inappropriate that it is difficult to see why it has not only survived but prospered. … Suffice it to say here, first, that organizations are never frozen, much less refrozen, but are fluid entities with many “personalities.” Second, to the extent that there are stages, they overlap and interpenetrate one another in important ways (p. 10).

Slow, incremental, and participative change was now out of fashion; change had to be fast, furious, and often brutal. Top managers came to focus less on people-oriented values and more on “the bottom line and/or the price of stock … [consequently] some executives [developed] a ‘slash and burn’ mentality” (French & Bell, 1995, p. 351).

Therefore, from the early 1980s, a powerful consensus began to build against OD in general and Lewin’s work in particular, which led to the eclipse of OD, at least in the academic world. The attack on OD did not just come from writers such as Peters; OD also found itself under fire from new perspectives on organizations such as postmodernism, social constructionism, and processualism, and from theories drawn from the natural ­sciences, especially the punctuated-equilibrium model of change and complexity theory (Brown & Eisenhardt, 1997; Burnes, 2005, 2009a; Dawson, 1994; Hatch, 1997; ­Pettigrew, 1985, 1997; Wheatley, 1992). By the end of the 1980s, these different strands had coalesced under the umbrella of “emergent change” (Wilson, 1992). From the emergent perspective:

Organizational change is not a linear process or a one-off isolated event but is a continuous, open-ended, cumulative, and unpredictable process of experimentation and adaptation aimed at matching an organization’s resources and capabilities to the opportunities, constraints, and demands of a dynamic and uncertain environment.

Change is a multilevel, cross-organization process that unfolds in an iterative and messy fashion over a period of years and comprises a series of interlocking projects.

Change is not an analytical-rational process. Instead, key change decisions evolve over time and are the outcome of political and cultural processes in organizations.



For Weick (2000), the advantages of emergent change include:

… sensitivity to local contingencies; suitability for on-line real-time experimentation, learning, and sensemaking; comprehensibility and manageability; likelihood of satisfying needs for autonomy, control, and expression; proneness to swift implementation; resistance to ­unravelling; ability to exploit existing tacit knowledge; and tightened and shortened feedback loops from results to action (p. 227).

Emergent change became the first substantial approach to change to challenge the ­dominance of OD. Indeed, by the end of the 1980s, OD seemed to be in decline and clinical psychologists appeared to have ceded the field of change to sociologists (Bunker et al., 2004; Burnes, 2009a).

In the 1970s and 1980s, OD had become practitioner-dominated and lost its close link with academia. The entrepreneurial OD practitioners developed new approaches to change which attempted to address organizations’ need for large-scale, transformational change (Bunker et al., 2004; Greiner & Cummings, 2004). However, these approaches were not as successful as OD practitioners had hoped (Greiner & Cummings, 2004), and the evidence appeared to show that they were still more comfortable with smaller-scale interventions which promoted OD’s traditional democratic-humanist values (Hurley et al., 1992). Also, the new OD interventions that had appeared in this period were not supported by the level of research and theory development needed to provide rigor, credibility, and effectiveness. This was not surprising in a practitioner-dominated field (Golembiewski, 1979; Porras & Robertson, 1987; Robertson, et al., 1993; Sashkin & Burke, 1987).





19.5 The 1990s and the 2000s—OD Lost and Found?


For OD, the 1990s and 2000s saw three apparently separate developments. The first was that for much of this period, the OD community appeared to be embroiled in a turmoil of soul-searching, debate, and dispute over the nature and future of OD (Bennis, 2000; Burke, 1997; Dunphy, 2000; Farias & Johnson, 2000; Hornstein, 2001; Marshak, 1993; Nicholl, 1998a, 1998b; Worley & Feyerherm, 2003). Certainly, many commentators were asking if OD had lost its sense of direction or even still existed (Greiner & Cummings, 2004). It was also the case that many managers considered it “an out-of-date fad” (Porras & Bradford, 2004, p. 401). In looking back over the period, Korten et al. (2010, p. 395) summarized the key issues and debates as:

What is OD and does it have a strong theoretical base?

Are the values of OD strong and appropriate?

Does OD add value to organizations?

Are OD practitioners responsive enough to organizations’ needs?

Does OD have the balance right between “soft” people concerns and “hard” economic concerns?

Should more be done to “quality-assure” OD practitioners?



OD’s crisis of confidence was starkly illustrated in 2004 with the appearance of a special issue of the Journal of Applied Behavioral Science (40(4)), which is published by the NTL and could therefore be considered the “house journal” of the OD movement. It contained articles by leading figures in the movement with titles such as “Introduction: Is OD in Crisis?” (Bradford & Burke, 2004), “Wanted: OD More Alive than Dead!” (Greiner & Cummings, 2004), and “Ideas in Currency and OD Practice: Has the Well Gone Dry?” (Bunker et al., 2004). As Bradford & Burke (2004) stated in their “Introduction”:

We would suggest that there is no agreement about what OD is. The field lacks a central, agreed-upon theory of change—or even approach to change (p. 370).

The second and more promising development was that a new generation of scholars started to take a critical and surprisingly supportive interest in the work of Kurt Lewin. The received wisdom for much of the 1980s and 1990s was that Lewin’s work was at best outdated, and at worst had always been wrong (Dawson, 1994; Hatch, 1997; Kanter et al., 1992). However, in 1992 the Journal of Social Issues (48(2)) published a special issue to mark (belatedly) the centenary of Lewin’s birth in 1890. Not only did it revisit Lewin’s work and bring it to a new generation but it also argued that it was still highly relevant to the needs of organizations and society at large. Indeed, the guest editors, in introducing the special issue, stated that “we believe that psychology as a field has moved much closer to Lewin’s worldview than it was during his lifetime” (Bargal et al., 1992, p. 4). Subsequently, both Hendry (1996) and Schein (1996) produced articles defending Lewin’s much-criticized three-step model. Elrod & Tippett (2002) found support for Lewin’s model from unexpected quarters:

Models of the change process were identified in diverse and seemingly unrelated disciplines [such as bereavement theory, personal-transition theory, creative processes, cultural revolutions, and scientific revolutions]. Examination of most of these models revealed … most ­followed Lewin’s [1947b] three-phase model of change (p. 288).

Other writers produced similarly supportive publications demonstrating the continuing relevance of Lewin’s work (Boje et al., 2011; Burnes, 2004a, 2004b, 2007, 2009b, 2011; By & Burnes, 2011; Coghlan & Jacobs, 2005). Therefore, while many in the OD community were busy questioning its purpose and values, other scholars, often from outside of the community, were rehabilitating Lewin and OD.

The third development, or rather continuing development, was that OD did not stand still—it continued to expand across the globe (Cummings & Worley, 2009; French & Bell, 1999; Mozenter, 2002; Piotrowski & Armstrong, 2005; Rees, 2011; Wirtenberg et al., 2004). Perhaps more importantly, new approaches to OD, often incorporating newer ­perspectives on the nature of organizational life, such as social contructionism and complexity theories, continued to emerge and be well received (Bushe, 2011; Freedman, 1999; ­Oswick and Marshak, 2011). As Freedman (1999) noted, process consultation, though having been around for many years, became increasingly popular in the 1990s. Process consultation does not focus so much on a particular approach to change or a set of tools but rather is about the consultant establishing an effective helping relationship with the client organization (Schein, 1999). As Schein (1988) states, it is based on the “assumption that all organizational problems are fundamentally problems involving human interactions and processes” (p. 12). Central to process consultation is the notion that learning and change are interrelated—the client has to learn about themselves and their organization before they can change it. In order to assist in this, the consultant has to establish a dialogue which allows the client to explore their situation and learn more about it. This idea of dialogue as central to the change process also links process consultation with another form of OD that began to gain attention in the 1990s—dialogic OD (Dixon, 1998; Oswick, 2009). Some proponents of dialogic OD see it as a transformational process, whereas others see it as a way in which the various parties involved in the change process can gain insights into each other’s organizational realities (Barratt et al., 1995; Bushe & Marshak, 2009; Gergen, 1999). Where its proponents see it as distinct from classical “diagnostic” OD is in its interpretative nature; it draws on the idea that reality is socially constructed and socially negotiated, and that organizations are seen as meaning-making systems (Bushe & Marshak, 2009; Oswick & Marshak, 2011). However, Wooten & White (1999) and others also argue that the philosophy of Lewinian OD, with its emphasis on democracy and fairness, fits neatly with the egalitarian nature of postmodern organizations (see Bergquist, 1993; Daft, 1998). Also, as shown earlier when discussing action research, Lewin’s (1946) view was that gaining insights and understanding was more important than gathering data and testing solutions.

It may seem strange that OD, which in the 1980s and 1990s was under attack from social-constructionist/postmodern critics (see Hatch, 1997), should itself attempt to incorporate these perspectives (Oswick, 2009). A number of leading OD scholars see social constuctionism as a way of further developing OD and providing it with a more coherent theoretical underpinning (Cummings & Worley, 2009; Marshak & Grant, 2008; Van Nistelrooij & Sminia, 2010; Worley & Feyerherm, 2003). A related area in which supporters of OD have shown an interest is that of organizational storytelling (Rhodes, 2011), which has links with both dialogic OD and social constructionism (Bryant & Wolfram Cox, 2011). Storytelling is seen as enabling the exploration of the dialogic nature of organizations, in which the ­organization is seen as a “multiplicity of discourses” which reflect different perspectives on reality (Grant et al., 1998, p. 7). It also, of course, has much common ground with ­sense-making, which has also found favor among proponents of OD (Werkman, 2010).

One of the most prominent new approaches in OD is appreciative inquiry (AI) (­Cooperrider & Srivastva, 1987). As Bushe (2011) states, AI is based on dialogic OD and social-constructionist theory. It consists of the four-D approach: Discovery, Dream, Design, and Deliver/Destiny. Bushe (2011) describes AI as one of the first post-Lewinian OD methods, but, as will be discussed later, this may be something of an exaggeration.

Another area in which OD was seemingly incorporating the work of its critics was ­complexity theory. This is an umbrella term for a number of theories, ideas, and research programs derived from scientific disciplines such as meteorology, biology, physics, ­chemistry, and mathematics. A wide range of writers and practitioners have argued that organizations, like natural systems, are complex, nonlinear systems whose members can shape their present and future behavior through spontaneous self-organizing, which is underpinned by a set of simple order-generating rules (Arndt & Bigelow, 2000; Bechtold 1997; Black 2000; Fitzgerald 2002; Lewis 1994; MacIntosh & MacLean 2001; Stacey 2003; Wheatley 1992). Complexity theory is, therefore, being seen as a way of understanding and changing organizations (Burnes, 2005). Though many of its proponents have seen the complexity perspective as incompatible with OD, proponents of the latter have come to take a different view (Olson & Eoyang, 2001; Worley & Feyerherm, 2003).

OD has gone through a period in which new approaches have been developed that claim to enable dialogue with multiple stakeholders, engage multiple realities, and address the needs of whole systems rather than just parts of them. However, a number of writers question the extent to which these are really new approaches, as opposed to a continuation, extension, or development of earlier forms of OD such as action research, sociotechnical systems, strategic change, and open-systems planning (Oswick, 2009; Worley & ­Feyerherm, 2003). Consequently, they argue that these new approaches are not necessarily incompatible with older OD theories and practices (Burnes, 2004a; Oswick, 2009; Van Nistelrooij & Sminia, 2010). The main issue which separates the two is their supposedly differing views on the nature of reality. Lewin’s work and classical OD are seen as being based on a modernist, unitary view of reality, while dialogic OD is based on the multiple-reality ­perspective of the social constructionists and postmodernists (Bushe & Marshak, 2009). However, Van Nistelrooij & Sminia (2010) paint a very different picture of Lewin and the early proponents of OD: one which sees them as more in line with a social-­constructionist view of the world. As Burnes (2009a) noted, this stems from the Gestalt psychology that underpins Lewin’s work and which argues that individuals and groups see and experience the world from their own perspectives (see Lewin, 1942; Lewin & Grabbe, 1945; Köhler, 1967; Rock & Palmer, 1990). Similar linkages can be made with storytelling approaches to change, as Boje & Rosile (2010) have shown in using Lewin’s field theory to develop their approach to storytelling. Finally, as Burnes (2004a) maintains, there are strong links between Lewin’s formulation of organizations and that of ­complexity theorists.

Taken together, the three developments discussed in this section appear to represent an intellectual renaissance in OD. If OD in the 1970s and 1980s was dominated by practitioners, the last 2 decades have seen the baton passed back to academia. This has opened up the possibility that the varying perspectives and approaches to OD can be reconciled. However, as the final section of this chapter will argue, that is only likely to happen if ­practitioners are brought back into the OD fold.





19.6 Conclusion: OD in the 21st Century—a Time for Cautious Optimism


This review has shown that since its inception in the work of Kurt Lewin, OD has grown considerably. Looking at OD today and comparing it with the past, we do not see a period when “classical” OD existed and a period now in which “new” OD holds sway. Rather, we see a continuously-evolving body of theory and practice that is capable of incorporating and developing new ideas, perspectives, and approaches and of discarding old ones as circumstances change (Dent, 2002; Oswick, 2009; Rees, 2011). This continuous evolution is driven by the twin forces of academic rigor and practical relevance. Sometimes, as in the 1950s, 1990s, and 2000s, academics appear to be in the driving seat, as they seek to develop theoretically strong and rigorous approaches to change. At other times, as in the 1970s and 1980s, it is practitioners seeking to develop approaches relevant to the needs of their clients who take the lead. Occasionally, as in the 1960s, we can see both groups vying for supremacy. This can lead the many individuals, groups, and organizations which make up the field to explore dead ends, to take paths that are not compatible with OD’s values, and to create dichotomies between theory and practice. It can and does also lead to the incorporation of new insights and knowledge and to a recombination of rigor and ­relevance (Bunker et al., 2004).

In the second decade of the 21st century, something strange seems to be happening to OD. On the one hand, it appears to be going through a process of renewal as it incorporates new ideas (Bushe & Marshak, 2009; Oswick & Marshak, 2011). On the other hand, the work of Kurt Lewin seems to be coming back into fashion (Boje et al., 2011; Burnes, 2009b; Coghlan & Jacobs, 2005; Elrod & Tippett, 2002). Though these may seem to be contrary developments, it needs to be recognized that as the impact on OD of new ideas becomes more distinct, these ideas may prove to both be compatible with and link back to some of the themes in Lewin’s work. This is especially the case with the relationship ­between Lewin’s Gestalt-based field theory and social constructionism (Van Nistelrooij & Sminia, 2010). It is also the case that the main challenger to OD, emergent change, seems to have faded and that its successors, such as complexity theory, appear to be being ­incorporated into OD (Burnes, 2011a, 2011c). Finally, the various organizational and financial scandals of the last decade have highlighted the importance of ethics and values in organizational life, which gives the original values of OD a renewed relevance (Burnes, 2009b; By & Burnes, 2011).

Does this mean we can be more optimistic about the present state and future trajectory of OD? Yes, but—and it is a big but—there are serious issues which OD needs to address.





19.6.1 Rigor and relevance


Without a strong theoretical and methodological basis, practice is likely to be flawed and suboptimal, but without practical relevance, theory has little impact (Bartunek, 2007; Polzer et al., 2009). The separation of theory and practice did not exist in the early days of OD, when the influence of Kurt Lewin—The Practical Theorist—was still strong (Bunker et al., 2004; Marrow, 1969). However, as in other areas of organizational research and practice, in OD a gap opened up between those seeking a theoretically strong and rigorous OD and those pursuing a practical and relevant OD (Gulati, 2007; Hutton & Liefooghe, 2011; Robertson et al., 1993; Sashkin & Burke, 1987). Yet, as Bunker et al. (2004) argue, there are signs that both academics and practitioners are once again seeking to work together to promote rigor and relevance, but the gap cannot be bridged by a few scholar-practitioners or practitioner-scholars, important though these are (Bartunek, 2007, 2008). What is needed is a genuine commitment by both academics and practitioners to the development of mechanisms and organizations through which both can work together to restore Lewinian rigor and relevance to OD.





19.6.2 The limits of OD


Implicit, if not explicit, in much of the literature on OD is the assumption that it is or should be applicable to all types of organization and all types of change (Cummings 2005; French & Bell, 1999). Yet in each decade of its existence, OD has attempted to offer approaches to achieving forms of organization-wide, radical, and transformational change. In successive decades, evidence has been presented which questions the efficacy of such approaches (Bartunek, 2008; Dawson, 1994; Greiner, 1972; Greiner & Cummings, 2004; Hurley et al., 1992; Kanter et al., 1992; Weick & Quinn, 1999; Worren et al., 1999). Despite some successful large-scale change initiatives (Bunker & Alban, 2006), it does appear that OD is more effective, and practitioners more comfortable, in cases of small-scale, group-based, participative change intervention (Beer & Walton, 1987; Burnes, 2009a; Greiner & Cummings, 2004; Hurley et al., 1992; Krell, 1981; Ramos & Rees, 2008). Given OD’s participative–democratic orientation, this is what one might expect. Rather than assuming that OD must have an answer for all organizational ills and thus attempting to develop such answers, it might be better to start by addressing where the limits of OD lie and what types of intervention it is not suitable for.





19.6.3 Value alignment


From its outset, OD has had a distinctive set of values which influence both practice and practitioners (Burnes, 2007; Hornstein, 2001; Hubbell, 2004; Rees, 2011; Van Eynde et al., 1992). As Rees (2011) comments, these values help us to distinguish what is and what is not OD. Without such a clear set of values, almost anything could be labeled OD, including what French & Bell (1995, p. 351) referred to as the “slash and burn” approach to change. As various typologies of value show, not all change situations or organizations share OD’s values (Beck & Cowan, 1996; Graves, 1970; Johnson, 1995; Rokeach, 1973; van Marrewijk, 2004). Yet there is much support for the view that successful change is associated with value alignment between the approach to change and those who are experiencing the change (Allaire & Firsirotu, 1984; Bouckenooghe & Devos, 2007; Burnes & Jackson, 2011; Chonko et al., 2002; Diefenbach, 2007; Mrotek, 2001; Schwartz & Davis, 1981; Walinga, 2008). Though OD has paid much attention to resistance to and readiness for change (Dent & Goldberg, 1999; Walinga, 2008), the issue of value alignment appears to have been neglected, yet it is one which may have significant implications for the ­effectiveness of OD interventions (Burnes & Jackson, 2011).





19.6.4 Big questions


The origins of OD lie in Lewin’s passionate belief that only by resolving social conflict, whether it be religious, racial, marital, or industrial, can the human condition be improved. In the 1960s, OD continued to address fundamental questions about the nature of work and society (Porras & Bradford, 2004; Zand, 2010). Since then, as the two quotes at the beginning of this chapter show, OD has narrowed its ambitions to such an extent that it is in danger of sacrificing “the goals of the individuals within the organization” (French & Bell, 1973, p. xvi) in order to “enable organizations to be effective” (Cummings, 2005, p. 25). Yet there has never been a time when the big questions were more important. The world is running out of natural resources and food, global warming is threatening to destroy the planet, and organizations in large and important sections of our economies seem to have decided that ethics, morality, and laws do not apply to them (Burnes, 2009a; Dunphy et al., 2007). Though these developments raise questions for governments and individuals, it is organizations that have perhaps the greatest ability to address them (Dunphy & Griffiths, 1998). This will involve them making significant changes to their purpose and operations; OD, with its humanistic values and wide range of tools and techniques, is ideally placed not just to aid organizations to make such changes but also to lead the movement towards a more ethical and sustainable future. However, if it is to do so, OD needs to reengage with the big questions concerning work and society.

OD has survived and grown for over 60 years, and it does not appear to be ready to go out of business anytime soon. Therefore, there are more grounds for optimism than some in the OD community appear to acknowledge. However, it is optimism tinged with caution. As this section indicates, there are serious issues which OD needs to address. If it does successfully address these issues, it may achieve the ambitions of its founders and become not just an approach to changing organizations, but a way of making the world a better place to live in.

Schwartz, H. & Davis, S. (1981). Matching corporate culture and business strategy. Organizational Dynamics, 10, 30–48.

Shani, A.B. & Pasmore W.A. (1985). Organization inquiry: towards a new model of the action research process. In D.D. Warrick, editor. Contemporary Organization Development: Current Thinking and Applications. Glenview, IL: Scott Foresman and Company.

Sheldrake, J. (1996). Management Theory: From Taylorism to Japanization. London: International Thompson Business Press.

Sosik, J.J. & Megerian, L.E. (1999). Understanding leader emotional intelligence and performance: the role of self–other agreement in transformational leadership perceptions. Group and Organization Management, 24(3), 367–390.

Stacey, R.D. (2003). Strategic Management and Organisational Dynamics: The Challenge of Complexity. Harlow: FT/Prentice Hall.

Van Eynde, D.F., Church, A., Hurley, R.F., & Burke, W.W. (1992). What OD practitioners believe. Training and Development, 46(4), 41–44.

van Marrewijk, M. (2004). A values based approach to organization types: towards a coherent set of stakeholder oriented management tools. Journal of Business Ethics, 55, 147–158.

Van Nistelrooij, A. & Sminia, H. (2010). Organization development: what’s actually happening? Journal of Change Management, 10(4), 393–405.

Walinga, J. (2008). Toward a theory of change readiness: the roles of appraisal, focus, and perceived control. Journal of Applied Behavioral Science, 44(3), 315–347.

Weick, K.E. (2000) Emergent change as a universal in organisations. In M. Beer & N. Nohria, ­editors. Breaking the Code of Change. Boston, MA: Harvard Business School Press.

Weick, K.E. & Quinn, R.E. (1999). Organizational change and development. Annual Review of Psychology, 50, 361–386.

Werkman, R. (2010). Reinventing organization development: how a sensemaking perspective can enrich OD theories and interventions. Journal of Change Management, 10(4), 421–438.

Wheatley, M.J. (1992). Leadership and the New Science: Learning about Organization from an Orderly Universe. San Francisco: Berrett-Koehler.

Wilson, D.C. (1992) A Strategy of Change. London: Routledge.

Wirtenberg, J., Abrams, L., & Ott, C. (2004). Assessing the field of organization development. Journal of Applied Behavioral Science, 40(4), 465–479.

Woodman, R. (1989). Organization change and development: new arenas for inquiry and action. Journal of Management, 15, 205–228.

Wooten, K.C. & White, L.P. (1999). Linking OD’s philosophy with justice theory: postmodern implications. Journal of Organizational Change Management, 12(1), 7–20.

Worley, C.G. & Feyerherm, A.E. (2003). Reflections on the future of organisation development. Journal of Applied Behavioral Science, 39(1), 97–115.

Worren, N.A.M., Ruddle, K., & Moore, K. (1999). From organization development to change management: the emergence of a new profession. Journal of Applied Behavioral Science, 35(3), 273–285.

Zand, D.E. (2010). An OD Odyssey: in search of inward light. Journal of Applied Behavioral Science, 46(4), 424–435.

Zimmerman, K.D. (1978). Participative management: a re-examination of the classics. Academy of Management Review, 3, 896–901.





20


The Application of Systems Theory to Organizational Diagnosis


Arthur M. Freedman





20.1 Introduction


When planning for change, decision-makers determine what to change in order to achieve their selected change objectives. These decisions depend upon the application of implicit or explicit mental models of organizations as complex systems. These models describe the systemic variables that decision-makers believe account for the causes of perceived gaps between an organization’s current and desired states. Much of the time, these mental models are implicit and are composed of leaders’ local or indigenous theory. That is, the models reflect the decision-makers’ beliefs, assumptions, and opinions. Further, implicit models are not usually accessible for explicit testing for either validity or ­comprehensiveness. However, there are explicit mental models of organizational systems that are accessible and can be tested. A number of these systems models are presented in this chapter—in historical order—along with commentaries about their contributions, advantages, and ­disadvantages. The intent is to provide decision-makers with sufficient information about their organizational systems to enable them to make informed decisions about where to look to: localize indicators of problems; identify root causes and sources; specify the involved parties that might contribute to problems and will be affected by executing plans for change; determine where to intervene in order to correct or prevent problems; and clarify the probable consequences of taking action. Further, by exploring these mental models of organizational systems, leaders and consultants can make informed decisions about which model is best suited to guide their planned change efforts. They can also use some or all of these models to make their own implicit, indigenous models explicit or to challenge the comprehensiveness of their local theories. Alternatively, leaders and ­consultants may choose to select elements from various models in order to create their own, personalized model.





20.2 Readiness for Change: Dissatisfaction with the Status Quo


The impetus for any organizational change is dissatisfaction with some aspect of a system (Freedman & Jules, 2006). Such dissatisfaction might focus on and emanate from any level or any part of the system. Those who are closest to and most familiar with the symptoms or causes of the dissatisfaction are most likely to express doubts, concerns, or reservations about the current state or condition. Dissatisfaction with the status quo emerges when people become aware that prevailing conditions can and should be improved. There are three general conditions that provoke the dissatisfaction: (1) something—machinery, a system, a process—is broken or underperforms and must be repaired or replaced; (2) an opportunity for significant improvement becomes apparent—a new market for existing products or services opens up, an acquisition target becomes available, a new time-saving or quality-enhancing technology is developed—and the organization does not seem to be exploiting it; or (3) something that stimulates dissatisfaction proves to be impervious to efforts to find or create an enduring solution—performance-management systems that overemphasize the contributions of either the individual or the team; the centralization or decentralization of operational or functional microsystems—and is recognized as a dilemma or polarity (Johnson, 1996) that must be continuously monitored and managed.

Given the perceived need to change, the first step in developing a viable plan for systemic change is an organizational diagnosis. Ideally, the diagnosis should locate the multiple critical systemic variables that contribute to dysfunctional or suboptimal performance. The diagnosis should also guide the choice of interventions that are most likely to address these critical root causes, such that they result in improved organizational performance.





20.3 The Scientific Method: An Approach to Organizational Diagnosis


All leaders and their consultants selectively focus on what they believe are the critical variables that affect their organization’s performance. Their beliefs are often based on some local or indigenous mental model of how organizational systems work, rather than one that is constructed on the basis of theory and/or extensive experience. These are mental models that are, too often, implicit and therefore not easily amenable to testing for validity or comprehensiveness. Decision-makers generally use these models intuitively to collect what they believe is pertinent information from samples of organizational members. The samples are frequently limited, as they often consist of trusted executives and/or relevant subject-matter experts (SMEs), rather than cross-sectional samples of all organizational members from all hierarchical levels and microsystems. The information is then used, intuitively, to decide what is dysfunctional, what caused the dysfunction, what the change objective(s) should be, what strategy to apply to change systems, and what set of action steps should be developed to actually implement the changes. When making these decisions, leaders are likely to apply their unrecognized biases, assumptions, and beliefs, which overemphasize some variables and overlook or minimize others. Decision-makers may also assume, simplistically, that this approach will enable them to discover a single cause for their system’s challenges, rather than have to deal with the greater probability that multiple factors are causing it. This way, leaders may avoid dealing with complexity—at least in the early phases of planning and implementing systemic change—but will also generally make faulty plans and experience disappointing results.





20.3.1 Three ways of knowing and understanding organizations


The approach just described corresponds to what Larry Starr (2012), Director, Center for Organizational Dynamics, University of Pennsylvania, believes is the first of three interacting frames for knowing and understanding organizational systems. Organizational leaders, their consultants, and the executive management team (or the leader’s “kitchen cabinet”) generate hypotheses about the nature and probable causes of evident sources of dissatisfaction. Discussions are held with the senior managers of the microsystems that are most closely involved with the apparent problem. The resulting personal narratives that correspond most closely to the original hypotheses are given considerably more weight than information that does not seem to fit. Decisions about goals, strategies, and plans are based on the refined (or reduced) results of the discussions. Starr (2012) calls this a heuristic and personal narrative approach for problem-solving that produces a recommended solution. He believes that this approach is based on the personal experiences of selected respondents and “is powerful and useful, but it is limited because of inadequate samples or data” (Starr, 2012). The process produces an incomplete picture of the problem and its context. It is vulnerable to both researcher and respondent bias. It reveals primarily what their implicit, indigenous, heuristic mental model claims is important.

Starr’s second frame is analysis and problem-solving. This “is the world of science, research and evidenced-based applications—also useful but also limited because it only fixes what has been identified as broken” (Starr, 2012).

Technical experts or techsperts (Freedman & Zackrison, 2001) typically apply their implicit idiosyncratic versions of the scientific method on the basis of two related assumptions. The first is that a complex system1 should be broken down into its individual components. This enables the techspert or technocrat to analyze each component as an independent entity. The second assumption is that the techspert or technocrat can then reconstitute and comprehend the total system by linking the components in a linear fashion. This approach is thought to lead to the discovery of a single root cause that can be corrected. These are reductionist assumptions that direct the technocrat and techspert to restrict their focus to the internal components of the subsystem that seems to contain the focal problem.

While these assumptions appear to be reasonable, a more realistic description of a system is that it is “characterized by the interactions of its components and the nonlinearity of those interactions” (Walonick, 1993). The major weakness of the reductionist perspective is that apparent sources of dissatisfaction may be symptoms—like the tip of an iceberg—of a multiplicity of contributing root causes. Organizational mental models that are too specialized and too narrow in scope run the risk of misleading leaders, as they may not look deeply or broadly enough to identify the root causes of perceived dissatisfaction. Unintentionally, they may satisfy themselves with premature diagnoses and efforts to provide short-term symptomatic relief.

Decision-makers, particularly technocratic leaders and various kinds of SME and techspert, typically employ some variation of the scientific method (e.g. Alhazen, 1962; Galileo Galilei, 1974) to identify their targets or goals and action options. Starr, a protege of Russell Ackoff, recently said of this analytic–diagnostic perspective:

Russ used to remind me that “analyze” is a Greek word meaning, break into small parts which is what is done to problems most of the time and which leads to a search for root causes. But some problems cannot be deconstructed and do not have any root causes which is why systems thinking is important. This approach while focusing on interactions/interdependencies looks outside rather than within the problem to the containing system for solutions. He would tell me, if you are seeking root causes or trying to solve a problem from within the system holding the obvious problem, you are not thinking systemically. A “mess” or “wicked problem” cannot be understood or adequately addressed with analysis.

Russ used to argue that what makes human systems so “immune” to analysis and research approaches is that analysis assumes that all the parts will do what they are designed to do. But human systems are made of parts—people and groups—that have their own purposes [and interests that] are interdependent with systems outside each stated problem and which change. This means that only methods that capture these interests and values will be effective. Experimental designs rarely do this (Starr, 2012).

Hackman (2011) illustrated the analytic–diagnostic or analysis and problem-solving approach. He observed that many organizations employ a team-based problem-solving approach that he calls co-acting teams. In this apparently efficient process, the team is composed of representative members of relevant microsystems. It identifies a member—often the most senior person—to serve as its leader or coordinator. It analyzes and deconstructs the problem into its component parts. Team members identify the microsystems whose work either contributes to the problem or is affected by it. They then engage relevant microsystem representatives—usually the microsystems’ senior managers—who are believed to know the most about each component of the problem and obtain pertinent information from them about its cause and consequences. The team members then return to the team and pool the information they have gathered. Typically, the coordinator alone reconstitutes the problem by organizing the collected information in a linear manner using their indigenous mental model. This approach is not really team-based. It is more similar to the manner in which managers solve problems with the assistance of their subordinates. It is likely to miss the context and history of the problem and the critical relationships among the interdependent microsystems that contribute to and/or are affected by the focal problem. This occurs because co-acting team members generally find only what they are prepared to see.

Starr says:

I find most people think about systems, but do not think systemically … People use language about systems or refer to holism but they still try to deconstruct or discuss the importance of parts. In a system one cannot easily optimize any part because this leads to suboptimization of other parts … Instead it is more productive to redesign based on overall system requirements (Starr, 2012).

Starr’s third frame is systemic thinking for design and problem-dissolving:

These methods do not require research evidence because the approach creates what is needed or desired via [the creation of] a new set of conditions (the redesign) where the problem cannot exist [the problem is essentially “dissolved” when the new organizational design is ­created and installed]. Design thinking is the methodology of what to do when confronted with a systems problem (Starr, 2012).

Organizations benefit when their leaders think and act systemically. Systems-oriented leaders consider organizational changes in the context of the total system and its interactions with its environment, rather than merely focusing on the primary element that attracts critical attention. By thinking and acting systemically, leaders focus their attention on the interactions among the interdependent parts and levels of their organizations. Leaders who think in terms of systems are more likely to set realistic, comprehensive goals and establish viable strategies and plans for change. Practitioners and theorists such as Ackoff & Emery (1972), Ackoff (1977, 1981), Senge (1990), Senge et al. (1994), Jackson (2004), and Gharajedaghi (2011), among others, offer useful approaches to teaching leaders to comprehend their systems’ dynamics by mapping inter- and intra­systemic interactions that identify what is working well and what is dysfunctional.

Systemic thinking for design and problem-dissolving involves two other related diagnostic perspectives: holistic or systemic and functionalistic or strategic. The holistic or systemic perspective examines the total system in the context of its environment as a complete functioning unit, rather than as an aggregation of parts and elements. The systemic orientation enables diagnosticians to determine where to look to discover what is working well and what can be fixed or improved. Effective leaders use their systems orientation to look beyond the primary organizational entities that are affected by and affect plans for change. Recognizing that a change in one part or level of a system will perturb all other parts and levels, they anticipate that the implementation of their plans for systems change will affect secondary sites and that these, in turn, will impact tertiary parties located in all parts and levels of their organizations, including their external stakeholders. Planners and implementers can then anticipate and actively scan for where and how involved parties’ probable responses will affect the goals and plans for organizational change.

The functionalist or strategic perspective considers the contributions made by each microsystem towards realizing the purposes and strategic direction of its interdependent microsystems as well as of its larger macrosystem. A strategic orientation focuses on the extent to which the moment-to-moment tactical decisions made and actions taken by leaders at all hierarchical levels contribute to the achievement of their systems’ strategic goals.

Each of these perspectives (see Table 20.1) yields different yet useful diagnostic information. Separately, each elicits incomplete information. Organization development and change (OD&C) practitioners and consulting organizational psychologists ideally use all perspectives in their systems-diagnostic efforts. An integrated scientific–strategic–systemic approach also enables organizational designers to construct or redesign the organization’s human and physical architecture to aid members in operating effectively (e.g. Galbraith, 2002; Herman, 2002; Mackenzie, 1986; Mohrman et al., 1995; Nadler et al., 1992).

Using open sociotechnical systems (STS) models2 that include an integrated scientific–strategic–systemic orientation, planners and implementers of organizational change can conduct a series of comprehensive organizational diagnoses before implementing complex systems-change initiatives to identify critical internal and external transactions between interdependent organizational subsystems that affect or are affected by the planned change. During implementation, an STS diagnostic approach can be used to monitor progress and identify the occurrence of predictable surprises (Bazerman & Watkins, 2008; Freedman, 1997) and unanticipated consequences. Thus, leaders can adjust and modify their goals, milestones, strategies, and action plans to make them increasingly realistic and to prepare their organizations to deal with the most likely unintended consequences of planned-change initiatives, as well as inevitable predictable surprises. They can also identify how planned-change initiatives affect and are affected by interdependent functional and operational microsystems. Thus, secondary and tertiary changes in related microsystems can be contemplated to ensure alignment and support the primary change.

Table 20.1 Three approaches to applying the scientific method.

Approach Activity Advantages and disadvantages

Heuristic and personal-narrative approach to problem-solving—reductionist Generate hypotheses about causes of problem. Collect information from senior managers. Match information to hypotheses. Focus on data that fit; ignore or minimize significance of data that do not fit the hypotheses. Relatively quick. No verification of accuracy or comprehensiveness of managers’ information.

Analysis and problem-solving Break the problem into parts; analyze the parts; reassemble the parts. Identify actions needed to address revealed problem and its apparent root cause. Efficient, reasonable approach. Focus on symptomatic relief; apparent sources of systemic problems are often only symptoms of various root causes that are not addressed. Implementation problems are not considered in detail.

Holistic–systemic and functionalist–strategic Examine total system in context of its environment as a complete functioning unit; implementation of plans for systems change will affect secondary sites and in turn tertiary parties located in all parts and levels of an organization. Consider contributions made by each microsystem to realizing the purposes and strategic direction of its related interdependent microsystems and of the macrosystem. Expensive and time-consuming but realistic and comprehensive. Planners and implementers can anticipate and actively ascertain where and how involved parties’ probable responses will affect the goals and plans for organizational change. Focuses on the extent to which the moment-to-moment tactical decisions made and actions taken by leaders at all hierarchical levels contribute to the achievement of their systems’ strategic goals.





20.4 A Brief History of Systems Theory


Systems theory has a rich and valuable history. A review of the major STS models is presented in this section in some detail. Each theorist contributed not only a model but also their principles for thinking about systemic dynamics. Each built upon their predecessors and offered system leaders, OD&C practitioners, and consulting organizational psychologists useful ideas to consider when identifying targets for change, choosing interventions, and redesigning organizational systems.





20.4.1 1928, 1950, 1968: Ludwig von Bertalanffy’s general system theory


It is generally agreed that the Austrian biologist Ludwig von Bertalanffy established the foundations for what he called general system theory or GST (1950, 1968). David Walonick (1993) says that von Bertalanffy originally proposed his GST in 1928. von Bertalanffy believed that the primary task of any system is to survive; that is, systems exist to: provide their members with the means to access food, water, air, clothing, and shelter; provide value to stakeholders and constituents; and produce a profit or surplus.

von Bertalanffy thought of systems as perceived wholes—that is, systems may consist of cross-functional teams or functional work units, functional or operational departments or divisions, product or regional groups, total organizations, suprasystems such as joint ventures that are composed of several organizations, communities, cities, states, total societies, and the like—whose component elements are interdependent, affect one another over time, and, ideally, contribute to the realization of a common systemic purpose. Repetitive cycles of events reveal the functional structure of a system—that is, how the system as a whole actually operates. Thus, a basic method for identifying organizational structures is to follow the energy and the work as they pass along the chain of interdependent events and activities—the value chain, delivery chain, or supply chain.
