von Bertalanffy (1968) believed that systems have five fundamental elements: a semipermeable boundary that surrounds and defines them; inputs; throughputs or transformational mechanisms; outputs; and feedback loops (see Figure 20.1).

Figure 20.1 The five essential elements of an open social system (von Bertalanffy, 1950). von Bertalanffy, L. (1950). The theory of open systems in physics and biology. Science, 3, 23–29.



The semipermeable boundary acts as a filter that allows some inputs and exports to pass into and out of the system and denies passage to others. Boundaries separate what is inside the organizational system from what is outside. The prevailing beliefs, values, histories, and mental models of the system’s members and, especially, its leaders determine the specific selective nature of the filter. Thus, the quality and nature of the boundary is a social construction (Gergen, 1999; Gergen & Gergen, 2003).

Inputs consist of the fundamental resources that the system believes it needs to produce its outputs. The organization’s external environment contains the sources of these resources (Rice, 1963). They include: qualified or trainable potential employees; information about customers’ needs and preferences; political, economic, regulatory, and competitive activity; raw material or components; capital for developmental or operational expenses; technology; advice and assistance from consultants; and the like. Changes in the environment and the reliability, quantity, quality, and costs of inputs force adaptive changes within the organizational system.

Outputs consist primarily of the goods and services produced by the system that are desired and valued by customers, and secondarily of such exports as salaries and wages that employees spend (in part or whole) to maintain their lifestyle while contributing to their local economies, banks, insurance companies, and utility companies. Outputs also include: dividends to stockholders; corporate in-kind contributions to local communities; waste products from the system’s transformational processes; reports to regulatory agencies; and accumulated knowledge about the system’s business operations. Changes in the environment—such as customers’ preferences or the emergence of competitors—require organizations to change their outputs.

Throughputs or transformational mechanisms consist of sequential or parallel and interdependent elements that contribute to the conversion of inputs (e.g. materials and components) into outputs (e.g. goods and services). These elements constitute the organization’s production line (or value chain, supply chain, or delivery chain), which adds incremental value to the production of outputs. It also includes all ancillary functions that do not directly contribute to production but support those who perform production functions—such as human-resource management. Changes in throughput elements—new technological innovations—require the organization to adapt and incorporate these changes into its transformational process and reestablish alignment among elements.

Feedback loops are the means by which organizational members can determine the extent to which the system, each of its subsystems, the interdependent interactions between subsystems, and the input–output transactions between the organization and involved supplier and consumer stakeholder groups in the external environment are operating as needed and as expected.

Feedback can be used to help organizational members maintain an existing equilibrium, restore a disrupted equilibrium, or assess progress towards a new equilibrium (von Bertalanffy, 1968). Feedback loops serve as the means through which critical information is provided to leaders and managers in order to enable them to conduct analyses of variance and to locate and adjust such elements as employee deployment, structures, processes, practices, transactions, relationships, and procedures, as needed.

von Bertalanffy believed that the GST applies to all living systems, the physical sciences, and all kinds and types of organization. He, Churchman (1968), and Ackoff & Trist (1974) demonstrated that open systems theory can be applied to planning, changing, and managing social systems.





20.4.2 1948: Norbert Weiner


Norbert Weiner (1948) introduced the term cybernetics to describe the science of control and communications. He differentiated between negative and positive feedback and how they influence purposive behavior that is directed towards goal attainment. Negative feedback refers to information about the divergence of actions from a goal. This guides the planning and taking of corrective action. Positive feedback is information that amplifies behavioral deviations and identifies situations where parts of a system operate out of alignment with the system’s goals. Communications is essential in exchanging information about both positive and negative feedback that is necessary to controlling the actions of machines and people.





20.4.3 1964: Harold J. Leavitt’s organizational systems model


Harold J. Leavitt (1964) was the first organizational theorist to create a diagrammatic model of an organizational system (Figure 20.2). However, he described a closed system that omitted the external environment, boundaries, inputs, outputs, feedback, and the flow of information and work from inputs to transformational mechanisms to outputs. He identified the key elements of his mental model as task, structure, technology, and people. These four categories and their interactions enabled practitioners to organize the raw data they collected during diagnostic activities into actionable categories. While breaking new ground, Leavitt’s model was far too simplistic to capture the complexities of modern organizations.

Figure 20.2 Harold J. Leavitt’s Sociotechnical System Model (1964).

Leavitt, H.J. (1964). “Applied organizational change in industry: Structural, technical, and human approaches.” In W.W. Cooper (Ed.), New perspectives in organization research. New York: John Wiley.



Task is the purpose of the organization in terms of the production of goods and services, including all further subtasks necessary for the accomplishment of the primary task. Structure includes all issues of communication, hierarchy, and work process. Technology includes all the technical instruments and procedures for task fulfillment. The people category includes all those who participate in the operations of the system.

Leavitt placed many incompatible systemic elements that should have been differentiated in each of the four categories. Other theorists have tried to correct this error (see Section 20.4.7). A critical element of Leavitt’s model is that all of the elements are connected by reciprocal transactions. Thus, whatever occurred or changed in one element would have an impact on all other elements. This interactivity is a fundamental characteristic of open social systems.





20.4.4 1963–1997: Eric Trist and the Tavistock Group’s STS theory


Eric Trist and his colleagues at the Tavistock Institute in the UK (e.g. Ackoff, 1981; Ackoff & Trist, 1972; Colman & Bexton, 1975; Colman & Geller, 1985; Cummings & Srivastva, 1977; Emery, 1969; Miller, 1976; Pasmore & Sherwood, 1978; Rice, 1963; Trist & Murray, 1990, 1993; Trist et al., 1963, 1997) introduced the concept of STS. Their studies revealed that efforts to change only the technical systems of an organization without addressing its social system—or vice versa—produce suboptimal results. They also emphasized the interdependent nature of transactions between organizations and their environments. As Russell L. Ackoff (1981) pointed out, “A technocratic culture runs the risk of getting the right solutions to the wrong problems. A humanistic culture runs the risk of getting the wrong solutions to the right problems” (p. 62). Thus, it is not a question of adopting either one approach or the other. Rather, it is a challenge of how to embrace both views as legitimate aspects of common issues. According to Ackoff (1981), an STS involves:

the joint operation of two independent but correlative systems: a social system composed of human beings who are the required actors in the performance of work, and a technological system made up of the tools, techniques, and methods of doing that are employed in task accomplishment. Specifically, “… it is impossible to optimize for overall performance without seeking to optimize jointly the correlative but independent social and technological systems” (Davis & Trist, 1972, p. 3).

Further, an STS:

continually interacts with an environment which both influences and is influenced by the work system. … a sociotechnical system exists and grows only to the extent that it maintains viable interchanges with its environment [that] involve a two-way flow of information and certain forms of matter and energy between the system and its environment (Cummings, 1978, p. 168).

While the Tavistock Group did not produce a collaborative mental model of an STS—except for Bridger (1977)—it did produce most of the theory and method for applying STSs to diagnosing and guiding the planning of change in large and small systems.





20.4.5 1969: Lawrence and Lorsch’s differentiation and integration model


This venerable, evidence-based model is extremely valuable in aiding leaders in designing and managing the mechanisms through which they attempt to reduce variance in their employees’ performance. While it is not a comprehensive STS model, it does utilize a number of STS elements and concepts. Lawrence & Lorsch (1969) suggest that STS models serve to expand human capacity to simultaneously consider and deal with many more variables in conscious awareness than the seven (plus or minus two) they could otherwise.

Lawrence & Lorsch (1969) see organizations as entities that are created by people in order to take collective action to improve their ability to cope with the demands of their environment. They state that “a framework for thinking about … organization development is an essential step toward understanding and action” (p. 9). As have other scholars, these theorists accept the STS principle that all parts of a system are interdependent and that “a change in one part has an impact on the others” (p. 10). A second principle of STSs is their capacity for morphogenesis—the primary distinguishing quality of human organizations. That is, organizations can decide to change themselves by comparing their desired results with the perceived actual outputs that have been produced by implementing their selected strategy. “Important [repetitive] discrepancies are defined as a problem and a search is undertaken for causes and remedies” (p. 11). Some discrepancies suggest problems that can be fixed, while others suggest the existence of opportunities that the organization might choose to exploit.

As systems grow, specialized functional and operational elements evolve to perform their unique functions. That is, decision-makers redesign and differentiate the component microsystems. “How much differentiation should exist … depends on what internal characteristics each group [or microsystem] must develop to carry out planned transactions with its assigned part of the environment” (p. 12). They imply that different microsystems within the same macrosystem are likely to have different cultures. When microsystems perform significantly different functions, they must be differentiated from one another in terms of how their members think and act. This mental model can be applied with any differentiated subsystem or with the macrosystem as a whole.

It is essential that the activities of differentiated microsystems are coordinated and achieve a high degree of integration so that each contributes to the realization of its macrosystem’s mission and strategic goals. However, it is quite difficult to integrate highly-differentiated but interdependent microsystems while the members of each think and act in their own very homogeneous manner. When microsystems must be highly differentiated and tightly integrated, supplemental integrating devices must be developed—individual coordinators, cross-system teams, entirely new microsystems—in order to achieve and maintain integration.

Since conflicts of interest are inevitable when differentiated but interdependent microsystems attempt to collaborate in the service of the macrosystem’s purpose and goals, Lawrence & Lorsch (1969) identified the need for conflict-management skills to facilitate and maintain integration among microsystems. They also believed that the people who have the greatest knowledge about relevant environmental conditions should use their informational or expert power and influence at the hierarchical level at which pertinent decisions are made.

Lawrence & Lorsch’s (1969) conceptual framework (Figure 20.3) spells out 10 critical interfaces: (1) a transactional strategy, including goals, spanning the boundary between the system and its external environment; if the system is a microsystem, its external environment is the larger macrosystem. Goals provide a target for the strategy. The strategy produces a set of three plans: (2) human-resource needs; (3) differentiation of task activities; and (4) an integrative structure for communications and decision-making. The (5) individual contributors that are recruited into the system bring with them not only (6) the knowledge, skills, and abilities (KSAs) needed to perform the expected tasks (their contributions) but also their own unique unplanned predispositions. The mix of these six factors combines to form (7) a complex and unique set of expectations for all system members—for themselves, each other, and the system. These expectations guide but do not totally determine (8) the system members’ actual behaviors. The sum of system members’ behaviors produces the system’s outputs, which are delivered through (9) the system’s transactional results. By conducting (10) a variance analysis based on feedback, which assesses the degree to which the results satisfied the goals, the information needed to determine whether changes or adjustments are needed in any or all of the 10 critical systemic interfaces is provided.

Figure 20.3 Lawrence & Lorsch’s Differentiation and Integration Model (1967).

Lawrence, P.R., & Lorsch, J.W. (1969). Developing organizations: Diagnosis and action. Reading, MA: Addison-Wesley, p. 17.



The criterion that should be used to assess:

whether a particular change will lead to the development of the organization at any one or all of these interfaces is whether the change will lead to either a better fit between the organization and the demands of its environment and/or to a better fit between the organization and the needs of individual contributors” (Lawrence & Lorsch, 1969, p. 18).

The results can be used to make a descriptive diagnosis of the current state at any organizational level—such as that of a macro- or microsystem. By analyzing the demand characteristics of the environment and/or the needs of individual contributors and stakeholders, a normative prescription can be created to provide the best fit between systemic characteristics and both task requirements and the needs of individual contributors.





20.4.6 1976: Weisbord’s six-box organizational model


Weisbord (1976) produced a model that is easy for unsophisticated leaders and consultants to comprehend and explain to others. According to Starr (2012), Weisbord based his model, in part, on Ackoff’s work. Thus, it remains very popular with some OD&C practitioners, who in turn present it to system leaders in explaining how the six boxes and their interdependencies help to diagnose systems and plan for change. The model illuminates the centrality of leadership and that rewards or incentives are needed to ensure that individuals perform essential tasks. Further, Weisbord draws attention to the idea that technical or structural changes must be supported by various “helpful mechanisms,” without which the change efforts are not likely to achieve their intended benefits. Thus, by identifying some STS elements, the six-box model (Figure 20.4) may help leaders to understand that any systemic change will have secondary and tertiary impacts on organizational purposes, structure, and relationships. Weisbord likened his model to a radar screen, which leaders can quickly scan to identify trouble spots. Unfortunately, he lumps dissimilar but related elements together rather than treating them separately. For example, formal organizational structure is combined with informal or cultural elements. He also lumps all helpful mechanisms into a separate, systemic box, even though they are more properly located in specific microsystems. For example, it is highly unlikely that mechanisms that are helpful to the human-resource management microsystem will also be helpful to marketing and sales microsystems—and vice versa. Similarly, the kind of leadership required at a systemic level is quite likely to be different from the leadership at executive-management, middle-management, and supervisory levels (Freedman, 1997; Jaques, 1987). In short, the six boxes are inadequate for conducting a comprehensive systemic diagnosis (Burke, 2002).

Figure 20.4 Weisbord’s six-box organizational model (1976).

Weisbord, M.R. (1976). Organizational diagnosis: Six places to look for trouble with or without a theory. Group and Organizational Studies, 1, pp. 430–447.



Weisbord (1976) used questions to explicate the contents of each of the six boxes. These questions are intended to evoke responses from organizational members during the data-collection phase. This reveals a weakness of the model: by asking questions regarding each of the six boxes that can be answered “yes” or “no,” diagnosticians restrict respondents’ focus of attention. Useful contextual information, elaborations, and clarifications may be ignored or unrecognized. Thus, the recommended questions may evoke incomplete—albeit still useful—data. Some questions require modifications. For example, to collect information about respondents’ perceptions concerning the first box, (1) the purpose of their system, Weisbord suggests asking, “How clear are you about the organization’s purpose and mission?” and “How supportive are you of the organization’s purpose and mission?” Understanding and supporting the organization’s purpose and mission are extremely important. But the questions are closed-ended and invite such cryptic responses as “Very clear,” “Not very clear,” “Not at all,” and the like. Open-ended questions that invite respondents to disclose more of what they are thinking would be more useful—such as, “How do you understand your organization’s purpose and mission?” and “Why are your organization’s purpose and mission important to you (or to organizational members in general)?”

(2) Structure raises the question of the fit between the whole system’s purpose and mission and the internal structure of the system. The internal structure comprises very different elements or microsystems that would be more appropriately separated, as in other STS models. (3) Rewards asks about the fit between what the system actually rewards and punishes and what system members believe they are rewarded or punished for doing or not doing. Again, different rewards and sanctions are likely to be applied for quite different behaviors in different microsystems, which might complement or conflict with rewards administrated at the total system level. (4) The helpful mechanisms box asks about the quality of processes and procedures that help (and hinder) members when doing their jobs. But this category should be applied to each component of an STS model, separately. (5) Relationships covers three categories: relationships between individuals; between and among microsystems; and between individuals and their job requirements. It anticipates that conflicts are inevitable and includes questions about how well the organization’s conflict-management mechanisms function. (6) Leadership asks how well leaders maintain balance among the other boxes. This is a rather limited explanation of leadership functions.

Weisbord (1976) did contribute three useful ideas about how his and other STS models might be applied. First, they may emphasize the need to consider the motivations and contributions of the individual. Second, they can help identify the size and nature of any gaps in the fit between the individual and the system. Third, STS models can illuminate the degree to which the system and its environment are congruent by identifying discrepancies between respondents’ perceptions of both (1) the system’s current and (2) its desired future states. These contrasting perceptions could serve to both establish baseline measures of the current state and identify potential targets for planned change. However, as Burke (2002) observed, Weisbord’s model provides little guidance about the critical nature of the cause-and-effect linkages among the six boxes.





20.4.7 1977: Bridger’s open-STS “pentagram” model


Harold Bridger (1977), a colleague of Emery, Rice, and Trist at the Tavistock Institute, created his pentagram model of an open STS using some of Leavitt’s elements and adding goal-setting to tasks, introducing leadership procedures as a new element and placing management activities (balancing and optimizing) in a central position (see Figure 20.5). Bridger’s model (1977) included all five of the fundamental characteristics of von Bertalanffy’s GST. In addition, he emphasized the interdependent interactivity of the elements. Interestingly, as far as I know, his is the only articulated mental model of an STS that came out of the Tavistock Institute.

Figure 20.5 Bridger’s Organizational Sociotechnical Pentagram Model (1977).

Bridger, H. (1977). “The value of the organization’s own systems in coping with stress.” Proceedings of the International Committee on Occupational Mental Health Congress, New York, 1977.



Bridger (1977) defines the environment as external to the organization and as distinguished by its semipermeable boundary. Thus, the system is in a continuous exchange with a turbulent, constantly-changing environment. People includes system members’ diverse values, vested interests, attitudes, education, group or peer ties, leadership behavior, power relationships, and dependencies. Goal-setting and tasks constitutes the primary purpose of the organization in terms of delivering goods and services at a profit. However, delivering outputs does not specify a system’s purpose; rather, outputs are a primary means of achieving that purpose. Technology refers to administrative resources and procedures, accounting, information systems, marketing, sales, advertising, investment goods, production, and so on. Structures encompasses division of labor, hierarchy, communications, leadership structures, organizational structure, and so on. Management activities includes selection, evaluation, and promotion systems, wage and salary systems, control systems, and monitoring systems. These functions are more appropriately included in a human-resource management function. Leadership procedures involves designing processes needed to balance and run the entire system.

Bridger’s (1977) model does not show how an organizational change flows through a system in a cause-and-effect manner such as that in Lawrence & Lorsch’s (1967) model. It seems like his model is scalable in the sense that it can be applied to the total system as well as to its component functional and operational microsystems. While it is an advancement beyond Leavitt (1964) and Weisbord (1976), its usefulness is still rather limited, since it is not as detailed as is needed for a truly comprehensive systemic diagnosis.





20.4.8 1977: Nadler–Tushman’s congruence model


David A. Nadler (1998) and Michael L. Tushman (1997) created their contingency model (Figure 20.6) to help managers and OD&C practitioners organize the way in which they understand how organizational systems function. The contingency model contains all of the qualities of an STS. As have other theorists, Nadler (1998) points out that all managers have developed an explicit or, most often, an implicit mental model of their organizations:

Figure 20.6 The Nadler–Tushman congruence model for diagnosing organizational behavior (1977).

Nadler, D.A., & Tushman, M.L. (1977). A diagnostic model for organization behavior. In Hackman, J.R., E.E. Lawler, & L.W. Porter, eds. Perspectives on behavior in organizations. New York: McGraw-Hill, pp. 85–100.



every manager’s personal organizational model largely determines what data he or she thinks is important to collect. In fact, the model shapes not only what data managers collect but how they collect it—in what form, and from whom—and how they analyze it, what they perceive as problems, what they conceive of as solutions, and how they go about putting those solutions into action (p. 26).

Input, according to Nadler (1998), has three component elements: (1) the environment, which exerts powerful influences on both macrosystems and microsystems—demands, constraints, opportunities; (2) resources, which are tangible (e.g. capital, plant, facilities, numbers and types of employees) but do not retain their value over time (e.g. customer relations and employees’ creativity); and (3) history, which includes the system’s crises, achievements, failures, mistakes, routine activities, problem-solving practices, and other significant events.

Business strategy3 represents the system’s set of enacted decisions about how to configure its resources with respect to its environment’s demands, constraints, and opportunities in terms of the system’s historical context. These decisions include: (1) markets—Who are our customers? What do they need or want?; (2) products and services to be produced—What satisfies customer needs and wants?; (3) competition—the qualities, attributes, or characteristics of outputs or the manner in which they are delivered, which give the system a competitive advantage over its competitors; and (4) performance objectives and the means by which they are expected to be measured.

The operating organization comprises the mechanisms through which inputs are transformed into outputs. It has four component elements: work, people, formal organizational arrangements, and informal organizational arrangements. More specifically:

(1) Work—The basic work performed by employees at both the macro- and microsystem levels. This includes: (a) required employee KSAs; (b) intrinsic rewards derived from work; (c) the uncertainty, interdependence, and repetitiveness of work; and (d) inherent constraints on performance.

(2) People—Employees’ individual characteristics, including: (a) KSAs; (b) expectations and perceptions, needs, and preferences for types of reward; and (c) demographics, personal backgrounds, and histories pertinent to work.

(3) Formal organizational arrangements—Formal structures, processes, and methods that induce employees to perform tasks required to achieve strategic objectives, which include: (a) groupings of functions, hierarchies, and coordination and control mechanisms; (b) job design; (c) work environment; and (d) human-resource management systems.

(4) Informal organizational arrangements—Emerging structures, processes, and arrange­ments that “balance the demands of the work and the needs of the people” (Nadler, 1998, p. 36). These include: (a) actual leadership behavior; (b) intra-subsystem relations; (c) inter-subsystem relations; (d) informal rules and working arrange­ments; (e) communications and influence patterns; and (f) organizational culture.



Nadler (1998) named his model after his notion of congruence. That is, the extent to which each internal component element fits or is aligned with all other internal elements relates to how effective the system is in “transforming [its] strategies into performance” (p. 38). He believed that the first step in determining what systemic changes should be made must be taken wherever there are misalignments—that is, where there is a lack of congruence. Leaders and consultants must attend to three critical assessments: (1) the extent to which the system’s strategy is congruent with the environment’s resources and history; (2) the extent to which the system’s strategy is congruent with its formal organization; and (3) the extent to which all internal organizational components are congruent with one another. Nadler’s notion of congruence is consistent with Kahle (1979), particularly with regard to person–environment fit.

The congruence model describes: (1) the system’s technical–structural dimension along the horizontal axis of the diagram; and (2) the social dimension (i.e. people and the informal components) along the vertical axis. Nadler & Tushman (1977) saw this not as a prescriptive contingency model but rather as one that could be adapted to any given set of external and internal conditions. Nadler (1998) also believed the contingency model helps us to understand the dynamics of the implementation of change efforts, because we can “trace the ripple of change through the organizational system” (p. 43). Finally, he saw the model as a tool that leaders and OD&C practitioners can use to organize their thinking about systemic dynamics in the context of a constantly changing set of environmental conditions.





20.4.9 1983: Tichy’s technical, political, cultural framework


Although he is best noted for his work on leadership and his close association with GE, Noel Tichy (2004) did create an STS “framework” that focuses on organizational change (Tichy, 1983). Essentially, his technical, political, cultural (TPC) framework (Figure 20.7) describes a system in terms of nine component “levers of change”: (1) the interface with the external environment, through which the system accesses its requisite inputs; (2) the system’s mission, describing its purposes; (3) the system’s strategy for realizing its objectives; (4) management of the system’s mission and strategic processes through engagement of all relevant stakeholder groups that have an interest in the enterprise; (5) tasks that are to be retained and replaced or added as a result of—or to enable—any systemic change; (6) the formal organization of prescribed intrasystem networks; (7) organizational processes such as communications, problem-solving, and decision-making; (8) people; and (9) the informal organization’s emergent networks. Management functions are conceived of as being spread throughout the system, so it is not characterized as a singular or distinctive lever of change. Output (systemic performance) is assumed to be “a function of the characteristics of each of the components of the model, as well as a function of how the components interrelate and align into a functioning system” (Tichy, 1983, p. 72).

Figure 20.7 The Tichy TPC framework (1983).

Tichy, N.M. (1983). Managing strategic change: Technical, political, and cultural dynamics. New York: John Wiley & Sons.



Cutting across the nine change levers, Tichy (1983) identified three primary systems that he believed were critical for understanding systemic dynamics and change (Figure 20.8): (1) the technical system, representing a rational–logical orientation; (2) the political system, representing the power dynamics operating within the system; and (3) the cultural system, related to the system’s shared norms and values—or “cultural schemes”—that link people together. Effective change efforts required that all three primary systems be aligned or realigned. He predicted that, when based upon only one or two of these three systems, change efforts would be relatively ineffective.

Figure 20.8 Tichy’s TPC matrix (1983).

Tichy, N.M. (1983). Managing strategic change: Technical, political, and cultural dynamics. New York: John Wiley & Sons.



To conduct a meaningful system diagnosis, Tichy (1983) believed that information must be collected—presumably from all relevant parties, including internal and external stakeholders—for each cell in the matrix. He was one of the few theorists to emphasize the importance of inviting system members to become actively involved in the diagnostic process. He believed these data could identify what was and was not in alignment, and that they could also clarify the nature and degree of change required to establish or reestablish alignment—horizontally and vertically.

The TPC matrix may also serve a predictive purpose: changing one cell perturbs the entire system. Thus, when focusing on changing the elements in that cell, leaders and consultants can expect secondary and tertiary changes in the elements contained in the cells bordering it.





20.4.10 1970–1980: Burke–Litwin model of organizational performance and change


W. Warner Burke and George H. Litwin (2002) co-created an STS model (Figure 20.9) that has two important characteristics. First, it has been partly verified by research. Second, it contains an explicit cause-and-effect sequence that prescribes how leaders and OD&C practitioners can plan a sequence of interventions that may achieve revolutionary change objectives. While recognizing overlaps with other models, Burke (2002) considers the 12 components of the model to be essential for understanding and analyzing organizational systems. Like others, Burke (2002) points out that changes in any component will impact all other components. He distinguishes five transformational factors (for radical change) from eight transactional factors (for incremental change) in the model.

Figure 20.9 The Burke–Litwin model of organizational performance and change (late 1970s to early 1980s).



Transformational factors include: external environment, mission and strategy, organizational culture, individual and organizational performance, and leadership. External environmental factors cause changes in one or more of the other four transformational factors, especially leadership. Changing transformational factors often results in radical, discontinuous, episodic, and revolutionary change.

External environment may influence system performance. Such forces and variables include politics, governmental regulations, financial–economic conditions, and changing technology. The transformational factors that are most influenced are mission, strategy, leadership, and culture.

Mission states the purpose and primary goals of the system. Strategy concerns how the system intends to realize its mission.Mission and strategy are influenced most by leadership.

Organizational culture encompasses the system’s norms and values—that is, the implicit rules governing acceptable behavior within the system. Culture also exerts considerable influence on mission and strategy.

Individual and organizational performance is not elaborated.

(Vision is not a distinct category but is implied by the model. It reflects the “passion” that leaders and members may feel about their collective future, as well as their aspirations and desired goals.)

Leadership permeates the entire system and ties the other four transformational factors together. It provides direction and orients members towards the desired future. It is seen as consisting of persuading, influencing, serving followers, and acting as a role model.



Transactional factors relate to management as they concern the system’s routine operations. Change in one or more of these eight elements causes incremental, evolutionary, or selective changes.

Management practices include roles, tasks, goal-setting, utilization of system resources, and rewarding of members. Managers use these practices to carry out the system’s strategy for realizing its goals and mission.

Structure defines the system’s functional and operational microsystems. It indicates levels of authority, responsibility, lines of communications, and relationships among microsystems. Strategy is determined before—and, to an extent, strongly influences—structure and tasks.

Systems consist of policies and procedures intended to support and enable system members to discharge their responsibilities. Included are management information systems, performance management systems (including goal-setting, performance appraisal, and reward systems), budgeting processes, and HR deployment systems and procedures. Strategic HR management effectiveness (e.g. internally-consistent rules, regulations, policies, and procedures) may be stronger predictors of positive performance than technical HR management effectiveness (e.g. recruiting, selecting, training, compensation/benefits, and performance measurement). However, to place such disparate systems in the same category makes it difficult to recognize and assess their unique, respective contributions.

Work-unit climate refers to members’ collective perceptions of, their managers, the clarity of managers’ expectations, recognition for work, involvement in decision-making, challenging and fair work standards, support from other work-unit members, and transactions with other systemic work units. Structure and management practices directly affect these task requirements. Burke (2002) believes that a participative climate positively influences motivation and productivity—and, ultimately, customer satisfaction.

Task requirements, and individual skills and abilities, refers to the congruence between the requirements of an individual’s job, role, and responsibilities and his or her KSAs—also referred to as P–E fit (Kahle, 1979).

Motivation is concerned with the arousal of individuals’ feelings about their own needs, which, when converted into action directed towards systemic goals, may satisfy those needs. Since human needs tend to persist until they are satisfied, Burke (2002) advises system leaders and managers to craft organizationally-relevant goals that also satisfy individuals’ unmet needs. Motivation influences performance through intervening variables: task requirements, individual KSAs, and individual needs and values. These intervening variables must be congruent with one another to result in effective performance.

Individual needs and values concern the extent to which there is congruence or fit between “what the individual believes is important, worthwhile, and valuable and what the organization stands for, such as its purpose, values, and how people in and out of the organization (including customers) are treated” (Burke, 2002, p. 208).

Individual and organizational performance may be directly influenced by the system’s responses to the external environment. Both strategy and leadership positively affect performance. Performance can be measured by such quantitative and qualitative variables as productivity, quality, innovation, customer satisfaction, profits, market share, and earnings per share.



Burke (2002) advises that planned change should flow either from the top to bottom or laterally from the external environment to performance. However, he acknowledges that the existing research does not validate the entire model and that more comprehensive studies are called for.





20.4.11 1987: Porras’s stream-analysis model


Jerry Porras’s (1987) stream-analysis model of organizations (Figure 20.10) enables leaders and OD&C practitioners to both diagnose problems and guide corrective action. This begins with the environment’s affect on the organization and, in particular, on the system’s purpose—that is, the system’s collective reasons for existing. Porras (1987) believed, “These reasons guide the decisions made by both the leaders and members of the system” (p. 75). Organizational purpose has a dual orientation. Looking outward, system leaders and members scan their pertinent environments and apprehend their ­constantly-changing multiple demands to identify such variables as: targets of opportunity, threats from competition, sources and availability of essential resources (e.g. raw material, components, and human, mechanical, technological, and fiscal resources), changes in the market, restrictions (or absence thereof) imposed by government regulations, and the influence of the media and the investment community. Looking inward, the significance and meanings attributed to their environment are viewed through members’ collective perceptual filter of the system’s purpose, and in turn influence the system’s responses to the environment.

Figure 20.10 Porras’s Stream Analysis Model (1987).

Porras, J.I. (1987, p. 84). Stream analysis: A powerful way to diagnose and manage organizational change. Reading, MA: Addison-Wesley.



The system’s vision, mission, and strategies are, then, derived from its purpose:

A vision is often a representation of what the organization might look like if it were ­accomplishing its purpose. Vision reflects the core reasons for the organization’s existence and … broadly guides decision makers as they pick and choose among the many short-term options and paths available to the organization” (Porras, 1987, p. 76).

However, there is little agreement among systems theorists or strategic-planning techsperts on a single definition of a vision or how it might best be used.

Interaction between the external environment and a system’s internal operations is multidirectional. The environment makes demands of the system. The system’s responses affect the environment. When either demands or responses change, one or the other must adapt. While many theorists identify various systemic components as the levers for change, Porras (1987) believes that the individual system member is the critical lever for change. Systemic change occurs “only when the people in the organization have changed the way they function” (p. 38). These changes include: how they make decisions; how they execute their assigned tasks; how they communicate information, to whom, and what that information is; the quality with which they perform their responsibilities; their creativity; and what, if any, actions they initiate. When members do not understand and/or accept their system’s espoused purpose, they are likely to substitute their personal purposes. Members at all levels and in all parts of the system may make idiosyncratic decisions that are incongruent with one another and with their system’s espoused purpose. They may focus on different aspects of environmental changes or decide that different actions are called for. A possible consequence is that microsystems may attempt to maximize their own interests—and operate against the interests of other microsystems and those of the macrosystem as a whole.

The stream-analysis model explicates the elements that a system creates to shape and guide its members’ on-the-job behavior. Specifically, Porras (1987) proposes four basic dimensions that, if changed, will also change the system’s expectations of members’ behavior, members’ expectations of themselves, and the value or valence members have for achieving specific outcomes. Porras (1987) credits Germane (1986) for elaborating the elements of the following four dimensions: (1) organizing arrangements; (2) social factors; (3) technology; and (4) physical setting. These dimensions provide individual system members with cues about the behaviors that will most likely be seen and rewarded and will enable them to achieve both their personal goals and their organization’s objectives. The assumption is that, based on these cues, people will choose to act in ways that either increase or decrease organizational performance. Of all STS models, this is probably the most extensive and precise description of a system’s component operational elements.

Organizing arrangements (Germane, 1986) include: (1) goals; (2) strategies; (3) formal structure; (4) administrative policies and procedures; (5) administrative systems; and (6) formal reward systems—that is, evaluation systems, pay systems, bonuses, and benefits packages.

Social factors (Germane, 1986) include: (1) culture—basic assumptions, values, norms, language and jargon, rituals, history, stories, myths, and symbols; (2) interaction processes—interpersonal, group, and intergroup; (3) social patterns and networks—formal and informal communications, problem-solving and decision-making, influence, and status; and (4) individual attributes—attitudes and beliefs, behavioral skills, and feelings.

Technology (Germane, 1986) includes: (1) tools, equipment, and machinery; (2) technical expertise; (3) job design; (4) work-flow design; (5) technical policies and procedures; and (6) technical systems.

The physical setting (Germane, 1986) includes: (1) space configuration—size, shape, and relative locations; (2) physical ambiance—light, heat, noise, air quality, and cleanliness; (3) interior design—decorations, furniture, window coverings, colors of floor, walls, and ceilings; and (4) architectural design. Porras’s attention to the physical setting is unique compared with other STS models and is consistent with Harry Levinson’s belief in the psychological importance of the physical setting for diagnostic purposes (e.g. Freedman & Bradt, 2009; Levinson, 1994, 2002).

Taken together, and when compared with other theorists, Porras’s four basic dimensions provide greater elaboration of the component STS elements. Porras (1987) points out that “all four dimensions are interconnected. If one is going to be changed and the effects on the others are not considered, then the impact of what is done will be reduced” (p. 71). He points out the importance of alignment of these areas by saying, “the key strategy for changing a system is to design each of the four areas in such a way that all give the same message to people about their behavior” (p. 71).

To change a system, one must first understand the component parts and how they fit together. One must also know how changing one part is likely to change the other parts and how, by changing the configuration of parts, one will change the system’s outputs. One must know how each component influences individual system members’ on-the-job behaviors. If contradictions between these four dimensions occur:

each organizational member will respond in a unique pattern of these messages, perceiving some and missing others, valuing some and ignoring others, remembering some and forgetting others. As a result, predicting how any one individual will behave as a consequence of a contradictory set of messages is very difficult (p. 74).

Complete system-wide consistency may not be possible. However, if the messages emitted by the four sets of dimensions are even relatively consistent, more system members will be similar in the ways they perceive the purpose of their system and consistent in making decisions and taking action. This is quite in accordance with the theory of Lawrence & Lorsch (1967).





20.4.12 1981–2011: Freedman’s swamp model of STSs


Freedman (1981–2011) created a mental model of a complex STS (see Figure 20.11). Participants in an NTL Institute workshop on organizational diagnosis named it “the swamp model” (Julie Noolan, personal communication). They chose this name “because things flow into it, things bubble up from the bottom, and things flow out.” To them, it looked like a schematic diagram of a very complex and complicated primordial swamp.

Figure 20.11 The Freedman STS “swamp model” for organizational diagnosis (1978–1987).



There are 14 elements to be considered in this model. Many of the detailed components of elements that Porras (1987) and Germane (1986) specified are relevant to it.

Input or supply-side stakeholders consist of: suppliers of raw materials, components, theoretical propositions, research results, and the like; regulatory commissions; investors; collectors of market information about customers and competitors; various sources of information about such environmental factors as industry trends, economics, politics, and weather conditions; and providers of potential employees. Inputs vary by their nature, quality, and reliability.

Supply-side boundary-spanning mechanisms consist of specialized systems, units, or func­tions that manage critical transactions across the lateral interface between external stakeholders and the organizational system. Specialized unit members are responsible for proactively scouting and scanning the environment (Holder & McKinney, 1993) for threats and opportunities, negotiating and conducting input–output transactions with external stakeholders, assessing suppliers’ compliance with mutually-negotiated standards, establishing and following procedures to determine the acceptability of inputs, and specifying the amount and type of decision-making authority for all involved parties. They also ensure effective communications between the organization and the supply-side stakeholders: clarifying and negotiating goals and roles, managing and utilizing conflict, clarifying and negotiating mutual expectations, negotiating decision-making procedures and strategies, jointly identi­fying and dealing with emerging issues, and negotiating commitments and consequences.

Strategic direction is formulated by organizational leaders and members, based in large part on the information about prevailing environmental conditions and trends that are obtained from supply-side stakeholders through various boundary-spanning units. According to Hambrick & Fredrickson (2005), systems leaders and their OD&C practitioner consultants must have “an integrated, overarching concept of how the business will achieve its objectives” (p. 51). A single, unified strategy must have components. Leaders are ultimately responsible for creating a viable strategy for their systems. They must organize that information to formulate a strategy design. Hambrick & Fredrickson (2005) present a functional framework for this, which consists of five elements, each of which is framed by questions. (1) Arenas asks, “Where will we be active?” For example, will the system operate locally, regionally, nationally, internationally, or globally? (2) Vehicles asks, “How will we get there?” (3) Differentiators asks, “How will we win in the marketplace?” or “What is our competitive advantage?” (4) Staging asks, “What will our speed and sequence of moves be?” (5) Economic logic asks, “How will we obtain our returns?”

Systems leaders vary considerably with regard to their optimism and pessimism about their organizations’ futures. Their attitudes along this dimension often result in a considerable number of frequently intense and counterproductive conflicts when trying to agree on a single future that is both desirable and feasible. Therefore, Freedman (1981–2011) recommends factoring the information gathered from Hambrick & Fredrickson’s (2005) five sets of questions into a set of alternative future scenarios that describe possible and probable environmental conditions in which the organization may operate over some predetermined span of years. Together, the alternative future scenarios constitute Freedman’s (1981–2011) version of a vision. Participants are divided into three teams based upon their self-assessments of their optimism–pessimism. The team of the most pessimistic leaders creates the worst-case scenario. The most optimistic leaders create the best-case scenario. The leaders who rated themselves in the middle create the most-likely-case scenario. For each scenario, teams specify six elements: the unique mission, the goals, the strategies, the milestones, the plans, and the philosophy or values that are intended to guide the organization’s operational decisions under the conditions of their scenario. Further, by creating at least three alternative future scenarios, giving serious consideration to alternative exigencies, it is easier for leaders to adjust and adapt to emerging, unprecedented environmental conditions—whether or not those conditions are accurately predicted.

Financial management includes systems that track projected and actual revenues and costs, auditing systems and procedures, profit-and-loss statements, cash flow, the incremental value added by each element of the organization’s value chain, and the financial costs and value of capital investments.

Human-resource management supports line management by performing such functions as: strategic HR planning for the system’s future needs, producing position descriptions, recruiting, selecting, and on-boarding; HR utilization or deploying of existing employees; HR development of existing employees for future jobs; and HR monitoring to determine integration and alignment of individuals to micro-, macro-, and suprasystems. To perform these functions effectively, HR management specialists must cooperate closely with one another and with other functional and operational executives and managers. HR management has also been reinventing itself in order to consult with and assist executive and line managers in creating motivating environments so that employees can satisfy their needs and desires by contributing to the realization of their subsystems’ strategic objectives—that is, becoming the business partners of line managers (Lawler & Mohrman, 2000; Ulrich, 1997; Ulrich et al., 1997). In addition, HR management is tasked with taking the lead in updating and modifying its planning, utilization, development, and monitoring systems to maintain alignment with and to provide appropriate support for the implementation of organizational changes. Without such updating, HR management systems will inappropriately maintain their orientation to their past history even when that orientation no longer serves a functional purpose.

Structure includes: hierarchical architecture; defined positions and roles; established levels of authority, responsibility, and accountability that are appropriate for each hierarchical level; and formal rules, regulations, policies, procedures, and practices. The latter functions are usually placed under HR management, but Freedman (1981–2011) sees them as contributing more to the fundamental purpose of structure—reducing the variability and increasing the predictability of human behavior. There may be an inherent dilemma: the more effective the structure is in serving these two purposes, the less the system members are able to quickly recognize and act on the need to adapt to changing conditions. That is, creativity may be suppressed and innovations may not be forthcoming when they are most needed. All of these structural elements must be continuously monitored and modified as needed to ensure they are aligned with and support any organizational change initiative.

Production is designed and organized around some combination or permutation of three core work processes: projects or “job shops,” production or assembly lines, and continuous process (Woodward, 1965, 1978). Creating outputs usually requires some combination of these three processes. Production requires operational plans for ­human-resource utilization, work flow (value-adding business processes), differentiation of responsibilities, integrative structures and processes for communications and making decisions, means for assessing expected versus actual results, and practices for scanning for unintended consequences and predictable surprises. Further, production requires viable plans for taking corrective and preventive maintenance action for human, fiscal, and physical resources. Lawrence & Lorsch (1967) provided a particularly useful model to guide the diagnostic process towards fully understanding the dynamics involved in production—and in any other supporting operational or functional microsystem.

Technology encompasses both “hard” equipment and “soft” processes and methods that enable organizational members to improve their performance in terms of safety, quality, speed, cost, and reliability. Different types of either hard or soft technologies may be applied in incremental- or radical-change efforts. Porras (1987) provides useful examples of technology. A new hard technology that might be considered soft is “the cloud.” This innovation appears to promise a revolutionary, cost-efficient opportunity to replace expensive system-owned and -maintained computer hardware and software with shared information capacity located off-site that is easily accessible though the Internet. Techspert consultants who specialize in IT or restructuring tend to focus and rely upon the hard technologies, while OD&C practitioners and consulting organizational psychologists also emphasize and incorporate the soft technologies. As is often said, “The soft stuff is the hard stuff.”

Leadership functions ensure that the right things are done. These are most evident at the boundary between a micro- or macrosystem and its external environment. The “right things” include: (1) ensuring that the external environment is constantly scouted and monitored for threats and opportunities; (2) linking external and internal environments; (3) building agreement among stakeholders on strategic imperatives; (4) securing capital resources; (5) coordinating business units’ activities; (6) setting and supporting strategic direction; (7) ensuring the integrity of systemic boundaries; (8) ensuring variance analysis (feedback) is used to establish and maintain fit among organizational elements and alignment between the organization and its stakeholders; and (9) ensuring that corrective and preventive maintenance activities are planned and executed in a timely, high-quality, and cost-effective manner. While leaders can delegate responsibility for implementing incremental changes, they must modify their priorities and take on primary responsibility for leading discontinuous, transformational change. Every operational and functional microsystem in the swamp model is to be understood as having a ring of leadership functions around it.

Management functions ensure that things are done right. These are most evident at the boundary between the production microsystem and all other internal organizational elements. Doing things right includes: (1) producing; (2) implementing (scheduling, coordinating, controlling, and maintaining discipline); (3) innovating; and (4) integrating strategies, risks, goals, and entrepreneurship (Adizes & Borgese, 1975). Management must also: (5) secure all subordinates’ commitment to a common understanding and acceptance of the organization’s vision, mission, and strategic goals; (6) align subordinates’ microsystem objectives with strategic goals; (7) monitor and ensure integration and coordination among the microsystem’s operational and functional microsystems; (8) ensure that variances between actual performance and expected progress and results are fully understood and that effective corrective and preventive measures are enacted; (9) involve employees in identifying the need for, planning, and implementing change; (10) help employees cope with change; (11) provide feedback to executives from customers and employees, and (12) design and revise (in collaboration with HR) then apply relevant, realistic performance-management systems. However, leaders at all organizational levels must perform many management functions in addition to their leadership responsibilities. Therefore, every organizational microsystem and element, and the larger organization itself, should be understood to have a ring of management functions around it.

Culture permeates the entire macrosystem and each of its microsystems. Specific unwritten cultural norms that define acceptable behavior are likely to be different in different microsystems and at different hierarchical levels. Organizational culture must be differentiated from societal culture (Hofstede & Hofstede, 2005). The latter is based on prevailing social values, while the former is derived from organizational practices. Organizational culture encompasses persistent manifestations and influences of the organizational traditions, practices, habits, beliefs, values, precedents, myths, and legends that guide system members’ perceptions, decisions, and actions. While members of an organization may carry their own very different social cultures into their organizations, they are generally induced to conform to the requirements of the macro- or microsystem’s culture. For example, an organization’s cultural norms and values contain pervasive belief systems about the nature of human motivation—theory x or theory y (McGregor, 2006). Individual employees who comply with organizational norms while on the job may or may not behave the same way outside of their job.

Political practices are inevitable aspects of culture. While the positions taken by politically-oriented persons may be overt, their underlying interests are likely to be covert (Marshack, 2005). However, which political dynamics prevail is often a function of the specific intersection of societal and organizational cultural norms.

Culture functions as either an enabling or an inhibiting—or even toxic—mediator and serves as the internal context for all organizational elements. As such, culture affects the operations of all organizational elements and is in turn affected by those elements. Culture may speed or slow the pace of change within any set or subset of system elements. Interestingly, although many have tried, there is no evidence that the nature and quality of an organization’s culture can be directly changed (Hofstede & Hofstede, 2005; Schein, 1985). Rather, changing the nature of and interrelationships among the systemic microsystems and elements may, incrementally, change the culture.

Climate or mood refers to the generalized emotional responses of system members to the current levels of stress, strain, and tension, to the sense of urgency, pace, or tempo of activities (generally in response to more or less temporary external events), and to the prevailing temporary systemic conditions (e.g. crisis, uncertainty, turbulence, fear, exhilaration, or anxiety) within the entire macrosystem and each of its microsystems. The climate is likely to be different in different microsystems and at different hierarchical levels. If culture is like the seasons of the year, climate is like today’s weather. Culture changes slowly, while climate can change quite rapidly.

Output or demand-side boundary-spanning mechanisms include those specialized units that are responsible for delivering the organization’s outputs and byproducts across the lateral interface to their respective appropriate stakeholders. Boundary-spanning unit members: (1) assess customers’ perceptions of the organization’s compliance with mutually-negotiated standards; (2) negotiate with customers to establish procedures to determine their acceptance of the outputs; (3) negotiate the amount and type of decision-making authority for all involved parties; and (4) assess customers’ satisfaction with the organization’s post-sales or post-delivery services. In general, (5) they ensure effective communications between the organization and the demand-side stakeholders. Specifically, they: (6) clarify and negotiate goals and roles; (7) participate in managing and utilizing conflict; (8) clarify and negotiate or renegotiate mutual expectations; (9) negotiate decision-making procedures and strategies; (10) jointly identify and deal with emerging issues; and (11) negotiate commitments and create positive and punitive consequences.

Demand-side stakeholders include clients, customers, or consumers of the organization’s outputs—goods, services, dividends, reports, and byproducts, including waste. These end users may include: (1) regulatory commissions, to which required reports are conveyed; (2) investors, business media, and Wall Street, which require quarterly reports; (3) collectors of market-research data about the organization and its industry; and (4) destination sites for the organization’s byproducts and waste. Outputs vary by their nature, quality, and reliability.

Feedback mechanisms and variance analysis. Feedback on performance is collected from every functional and operational microsystem and delivered to every other microsystem. The feedback focuses not only on the internal operations of each microsystem but also on the interface transactions among microsystems and between the macrosystem and its relevant stakeholders in the external environment. This is especially important for leadership and management. Actual performance is compared with desired or expected performance and specifies: (1) what activities and conditions are functioning well, and where; and (2) what activities and conditions can be improved—including performance gaps and duplication of functions—and where. Variance analysis serves such purposes as: (1) validating the system’s strengths in all of its parts at all levels; (2) identifying dysfunctional or suboptimal performance areas as potential change objectives; (3) identifying the root causes of symptomatic problems; and (4) identifying all microsystems that are affected by current conditions at the primary, secondary, and tertiary levels.

Once the feedback data have been collected, they must be organized. Steve Ruma (1974) created a method for reducing and categorizing the data obtained through the feedback process to enable diagnosticians to make informed judgments as to where—within or between microsystems—to invest scarce resources in order to correct and prevent revealed systemic problems and improve performance (Figure 20.12). The flexible-functional model integrates two significant dimensions in assessing data provided by the feedback. Laterally, the model distinguishes between systemic functions that are either (1) functional and effective or (2) dysfunctional and without useful purpose. Vertically, the model distinguishes between functions that are either (1) inflexible and not amenable to change or (2) flexible and amenable to change. The resulting 2 × 2 matrix yields four categories into which data can be placed and converted into useful information. The four categories are: (I) an area of low anxiety and maximum health, which contains elements that are both functional and effective and amenable to change; (II) an area of natural targets for planned change, which contains elements that are dysfunctional and without useful purpose but are amenable to change; (III) an area of stop-gap measures or functional fixedness, which contains elements that are functional and effective but are not amenable to change; and (IV) an area of organizational disasters, which contains elements that are both dysfunctional and without useful purpose and are not amenable to change.

Figure 20.12 Reducing data: Ruma’s flexible-functional model (1974).

Ruma, S. (1974). A diagnostic model for organizational change: Where the flexible is functional. Social Change, 4 (4).



The flexible-functional model can be applied to data from a macrosystem as a whole, from any microsystem, from the transactions among microsystems, from the interactions between a macrosystem and its external supply-and-demand-side stakeholders, and from vertical relations up and down a system’s hierarchy. Obviously, the problems, opportunities, and dilemmas placed in area 2 would be a logical place from which to start a complex organizational-change effort, since they represent the “low-hanging fruit” that can demonstrate to all involved parties that change is imminent and possible. This strategy also serves as a pilot project and provides leaders with practice opportunities in leading and managing change.

Ruma (1974) points out that: (1) movement from area II to area I is most likely a result of effective planned-change interventions; but (2) lack of maintenance of area-I elements or complacency reduces the possibility of intentional, self-conscious change—the result is that, taken for granted and left to themselves, elements contained in area I will devolve and migrate to area III; and (3) inflexibility with regard to the contents of area III leads to reduced levels of effectiveness and a tendency for those elements to gravitate towards area IV.

Ruma (1974) suggests various interventions to address system elements that have been placed in each category. For example, for elements placed in area IV (i.e. organizational disasters), he suggests that since (1) either those affected by these conditions have no legitimate authority to influence them and/or (2) those with authority are unresponsive to those who feel imposed upon, it is appropriate to (3) treat area-IV conditions as pathogenic and create an impermeable barrier around them to prevent them from contaminating healthier parts of the organization and (4) “starve” area-IV conditions by refraining from making any investment of energy to attempt a corrective intervention, and instead withholding resources, supplies, information, time, and attention.

Lateral-interface transactions. The swamp model, like other comprehensive STS models, is scalable. It can be used to assess the internal operations of organizational units of any size—from an intact team to the entire organization. This range includes any team, department, division, product group, or region. By extension, the swamp model helps us to understand the number and nature of lateral-interface transactions between interdependent systems of any size or level of complexity. The fundamental characteristic of these lateral transactions is that the outputs of subsystem “A” constitute some—but not all—of the essential inputs that subsystem “B” requires to perform its designated functions (see Figure 20.13).

Figure 20.13 Lateral-interface transactions between interdependent subsystems (Freedman, 1978–1980).



Many organizational-change efforts focus exclusively on the dynamics operating internally within an organization’s various microsystems. The swamp model clearly indicates that, when a subsystem is assessed independently, it may be thought to be operating effectively. However, when the patterns of transactions between and among interdependent microsystems are examined, previously-ignored dysfunctions may be revealed. This is seen most clearly in a value or supply chain of interdependent subsystems. Similarly, vertical interactions across hierarchical levels must be assessed to identify what is functional or dysfunctional and what is flexible or not amenable to change.





20.4.13 Characteristics of open STSs


STS models can guide planners in both designing (or redesigning) organizational architecture and making organizational diagnoses of a system’s internal strengths and vulnerabilities relative to multiple discontinuous changes in its relevant environment. STS diagnoses also enable organizational leaders to track symptomatic indicators of problems, opportunities, and dilemmas throughout the system—from the beginning to the end and from the top to the bottom—as they seek to identify both their root causes and their primary, secondary, and tertiary consequences. Based on their assessments, leaders can determine how and in what ways organizational systems constantly evolve, change, and adapt, allowing them to reveal their past and present and to anticipate their probable and possible futures (Porras, 1987; Senge, 1990; Senge et al., 1994).

STS theory consists of a number of propositions about the dynamic nature of organizational systems of any size or type. Collectively, these propositions describe complex systems and how they operate, grow, and survive—or not.

The most fundamental STS proposition is the universal law of entropy: without a constant infusion of energy (i.e. negative entropy), systems inexorably move towards disorganization, decay, and ultimately death. That is, with only positive entropy, systems tend to maintain their equilibrium and replicate their past histories and traditions while their worlds change around them, often passing them by. Thus, they progress along a predictable life-cycle trajectory: birth, infancy, childhood, adolescence, maturity, old age/stagnation, decline, and death. However, with sufficient negative entropy, revitalization is possible at any life-cycle stage. Efforts at renewal are most likely to be accepted by organizational members during declining stages when adverse consequences become evident. Unfortunately, by this time efforts to revitalize the organization may be too little, too late. During their growth stages, when organizations are on a relatively healthy trajectory, leaders tend to be complacent and rarely recognize the need for revitalization.

A second fundamental proposition is that any systemic change will create primary, secondary, and tertiary perturbations that impact all other parts and levels of the STS. Systemic changes may be structural, technological, or procedural. If, for example, a change is technological, Porras (1987) and Germane (1986) point out that related primary changes can be expected. These might include: (1) new tools, equipment, and machinery that employees must learn to operate and which must be purchased and installed; (2) importing and subsequent institutionalization of technical expertise ; (3) design or redesign of jobs, to fit the new demand characteristics of the work and the new KSAs required by employees to operate the new technology; (4) redesign of work flow, since the technology will probably replace some human effort and increase the pace of work; (5) rewriting of technical policies and procedures to maintain alignment with the new capabilities of the new technology; (6) integration of the new technology into existing technical systems; (7) elimination of some existing jobs and laying-off of incumbents; and (8) recruitment, hiring, and integration of qualified employee prospects who have relevant experience and KSAs. Secondary changes would be expected in the goals, relationships, and practices of operational and functional microsystem members who must provide assistance in any of the primary changes. Both primary and secondary changes will inevitably create tertiary changes in the interactions and transactions with members of microsystems that are indirectly affected by them.

The impacts of primary, secondary, and tertiary changes evoke defensive responses that are intended to contain anxiety and maintain the status quo (Levinson, 2002), rather than adapting to changing conditions. A primary cause of this anxiety is the organizational members’ fear that they will not be able to master the new demands that will be made of them. This generally leads to additional anxiety related to job security.

Kuhn (1974) described how all STSs and their microsystems communicate and conduct transactions across their boundaries to perform three core functions that are essential to enabling leaders and managers to deal with the unexpected, unprecedented, discontinuous challenges common during complex systems change: detecting, selecting, and effecting. Detecting relates to communicating information among supra-, macro-, or microsystems. Selecting is determined by the rules and criteria the STS uses to make decisions. Effecting refers to the means through which transactions are made between systems or subsystems.

Carlisle (1981) proposed the following additional descriptions of open STSs. (1) Input–output transactions dominate how open STSs function as they interact with their environments. These transactions constitute vital interdependent relationships between a system and its environmental context. (2) An STS constitutes the whole that is greater than the sum of its individual parts. Relationships are of the greatest importance. (3) Open STSs are typically hierarchical. Thus, relationships to be studied are vertical, lateral, and multidirectional. Perturbations caused by any change ripple upward through levels, sidewise from one subsystem to another, and diagonally across both levels and subsystems. (4) STSs always display some characteristics of the environment in which they operate. Similarly, microsystems always display some of the characteristics of their larger macrosystem. (5) Macrosystems are always more complex than their component microsystems, since they incorporate different kinds of microsystems as well as an increased number and variety of relationships, which result from linking the individual parts. (6) The ways in which an STS manages its boundaries influence how effective it is in its exchanges with its environment. (7) Growth in open STSs always results in increasing differentiation among microsystems and, consequently, complexity. (8) Some built-in conflict exists in all complex open STSs. Individuals, groups, and microsystems always have some purposes or desired outcomes that are at odds with each other, with the larger macrosystem, and with various levels in the organization’s hierarchy. (9) Open-STS theory emphasizes relativism, not absolutes. Thus, equifinality predominates. That is, there are always several different pathways that can lead to a final desired state or goal. (10) The condition of any STS at a given point in time is the result of multiple causes. The variability among the diverse factors that constitute multicausation requires that meaningful analysis be conducted in every situational context.

Keusch (1981) added several more propositions. (1) Organizational charts can convey the erroneous impression that workers on the same hierarchical level work at the same operational or technical level. However, such workers may be engaged in quite different types and levels of work (Jaques, 1989). Further, people that work many hierarchical levels removed from each other may be working at the same operating or technical level. (2) When line managers create and follow strategic plans for their microsystems that are aligned with the plan of the larger organization, they make the organization properly proactive, rather than reactive. (3) Very large complex systems experience wall-building, jurisdictional disputes, empire-building, and deflection of responsibility by the blaming of others.

Organizational leaders and OD&C practitioners should consider these systemic dynamics when analyzing the results of an organizational diagnosis that is intended to define current conditions and to anticipate how incremental and radical organizational changes might affect the macrosystem, its component microsystems, and the interdependent transactions among microsystems, between the macrosystem and its stakeholders in its external environment, and across hierarchical levels. Leaders and OD&C practitioners should also consider how these involved parties might react to and affect the planning and implementation of complex systems changes.





20.5 Conclusions: STS Models and Organizational Diagnosis


An STS approach to organizational diagnosis provides a common language and method for the study of societal and organizational dynamics. Its vocabulary optimizes communication across systems, professional disciplines, and organizational levels. STS theory is a way of looking at the component elements of micro-, macro-, and suprasystems, their vertical, hierarchical relationships, and their interactions with their respective external environments. An STS approach comprises an internally-consistent method of inquiry that can be applied to all social and systemic studies (Walonick, 1993). The major difficulty in applying STS theory is that various STS models contain different elements that reflect the perceptions and biases of their designers. No single model can realistically claim to capture all of the relevant aspects of any system.

Historically, as various organizational theorists and practitioners undoubtedly reflected on their experiences with the early STS models, they crafted increasingly complex models.
